title: PPO KL early-stop only breaks the inner minibatch loop, not the epoch loop — early stopping is ineffective
description: The KL-divergence early-stopping `break` is nested inside the `for batch_idx in 0..n_batches` loop, which is itself inside `for _epoch in 0..self.config.n_epochs`. A `break` therefore only aborts the remaining minibatches of the CURRENT epoch; the outer epoch loop then proceeds to the next epoch and resumes updating. Standard PPO (e.g. SB3) sets a `continue_training=false` flag and breaks out of BOTH loops so that no further gradient steps are taken once approx_kl exceeds target_kl. As written, target_kl provides essentially no protection: after tripping it the agent still runs all remaining epochs (just starting each from batch 0).
proposed_fix: Introduce a `let mut continue_training = true;` before the epoch loop; on KL trip set it false and `break` the batch loop, then `if !continue_training { break; }` at the top/bottom of the epoch loop. This makes early stopping actually halt the update.
bench_to_confirm: ppo_benchmark with target_kl set low; assert n_updates stops growing after the first trip (add a counter).
verify_reasoning: no verdict returned

title: collect_rollout resets ALL environments at the start of every rollout, discarding episode continuation
description: `let mut obs = self.env.reset(&self.device)?;` is executed at the start of every rollout, and collect_rollout is called once per training iteration (train() line 659, train_step() line 738). This forcibly resets all envs to their initial state before each rollout. VecEnv already auto-resets individual envs on done (vecenv.rs auto_reset=true), and the rollout correctly bootstraps the tail with last_value (line 630-635) — which only makes sense if the episode CONTINUES into the next rollout. Resetting every rollout contradicts that design: the bootstrapped continuation state is immediately thrown away, and for any environment whose episode is longer than n_steps the agent never observes states beyond step n_steps (every rollout restarts from the initial-state distribution). Canonical PPO resets once at training start and then carries obs across rollouts.
proposed_fix: Persist the last observation across rollouts in the agent (e.g. an `Option<Tensor> last_obs`); reset only once (lazily on first rollout) and otherwise continue from the stored obs. Rely on VecEnv auto_reset for per-env episode boundaries.
bench_to_confirm: env/ppo bench on an env with episode length > n_steps; log distribution of observed states or mean episode length seen during training.
verify_reasoning: no verdict returned

title: Discrete action sampling does one GPU→CPU sync per environment per step
description: Inside the per-environment loop `for b in 0..batch_size`, the selected action's log-prob is fetched with `log_probs.get(b)?.flatten_all()?.to_vec1()?`. `to_vec1()` forces a device read; doing it once per environment means O(num_envs) separate device syncs per env step, on top of the single full `probs.to_vec1()` at line 278. On Metal/CUDA each sync serializes the pipeline. The whole log_probs tensor is already materializable in one transfer.
proposed_fix: Hoist `let log_probs_vec: Vec<f32> = log_probs.flatten_all()?.to_vec1()?;` before the loop and index `log_probs_vec[b * num_actions + action]`.
bench_to_confirm: env_benchmark / ppo_benchmark on a discrete env with large num_envs on Metal; compare rollout-collection time before/after.
verify_reasoning: no verdict returned

title: clip_gradients pulls each parameter's grad norm to a scalar individually (one device sync per Var per minibatch)
description: The global grad-norm loop calls `grad.sqr()?.sum_all()?.to_scalar()?` for every Var. Each `to_scalar()` is a separate GPU→CPU sync, so the number of device round-trips per minibatch equals the number of parameter tensors, and this runs for every minibatch of every epoch. The per-Var squared sums could be accumulated on-device into a single tensor and read once.
proposed_fix: Accumulate `grad.sqr()?.sum_all()?` into a running Tensor (broadcast_add) and call `.to_scalar()` once after the loop; or build a stacked tensor of per-var sums and sum_all once.
bench_to_confirm: gpu_benchmark / ppo_benchmark on Metal measuring update() time vs parameter count.
verify_reasoning: no verdict returned

title: CandleDevice is reconstructed on every forward and inside the minibatch loop instead of being cached
location: to_candle() call sites: 208, 239, 309, 332, 374, and 466 (inside batch loop)
description: `self.device.to_candle()?` is re-derived in policy_forward, value_forward, sample_action, evaluate_actions, and notably at line 466 INSIDE the `for batch_idx` minibatch loop. For the default `cpu` build this returns the trivial Cpu unit variant (cheap), but for `metal`/`cuda` builds `Device::to_candle` calls `CandleDevice::new_metal(0)` / `new_cuda(ordinal)` (src/core/device.rs:40-48), re-deriving the device handle on each call rather than reusing one. The agent owns a fixed Device, so the candle device can be built once and stored.
evidence:
device.rs: Device::Metal => CandleDevice::new_metal(0),  // re-derived each call
ppo.rs:466 (inside minibatch loop): let candle_device = self.device.to_candle()?;
proposed_fix: Compute `candle_device` once in PPOAgent::new and store it (e.g. a `candle_device: CandleDevice` field), reusing it in all forward/update paths instead of calling to_candle() repeatedly.
bench_to_confirm: ppo_benchmark/gpu_benchmark under the metal feature comparing update() and rollout times before/after caching.
verify_reasoning: no verdict returned

title: No PPO value clipping; old values are stored but never used in the update (and explained_variance is hardcoded 0)
location: update() value loss lines 495-497; metrics line 553; rollout.rs RolloutSample.values; buffer/mod.rs RolloutBatch.old_values
description: The value loss is plain MSE `(values - returns)^2` with no clipping against the old value predictions. PPOConfig exposes no clip_range_vf, and the stored old values are never consumed: rollout.rs::get_all() materializes `samples.values` (a tensor build per update) but update() only uses observations/actions/log_probs/advantages/returns. The companion buffer/mod.rs even documents `old_values` as '[batch_size] (for value clipping)' yet no code path applies it. Additionally explained_variance is hardcoded to 0.0 with a `// TODO: Compute`. Value clipping is optional in PPO, so this is not a numerical bug, but it is an unfinished feature and a wasted per-update tensor allocation.
evidence:
let value_diff = (&values - &batch_returns)?;
let value_loss = value_diff.sqr()?.mean_all()?;   // no clip vs old values
...
explained_variance: 0.0, // TODO: Compute
proposed_fix: Either (a) implement SB3-style clipped value loss using the stored old values when a clip_range_vf is configured, or (b) drop the unused values tensor from get_all/update to avoid the allocation. Also compute explained_variance from returns/values.
bench_to_confirm: ppo_benchmark; correctness via a unit test comparing clipped vs unclipped value loss.
verify_reasoning: no verdict returned

title: Policy/value networks are rebuilt from the VarMap (with format! string allocs) on every forward call
description: Each forward pass reconstructs every `candle_nn::Linear` via `candle_nn::linear(..., vb.pp(format!("{}.layer_{}", prefix, i)))`, allocating a fresh format! String per layer and re-fetching weight/bias Vars from the VarMap (HashMap lookups) on every call. This happens once per env step during rollout (sample_action + value_forward) and once per minibatch per epoch during update (evaluate_actions = policy_forward + value_forward). The module structure is fixed; the Linear layers could be constructed once and reused.
proposed_fix: Build the policy and value `Sequential`/`Linear` modules once at construction (or cache them) referencing the VarMap Vars, and call `.forward()` on the cached modules; avoid per-call format! + linear() reconstruction.
bench_to_confirm: ppo_benchmark measuring update()/rollout throughput before/after caching modules.
verify_reasoning: no verdict returned

title: Dead PPOAgent::compute_gae that, if used, would conflate terminated and truncated (mask bootstrap on truncation)
description: PPOAgent::compute_gae is never called (grep confirms only the definition; GAE is actually done in RolloutBuffer::compute_returns_and_advantages). The method takes a single combined `dones` and uses `mask = 1 - dones[t]` for BOTH the value bootstrap (`gamma * next_value * mask`) and the trace decay. That conflates terminated/truncated: if it were ever wired in, truncated steps would incorrectly get their bootstrap zeroed, reintroducing the exact value-collapse bug the rollout buffer was written to avoid. Dead, but a latent trap.
proposed_fix: Delete the unused method (the buffer already implements correct truncation-aware GAE), or if kept, split terminated/truncated like the buffer does.
verify_reasoning: no verdict returned

title: Two distinct public types both named RolloutBuffer (one unused by algorithms, with slower per-step tensor GAE)
location: buffer/mod.rs:76 RolloutBuffer vs algorithms/rollout.rs:58 RolloutBuffer; both re-exported in src/lib.rs:65 and 220
description: Two different `RolloutBuffer` structs are exported publicly: `crate::buffer::RolloutBuffer` (lib.rs:220, lib.rs:65) and `crate::algorithms::rollout::RolloutBuffer` (used by PPO). They have different constructors (`new(buffer_size, num_envs, &[usize] obs_shape, action_dim, &Device)` vs `new(n_steps, num_envs, obs_dim, act_dim, Device)`) and different GAE implementations. The buffer/mod.rs variant is not used by any algorithm (only its own tests), yet it is the one surfaced in the prelude-style re-export. This is an API/packaging hazard: users importing `octane::RolloutBuffer` get the slower, algorithm-unused implementation.
evidence:
lib.rs:220: pub use crate::buffer::{ReplayBuffer, ReplayBufferConfig, RolloutBuffer};
ppo.rs:11: use crate::algorithms::rollout::RolloutBuffer;  // different type
proposed_fix: Consolidate to a single RolloutBuffer (keep the flat-Vec/SIMD algorithms::rollout version used by PPO), or rename the buffer/mod.rs one (e.g. TensorRolloutBuffer) and stop re-exporting the unused variant under the same name.
verify_reasoning: no verdict returned

title: buffer/mod.rs GAE allocates two Tensor::ones per step and runs thousands of tiny tensor ops in the reverse loop
description: The tensor-based GAE loop creates a fresh `Tensor::ones([num_envs])` twice per timestep (lines 411 and 423) plus a chain of broadcast sub/mul/add ops and a `last_gae_lam.clone()` push, then `Tensor::stack`. For buffer_size=2048 that is ~4096 ones-allocations and tens of thousands of tiny per-step kernel launches/host dispatches — exactly the 'old suboptimal' pattern the sibling rollout.rs replaced with a scalar/SIMD loop over Vec<f32>. The two ones tensors are loop-invariant and should be hoisted; better, the whole computation should mirror rollout.rs's scalar/SIMD path.
proposed_fix: At minimum hoist a single `ones` tensor out of the loop. Better: compute GAE on flat Vec<f32> (CPU) like algorithms/rollout.rs and only build the final advantages/returns tensors once, or delegate to crate::simd::gae::compute_gae_simd_inplace.
bench_to_confirm: Micro-bench buffer::RolloutBuffer::compute_returns_and_advantages at buffer_size=2048, num_envs=64 vs the rollout.rs scalar path.
verify_reasoning: no verdict returned

title: rollout.rs module docs claim the SIMD GAE path requires the `simd` cargo feature, but it is gated on target_feature=neon (always-on for aarch64)
location: module doc lines 24-32 vs cfg gate lines 263-270
description: The doc comment instructs users to enable the `simd` cargo feature to get SIMD GAE, but the actual cfg gate is `all(target_arch="aarch64", target_feature="neon")` (and the AVX2 equivalent), which does not reference the cargo feature at all. NEON is baseline on aarch64, so the vectorized `compute_gae_simd_inplace` path runs on every Apple Silicon build regardless of cargo features. The docs are misleading about how to enable/what runs. (The SIMD path itself, verified in src/simd/gae.rs, is correct: bootstrap masked by terminated only, trace reset on terminated|truncated — matches the scalar fallback.)
evidence:
doc: `octane = { ..., features = ["simd"] }`  // implies opt-in
cfg: #[cfg(any(all(target_arch="aarch64", target_feature="neon"), all(target_arch="x86_64", target_feature="avx2", target_feature="fma")))]  // no `feature = "simd"`
proposed_fix: Update the docs to state the SIMD GAE path is selected automatically via target features (NEON baseline on Apple Silicon; AVX2+FMA on x86_64), independent of the `simd` cargo feature; or actually gate on the cargo feature if opt-in is intended.
verify_reasoning: no verdict returned

title: clip_fraction metric forces a full GPU→CPU transfer of the ratio tensor every minibatch
description: For the clip-fraction diagnostic, `ratio.flatten_all()?.to_vec1()?` copies the entire ratio tensor to host and filters in Rust, once per minibatch per epoch. This is a metric-only sync that can be computed on-device (count where |ratio-1| > clip_range) and read as a single scalar, avoiding a full-batch host transfer in the hot update loop.
evidence:
let ratio_vec: Vec<f32> = ratio.flatten_all()?.to_vec1()?;
let clip_frac = ratio_vec.iter().filter(|&&r| (r - 1.0).abs() > self.config.clip_range).count() as f32 / ratio_vec.len() as f32;
proposed_fix: Compute clip fraction on-device: build a mask `(ratio - 1).abs().gt(clip_range)`, take mean_all(), and to_scalar() once. Or compute it only every N updates.
bench_to_confirm: ppo_benchmark on Metal measuring update() time with/without the host-side clip_fraction computation.
verify_reasoning: no verdict returned

title: AdamW optimizer is recreated every gradient step, resetting moment buffers (degenerates to sign-SGD)
description: Every call to update() constructs brand-new AdamW optimizers (one per network) and performs a single backward_step before dropping them. Candle's AdamW::new() initializes first_moment/second_moment Vars to zeros and step_t=0; the moment estimates and bias-correction step counter therefore NEVER persist across training steps. With t always = 1, the bias-corrected update reduces to m_hat=g, v_hat=g^2, so adjusted_grad = g/(|g|+eps) ~= sign(g), i.e. a fixed-magnitude sign-SGD step of size lr regardless of gradient scale. Adam's adaptive momentum is completely lost for ALL four algorithms (SAC/TD3/DDPG/REDQ). REDQ is worst: it recreates ensemble_size (10) + policy optimizers, utd_ratio (20) times per env step. This is also a per-step heap/alloc + zeroed-tensor-creation hotspot.
evidence: sac.rs: `let mut q1_optimizer = AdamW::new(self.q1_var_map.all_vars(), params.clone())?; q1_optimizer.backward_step(&q1_loss)?;` (fresh each update). candle-nn 0.8.4 optim.rs: `fn new(...) { let first_moment = Var::zeros(...)?; let second_moment = Var::zeros(...)?; ... Ok(Self { vars, params, step_t: 0 }) }` and `fn step(...) { self.step_t += 1; let scale_m = 1/(1-beta1.powi(step_t)); ... }` -> step_t resets to 0 on every new optimizer.
proposed_fix: Construct the optimizers once (store them as fields on the agent, e.g. q1_optimizer: AdamW) and reuse across update() calls so moment buffers and step_t accumulate. Same fix for actor/critic/policy/ensemble optimizers in all four agents.
bench_to_confirm: ppo/sac convergence micro-bench comparing persistent vs per-step optimizer on Pendulum-like env; also a per-update wall-clock micro-bench to quantify alloc overhead.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed against source. sac.rs:435-453, td3.rs:441-471, ddpg.rs:433-452, redq.rs:704-727 all construct `AdamW::new(...)` locally inside update() and call backward_step once, then drop. candle-nn 0.8.4 optim.rs:120-142 shows new() initializes first_moment/second_moment as Var::zeros and step_t=0; step() (line 153) increments step_t to 1. With t=1 and default betas (0.9/0.999): next_m=0.1g, m_hat=0.1g*10=g; next_v=0.001g^2, v_hat=0.001g^2*1000=g^2; adjusted_grad=g/(|g|+eps)~=sign(g), a fixed-magnitude lr step. Momentum/adaptive scaling never persist. Real correctness/training defect across all four agents; REDQ recreates ensemble_size(10)+policy optimizers utd_ratio(20)x per step. Fix (store optimizers as private struct fields and reuse) is the canonical candle loop; Var::set mutates storage in place at a stable TensorId so a reused optimizer's grads still resolve. Struct fields are private, no public API break.

title: SAC automatic temperature (alpha) update has inverted sign
description: The manual log_alpha update moves the temperature in the WRONG direction. Reference (CleanRL/Haarnoja): alpha_loss = (-log_alpha * (log_pi + target_entropy)).detach(), grad wrt log_alpha = alpha*(mean(log_pi)+target_entropy), gradient DESCENT gives log_alpha -= lr*alpha*(mean(log_pi)+target_entropy). Here diff = mean(-log_pi) - target_entropy = current_entropy - target_entropy, so (mean(log_pi)+target_entropy) = -diff, and the correct update is log_alpha -= lr*alpha*diff i.e. log_alpha - lr*alpha*diff. The code computes alpha_grad = -diff*alpha and then new_log_alpha = log_alpha - lr*alpha_grad = log_alpha + lr*diff*alpha. Numeric check: action_dim=2 -> target_entropy=-2; policy too deterministic so current_entropy=-5 -> diff=-3<0; we WANT alpha to increase. Code gives log_alpha + lr*(-3)*alpha -> alpha DECREASES (wrong). This drives alpha away from the target-entropy fixed point (collapse toward 0 or divergence). auto_entropy_tuning defaults to true, so the bug is active by default.
evidence: Lines: `let neg_log_pi = log_pi_detached.neg()?; let diff = neg_log_pi.mean_all()?.to_scalar::<f32>()? - self.target_entropy; let alpha_grad = -diff * self.alpha; let new_log_alpha = self.log_alpha.to_scalar::<f32>()? - self.config.learning_rate * alpha_grad; self.alpha = new_log_alpha.exp();` -> expands to log_alpha + lr*diff*alpha.
proposed_fix: Flip the sign: alpha_grad = diff * self.alpha (so new_log_alpha = log_alpha - lr*diff*alpha), or equivalently new_log_alpha = log_alpha - lr*(mean(-log_pi)-target_entropy)*alpha with the negation removed. Also clamp log_alpha (e.g. to [-10, 10]) before exp() to avoid f32 overflow given the manual update has no autograd safeguards.
bench_to_confirm: SAC micro-run logging alpha and policy entropy over time; with the fix entropy should converge toward target_entropy, currently it diverges.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at sac.rs:456-471. Correct dual update (CleanRL/Haarnoja) on log_alpha is log_alpha + lr*alpha*(mean(log_pi)+target_entropy). With code's diff = mean(-log_pi) - target_entropy = -(mean(log_pi)+target_entropy), the correct update equals log_alpha - lr*alpha*diff. Code computes alpha_grad=-diff*alpha and new_log_alpha = log_alpha - lr*alpha_grad = log_alpha + lr*diff*alpha -- the exact negation of the gradient, independent of target_entropy sign convention. When entropy is below target (diff<0) alpha should rise but the code lowers it, pushing away from the fixed point. auto_entropy_tuning defaults true (config.rs:567), so active by default. Fix (flip sign: alpha_grad = diff*alpha) corrects the bug without API change.

title: REDQ automatic temperature (alpha) update has the same inverted sign as SAC
description: Identical inverted-sign bug as SAC. diff = mean(-log_pi) - target_entropy; alpha_grad = -diff*alpha; new_log_alpha = log_alpha - lr*alpha_grad = log_alpha + lr*diff*alpha, which is gradient ascent on the alpha objective. The temperature is pushed away from the target-entropy fixed point. auto_entropy_tuning defaults to true in REDQConfig.
evidence: `let diff = neg_log_pi.mean_all()?.to_scalar::<f32>()? - self.target_entropy; let alpha_grad = -diff * self.alpha; let new_log_alpha = self.log_alpha.to_scalar::<f32>()? - self.config.learning_rate * alpha_grad; self.alpha = new_log_alpha.exp();`
proposed_fix: Same as SAC: use alpha_grad = diff * self.alpha (descent: log_alpha - lr*diff*alpha) and clamp log_alpha before exp().
bench_to_confirm: REDQ micro-run logging alpha/entropy; same as SAC.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at redq.rs:730-745: identical pattern to SAC -- diff = mean(-log_pi) - target_entropy; alpha_grad = -diff*alpha; new_log_alpha = log_alpha - lr*alpha_grad = log_alpha + lr*diff*alpha, the negation of the correct gradient-descent step (should be log_alpha - lr*diff*alpha). REDQConfig auto_entropy_tuning defaults true (redq.rs:111), so active by default. Same safe sign-flip fix.

title: REDQ policy update uses MIN over the full ensemble instead of the MEAN (deviates from REDQ paper)
description: REDQ (Chen et al. 2021, Algorithm 1) updates the policy using the AVERAGE of all N Q-functions: grad of (1/N) sum_i Q_i(s, a~) - alpha*log_pi. The code instead takes the elementwise minimum over ALL ensemble members (10 by default) for the policy loss. min over 10 networks is far more pessimistic than the intended mean and systematically underestimates Q, biasing the actor. The in-code comment even says 'Use all Q-networks for policy update, take mean' but the implementation takes min. (Note: using a random-subset min is correct for the TARGET computation at line 679, but not for the policy update.)
proposed_fix: Replace the min-reduction with a mean over the ensemble: sum the q_values and divide by ensemble_size (or stack and mean over the ensemble axis) before forming policy_loss = (alpha*log_pi - mean_q).mean().
bench_to_confirm: REDQ sample-efficiency micro-bench (e.g. on a Pendulum/HalfCheetah-like env) comparing mean vs min policy target; mean should match published REDQ behavior.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at redq.rs:715-724: comment says 'take mean' but code folds q_values[0..] with .minimum() over all ensemble_size(=10) Q-networks, then policy_loss = (alpha*log_pi - min_q).mean(). REDQ (Chen et al. 2021, Alg 1) updates the actor with the AVERAGE of the N Q-functions; in-target minimization over a random subset (num_q_samples=2) is correct only for the target (redq.rs:679 random_subset_target_q), not the policy update. min over 10 networks is a strong downward bias on the actor objective -- a genuine algorithmic deviation. Marked medium rather than high because the actor still receives a valid ascent direction on a pessimistic lower bound (bias, not divergence); high is also defensible. Fix (sum q_values / ensemble_size, or stack+mean) matches the paper and breaks no public API.

title: Time-limit truncation is treated as termination -> Bellman bootstrap incorrectly cut
location: update() lines 415-418 + train() 514/525; recurs td3.rs:415-417, ddpg.rs:417-419, redq.rs:682-685
description: All four agents store a single boolean done = dones_vec[i] > 0.5, where VecStepResult::dones() returns terminated OR truncated (vecenv.rs:184-190). ReplayBuffer::add (replay.rs:192) only accepts one `done` flag, so truncation (time-limit) is indistinguishable from true termination. The TD target uses not_done = 1 - done, so on a time-limit truncation the bootstrap gamma*Q(s', a') is zeroed even though the MDP did not actually end. Standard implementations (SB3, CleanRL) bootstrap on truncation. For typical fixed-horizon continuous-control / trading episodes this injects a systematic negative bias into value estimates near episode boundaries.
evidence: vecenv.rs:184 `pub fn dones(&self) -> Result<Tensor> { ... (terminated + truncated).ge(1.0) }`; train loop stores `dones_vec[i] > 0.5`; update(): `let not_done = (Tensor::ones_like(&batch.dones)? - &batch.dones)?; let td_target = (&batch.rewards + (&target_q * gamma)? * &not_done)?.detach();`
proposed_fix: Propagate terminated and truncated separately into the replay buffer; use only `terminated` (not truncated) for the not_done mask in the Bellman target. Requires extending ReplayBuffer::add and the transition struct to carry a separate truncated/terminated flag.
bench_to_confirm: Off-policy env bench on a TimeLimit-wrapped task comparing value-estimate bias / return with terminated-only vs terminated|truncated masking.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed. vecenv.rs:184-190 dones() returns terminated OR truncated as f32. Train loops store a single bool done=dones_vec[i]>0.5 (sac.rs:525, td3.rs:522, ddpg.rs:497, redq.rs ~810) and ReplayBuffer::add (replay.rs:192) stores one done flag. TD target uses not_done=1-done (sac.rs:415-418, redq.rs:682-685, td3.rs:415-417, ddpg.rs:417-419), so on a time-limit truncation gamma*Q(s',a') is zeroed even though the MDP did not end -- a known correctness gap vs SB3/CleanRL that injects negative value bias near fixed-horizon boundaries (relevant for the trading/continuous-control envs here). is_real=true. fix_is_safe=false ONLY because the proposed fix as written extends `pub fn add` (replay.rs:192), a breaking public-API change; the correctness logic itself is sound (it changes no currently-correct result, only the buggy truncation path). An additive method (e.g. add_with_truncation) or an extra field would be an API-compatible alternative.

title: Networks (Linear layers) are reconstructed with format!-allocated names on every forward pass
location: q_forward 523-560, policy_forward 446-476, ensemble_q_values 562-570 (same pattern in sac.rs:203-321, td3.rs:200-269, ddpg.rs:229-296)
description: Each forward pass rebuilds every layer via candle_nn::linear(...) inside a loop, allocating a fresh String per layer with format!("{}.layer_{}", prefix, i) and constructing a new Linear/VarBuilder, plus calling self.device.to_candle()? again. gaussian_log_prob also allocates a fresh scalar tensor Tensor::new(&[log_2pi], ...) every call. This runs for every Q/policy evaluation; in REDQ the cost is multiplied by ensemble_size and utd_ratio (10 x 20 = 200 full ensemble rebuilds + hundreds of format! allocs per env step). Weights themselves are not re-initialized (VarBuilder reuses existing Vars), but the per-call struct construction, string formatting, and device lookups are pure overhead on the hot path.
evidence: `let linear = candle_nn::linear(in_dim, hidden_size, vb.pp(format!("{}.layer_{}", prefix, i)))?; h = linear.forward(&h)?;` inside the per-layer loop of every forward fn; `let candle_device = self.device.to_candle()?;` at the top of each forward; `Tensor::new(&[log_2pi], &candle_device)?` per gaussian_log_prob call.
proposed_fix: Build each network's Linear modules once at init (store Vec<Linear> or a Sequential per network) and reuse them in forward; cache the candle Device on the struct; precompute the log_2pi constant once. This removes per-step allocations and string formatting from the training inner loop.
bench_to_confirm: gpu/ppo micro-bench timing update() before/after caching modules, especially REDQ with default ensemble_size=10, utd_ratio=20.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed. q_forward/policy_forward in redq.rs:446-560, sac.rs:202-321, td3.rs and ddpg.rs rebuild every Linear via candle_nn::linear(... vb.pp(format!("{}.layer_{}", prefix, i))) inside the per-layer loop, call self.device.to_candle()? at function entry, and gaussian_log_prob allocates Tensor::new(&[log_2pi],...) per call (sac.rs:272-274, redq.rs:512-514). Weights are NOT re-initialized (VarBuilder::from_varmap retrieves existing Vars), but per-call String formatting, Linear struct construction, varmap RwLock get(), and device lookup are genuine hot-path overhead, multiplied by ensemble_size*utd_ratio in REDQ. Downgraded to low because matmul/GPU compute (and on-GPU the device syncs) dominate cost relative to string allocs/lock churn. Fix (build modules once at init, cache Device, precompute log_2pi) is the canonical candle pattern and an internal-only refactor; Var::set mutates storage in place so cached modules see updated weights -- no stale-weight risk.

title: Five full GPU->CPU tensor syncs per environment step in the training loops
description: Every environment step copies obs, actions, next_obs, rewards, and dones from device to host via flatten_all()?.to_vec1()? / to_vec1()? to feed the CPU-side ReplayBuffer one transition at a time in a per-env loop. On Metal/CUDA each to_vec1() is a blocking device->host sync; doing five per step (plus per-env slice copies) serializes the pipeline and stalls the GPU. This is structural (ReplayBuffer stores host f32), but the per-step, per-tensor sync pattern is the dominant CPU<->GPU churn in the rollout phase.
evidence: `let obs_vec: Vec<f32> = obs.flatten_all()?.to_vec1()?; let action_vec: Vec<f32> = actions...to_vec1()?; let next_obs_vec ...; let rewards_vec ...; let dones_vec ...;` followed by a per-env loop calling replay_buffer.add(slice, slice, ...).
proposed_fix: Batch the device->host transfer (single contiguous copy or a ReplayBuffer::add_tensor batched insert, which already exists at replay.rs:231) and/or keep rollout tensors on host only when actually inserting; avoid repeated flatten_all/to_vec1 per field per step.
bench_to_confirm: env/gpu bench measuring steps/sec on Metal with batched add_tensor vs per-field to_vec1 inserts.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at sac.rs:510-514, td3.rs:507-511, ddpg.rs:482-486, redq.rs:792-796: each env step calls flatten_all()?.to_vec1()? / to_vec1()? on obs, actions, next_obs, rewards, dones to feed the host-side ReplayBuffer one transition per env. On Metal/CUDA each to_vec1() is a blocking device->host sync, so 5 per step serialize the rollout pipeline -- a real hot-path cost. is_real=true, low (structural since ReplayBuffer stores host f32; on CPU device these are not true syncs). Precision note: the finding's suggested fix references add_tensor at replay.rs:231 as a 'batched insert', but add_tensor is single-transition and itself calls to_vec1 per tensor (replay.rs:239-243), so it is NOT batched. The core perf claim still stands; the safe fix is a single contiguous host transfer / genuinely batched insert, which does not change results.

title: AdamW optimizer recreated every gradient step — moment estimates and step counter reset to zero each update
description: Every call to update() constructs a brand-new AdamW from all_vars() and immediately does one backward_step. AdamW::new zeroes the first/second moment buffers (m,v) and the timestep t, so EVERY gradient step runs as Adam's t=1: bias-corrected m_hat=g, v_hat=g^2, giving an update of ~lr*g/(|g|+eps) ≈ lr*sign(g). Momentum and adaptive scaling never accumulate across steps. This silently cripples optimization for DQN, IQN and CQL (CQL recreates 3 separate optimizers per step). It also makes loss-scale findings moot since updates become scale-invariant.
evidence: let mut optimizer = AdamW::new(self.q_var_map.all_vars(), params)?;\n optimizer.backward_step(&loss)?;  // fresh optimizer => state(m,v,t) reset every step. Confirmed: candle-core 0.8.4 AdamW::new initializes zeroed VarAdamW state.
proposed_fix: Construct the AdamW optimizer(s) once at agent init (store in the struct) and reuse across update() calls so momentum/variance/timestep persist. For CQL keep three persistent optimizers (q1, q2, policy) plus the alpha updates.
bench_to_confirm: ppo/dqn micro-bench: compare learning curve with persistent vs per-step optimizer on a toy env
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: dqn.rs:327, iqn.rs:637, and cql.rs:637/640/653 each call `AdamW::new(...all_vars(), params)` inside `update()` then immediately `backward_step`. Verified against candle-nn 0.8.4 optim.rs: AdamW::new sets first_moment/second_moment = Var::zeros and step_t=0 (lines 128-140); backward_step does step_t += 1 (line 153). So every gradient step runs as Adam t=1: m_hat=g, v_hat=g^2, update = lr*g/(|g|+eps) ≈ lr*sign(g). Momentum/adaptive scaling never accumulate; this affects DQN, IQN, and all three CQL optimizers. Severity high (not critical): it degrades Adam to ~sign-SGD but training still proceeds and does not crash/produce wrong outputs. Fix (persist optimizer(s) in the struct) is safe — candle Var::set updates storage in place and AdamW holds Var clones sharing that storage, so reuse correctly accumulates state; no API change.

title: IQN quantile-Huber TD-error sign inverted — learned quantiles are mirrored (tau <-> 1-tau), inverting CVaR/Optimistic risk selection
description: The pinball/quantile-Huber loss is asymmetric in the sign of u = (target - prediction): rho = |tau - I(u<0)|*L_kappa(u). Here td_errors = current - target = -u, so I(td_errors<0)=I(prediction>target)=I(u>0), the COMPLEMENT of the canonical indicator. Solving the equilibrium for tau=0.9 gives a prediction with 0.9 mass ABOVE it (the 0.1 quantile): Z_tau converges to the (1-tau) quantile. Mean over uniform taus is mirror-invariant so risk-neutral argmax survives, but compute_risk_q_values CVaR(alpha) (averages taus<=alpha) then averages what are really the UPPER quantiles — risk-averse selection becomes risk-seeking. This breaks the headline risk-sensitive feature for the trading use case.
evidence: let current_expanded = current_values_selected.unsqueeze(2)?; let target_expanded = td_targets.unsqueeze(1)?; let td_errors = current_expanded.broadcast_sub(&target_expanded)?;  // = current - target = -delta\n// in quantile_huber_loss: let negative_mask = td_errors.lt(0.0)?...; let tau_weight = (taus - &negative_mask)?.abs()?;
proposed_fix: Define td_errors = target - current (target_expanded.broadcast_sub(&current_expanded)), or equivalently flip the indicator to I(td_errors>0). Add a unit test asserting Z_tau for a fixed target distribution recovers the correct quantile (e.g. tau=0.9 -> high value).
bench_to_confirm: micro-test: fit IQN to a fixed skewed target distribution, check Z_0.1 < Z_0.9
verify_reasoning: Confirmed. iqn.rs:620-622 sets td_errors = current - target = -delta (delta = target - prediction is the canonical IQN u). quantile_huber_loss (iqn.rs:489-490) uses negative_mask = I(td_errors<0) = I(delta>0), the complement of the canonical I(delta<0); Huber is symmetric so only the indicator matters. Weight |tau - I(delta>0)| equals canonical |tau' - I(delta<0)| with tau'=1-tau, so Z_tau converges to the (1-tau) quantile. compute_risk_q_values Mean (the default RiskMeasure, iqn.rs:391-393) is mirror-invariant over uniform taus so risk-neutral argmax survives, but CVaR(alpha) (averages taus<=alpha) then averages what are really the UPPER quantiles, inverting risk-averse into risk-seeking — breaking the headline risk-sensitive feature for non-default risk measures. Fix (td_errors = target - current, or flip the indicator) corrects to the intended quantile, leaves PER priorities/Huber magnitude unchanged, no API change.

title: CQL SAC entropy-temperature (alpha) auto-tuning update has inverted sign
description: Canonical SAC temperature update (descent on alpha_loss=-alpha*(log_pi+H_target)) gives log_alpha <- log_alpha - lr*alpha*(H - H_target), where H=E[-log_pi]. The code computes diff=H-H_target, alpha_grad=-diff*alpha, then new_log_alpha = log_alpha - lr*alpha_grad = log_alpha + lr*alpha*(H - H_target) — the OPPOSITE sign. So when entropy is above target it INCREASES alpha (stronger entropy bonus), a positive-feedback loop driving alpha to its clamp instead of regulating entropy. Corroboration: the CQL-Lagrange update 40 lines up (cql.rs:615) correctly uses '+' for ascent, so the two manual updates in the same file disagree and the SAC one is wrong.
evidence:
let neg_log_pi = log_pi_detached.neg()?;            // = -log_pi = H
 let diff = neg_log_pi.mean_all()?...?- self.target_entropy; // H - H_target
 let alpha_grad = -diff * self.alpha;                 // -(H-H_target)*alpha
 let new_log_alpha = self.log_alpha...? - self.config.learning_rate * alpha_grad; // => +lr*alpha*(H-H_target)
proposed_fix: Use alpha_grad = diff * self.alpha (i.e. log_alpha <- log_alpha - lr*alpha*(H - H_target)); equivalently drop the leading minus on alpha_grad.
bench_to_confirm: offline CQL run: log alpha trajectory — should converge, not saturate at clamp
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at cql.rs:659-666. neg_log_pi = -log_pi = H; diff = H - target_entropy; alpha_grad = -diff*alpha; new_log_alpha = log_alpha - lr*alpha_grad = log_alpha + lr*alpha*(H - H_target). Canonical SAC temperature descent is log_alpha <- log_alpha - lr*(H - H_target) (alpha factor is a positive rescale, irrelevant to sign), so the sign is flipped: when entropy exceeds target it INCREASES alpha, a positive-feedback loop driving alpha to its clamp. auto_entropy_tuning defaults to true (config.rs:982), so this is active in the default config. Corroborated by the CQL-Lagrange dual ascent at cql.rs:615 correctly using '+', confirming the two manual updates disagree and the SAC one is wrong. Fix (alpha_grad = diff*alpha) restores correct regulation; no API change, fixes a currently-wrong result.

title: CQL conservative penalty mixes Q-values across different states (repeat/reshape order mismatch)
description: obs.repeat(&[num_random,1]) block-tiles: confirmed in candle-core 0.8.4 (repeat = Tensor::cat(vec![&inp; repeat], dim0)), so row r = obs[r mod batch]. q1_rand is then reshape((batch_size, num_random)) which is row-major: q2d[i][j] = flat[i*num_random + j] = Q for state obs[(i*num_random+j) mod batch]. Thus each row's logsumexp (over dim 1) aggregates Q-values from num_random DIFFERENT states, not num_random random actions for one state, and is then combined with current_q1[i] for the true state i. The conservative term — the entire point of CQL — is computed over a state/action grid that is misaligned. The estimate is corrupted for any batch_size>1.
evidence: let obs_repeated = obs.repeat(&[num_random, 1])?;   // row r = obs[r % batch]\n let q1_rand = self.q_forward(&obs_repeated, &random_actions, ...)?; // [batch*num_random]\n let q1_rand = q1_rand.reshape((batch_size, num_random))?; // q2d[i][j]=flat[i*num_random+j] -> state obs[(i*num_random+j)%batch]
proposed_fix: Tile per-row interleaved so each state is repeated num_random consecutive times: obs.unsqueeze(1).broadcast_as((batch, num_random, dim)).reshape((batch*num_random, dim)); generate random_actions in the same [batch, num_random, action_dim] layout. Then reshape((batch_size,num_random)) is consistent.
bench_to_confirm: 5-line cargo test: confirm repeat tiling order (done: block-tile) + assert q2d row i uses obs[i]
verify_reasoning: Confirmed against candle-core 0.8.4 tensor.rs:620: Tensor::repeat([num_random,1]) = cat(&vec![&inp; num_random], 0), block-tiling so obs_repeated[r] = obs[r % batch] (cql.rs:423). q1_rand is then reshape((batch_size, num_random)) row-major (cql.rs:430), so row i contains Q for states obs[(i*num_random+j) % batch] — num_random DIFFERENT states, not num_random random actions for state i. The dominant CQL(H) term (default 10 random actions vs 2 policy) is logsumexp'd over a misaligned state/action grid and then subtracted against current_q1[i] for the true state i, corrupting the conservative penalty for any batch_size>1. Fix (broadcast obs to [batch,num_random,dim] then reshape) is correct and safe; note the broadcast result is non-contiguous so a .contiguous() is needed before reshape — trivial, does not affect correctness or API.

title: CQL logsumexp has no max-subtraction — f32 overflow risk in conservative penalty
description: logsumexp is computed as temp*log(sum(exp(q/temp))) with no max-subtraction. In offline RL Q-values can grow large; with cql_temp default 1.0, exp(q) overflows to +inf in f32 for q>~88, producing inf/NaN gradients precisely when conservative regularization matters most. Standard stable form subtracts the per-row max.
evidence: let q1_scaled = (&q1_all / temp as f64)?;\n let logsumexp_q1 = (q1_scaled.exp()?.sum(1)?.log()? * temp as f64)?;  // no max subtraction
proposed_fix: m = q_scaled.max_keepdim(1); logsumexp = temp*(m.squeeze + (q_scaled - m).exp().sum(1).log()). Subtract the row max before exp.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at cql.rs:464-469: logsumexp = temp*log(sum(exp(q/temp))) with no per-row max subtraction; candle does not auto-stabilize a manual exp/sum/log chain. With default cql_temp=1.0, exp(q) overflows f32 to +inf for q>~88, yielding inf/NaN gradients. The trigger is realistic: the logsumexp is evaluated over OOD/random-action Q-values in offline RL — precisely the overestimation regime CQL exists to counter — before the penalty suppresses them. Standard stable logsumexp subtracts the row max. Fix (subtract max_keepdim before exp, add it back) is numerically identical in exact arithmetic, just stable — safe, no API change.

title: Truncation (time-limit) treated as termination — bootstrap incorrectly cut at episode time limits
description: The train loop stores done = step_result.dones() into the replay buffer, and VecStepResult::dones() (vecenv.rs:184) returns terminated OR truncated. compute_td_target uses not_done=(1-dones) to zero the bootstrap. For time-limit truncations (truncated=true, terminated=false) the value bootstrap is wrongly removed, biasing Q targets low on every truncated episode. Reference (SB3/Gym) bootstraps on truncation and only cuts on true termination.
evidence: dones_vec = step_result.dones()?...; replay_buffer.add(..., dones_vec[i] > 0.5);  // dones = terminated|truncated\n compute_td_target: let not_done = (Tensor::ones_like(&batch.dones)? - &batch.dones)?;
proposed_fix: Store terminated (not terminated|truncated) as the bootstrap mask, or store both and bootstrap with (1-terminated) while still ending the episode on truncation. Add the bootstrap value of the truncated next state.
bench_to_confirm: env bench with a time-limit wrapper: compare returns with terminated-only vs done mask
verify_reasoning: Core issue is real: VecStepResult::dones() returns terminated OR truncated (vecenv.rs:184), this single mask is stored as the buffer terminal flag (dqn.rs:388, iqn.rs:703), and compute_td_target uses not_done=(1-dones) to zero the bootstrap (dqn.rs:285, iqn.rs:583). So time-limit truncations wrongly remove the value bootstrap, biasing Q targets low — contrary to SB3/Gym which bootstrap on truncation. However fix_is_safe=false: VecEnv has auto_reset=true by default (vecenv.rs:25,75,402), so step_result.observations on a done step is already the RESET (start-of-next-episode) observation and the true terminal next state is discarded. Naively storing `terminated` and bootstrapping on truncation would bootstrap V(reset_obs) from the wrong state — a new correctness bug. A correct fix must first capture the true terminal observation (e.g., via StepInfo) before flipping the mask.

title: Epsilon-greedy uses a single RNG draw for the entire vectorized batch — all envs explore or exploit together
description: select_action draws one self.rng.gen::<f32>() and compares to epsilon to decide exploration for ALL num_envs at once: either the whole batch takes random actions or the whole batch takes greedy actions. Per-env independent exploration is required; this collapses exploration diversity across the vectorized batch every step, strongly correlating the data written to the replay buffer.
evidence: if training && self.rng.gen::<f32>() < self.epsilon { /* random actions for all envs */ } else { /* greedy for all envs */ }
proposed_fix: Make the epsilon decision per environment: draw a Bernoulli(epsilon) per row and select random vs greedy action element-wise (e.g. compute greedy argmax for the batch, then overwrite the subset of rows chosen for exploration).
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at dqn.rs:245-261 and iqn.rs:443-468: a single self.rng.gen::<f32>() is compared to epsilon to decide exploration for the whole batch — either all num_envs take random actions or all take greedy actions. Per-env independent Bernoulli(epsilon) exploration is standard; collapsing it across the vectorized batch strongly correlates the transitions written to the replay buffer each step. Only impactful when num_envs>1, but the code explicitly supports VecEnv. Fix (per-row Bernoulli draw, overwrite the explored subset with random actions while computing greedy argmax for the rest) is safe and does not change the API.

title: CQL importance-sampling / uniform-action density correction computed then discarded — biased conservative penalty
description: CQL(H) requires importance-weighting the logsumexp: random actions contribute log(1/(2^d)) = -d*log(2) (uniform density on [-1,1]^d) and policy actions contribute -log pi(a|s). The code computes policy_log_prob_correction but assigns it to _correction and never applies it; the uniform-action log-density term is absent entirely. The resulting logsumexp over a raw concat of [num_random random + 2 policy] Q-values is a biased estimate of the soft-max, so the conservative lower bound is not the intended one.
evidence: let policy_log_prob_correction = (policy_log_probs.detach() + next_policy_log_probs.detach())?;\n let _correction = policy_log_prob_correction; // Used in full implementation\n// q1_all = cat([q1_rand, q1_policy, q1_next_policy]); logsumexp over it with no density correction
proposed_fix: Subtract the per-sample log-densities before logsumexp: for random actions use -d*ln(2); for policy actions use the detached policy log-prob. cat the corrected (q - logprob) terms, then logsumexp.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at cql.rs:471-474: policy_log_prob_correction is computed (detached) then bound to _correction and never applied; the uniform random-action log-density term (-action_dim*ln(2) for uniform on [-1,1]^d) is absent entirely. The logsumexp (cql.rs:445-469) is taken over a raw concat of [num_random random + 2 policy] Q-values with no density correction, so it is a biased estimate of the intended soft-max and the conservative lower bound is not the CQL(H) one (matches official CQL: cat([q_rand - random_density, q_pi - log_pi.detach(), ...]) before logsumexp). Fix (subtract -d*ln(2) for random and detached policy log-probs for policy actions before logsumexp) is safe as specified — the log-probs must be detached (as in the existing computation) so no spurious gradients are injected; no API change.

title: Per-step network module rebuild, string allocs, and GPU<->CPU syncs in hot train/update loops
location: q_forward dqn.rs:169-203 & iqn.rs:292-377 & cql.rs:354-390; train loop tensor_to_batch_vecs dqn.rs:375-400, 483-491
description: Every forward pass reconstructs the VarBuilder and each candle_nn::Linear via format!("{}.layer_{}") string allocations and hashmap lookups (multiple forwards per env step; CQL q_forward is called 6x per update in compute_cql_penalty plus more). Separately, each train step calls tensor_to_batch_vecs (flatten_all + to_vec1 = GPU->CPU sync + heap Vec<Vec<f32>>) on obs, actions, and next_obs, plus rewards/dones to_vec1, then adds to the replay buffer one transition at a time. On Metal this forces a device sync and many small allocations every single step.
evidence: let linear = candle_nn::linear(in_dim, hidden_size, vb.pp(format!("{}.layer_{}", prefix, i)))?; // rebuilt every forward\n let flat: Vec<f32> = tensor.flatten_all()?.to_vec1()?; // GPU->CPU sync per step, per tensor
proposed_fix: Build the Linear/Sequential modules once at init and store them (or cache module structs keyed by prefix); reuse across forwards. Batch the replay-buffer insertion (add a slice API) and minimize per-step to_vec1 syncs.
bench_to_confirm: gpu_benchmark / ppo_benchmark style micro-bench: steps/sec with cached modules vs rebuild
verify_reasoning: Confirmed on hot paths: q_forward (dqn.rs:187-200), iqn_forward_with_taus (iqn.rs:312-371), and cql q_forward (cql.rs:374-387) reconstruct each candle_nn::Linear via vb.pp(format!("{}.layer_{}")) on every forward — String allocation + hashmap lookup + Module struct creation per layer, multiple forwards per step (CQL calls q_forward 6x in compute_cql_penalty alone). Separately, tensor_to_batch_vecs (dqn.rs:483-491) does flatten_all().to_vec1() = a device->host sync per tensor, called on obs/actions/next_obs plus rewards/dones every train iteration, then adds one transition at a time. On Metal/CUDA each to_vec1 forces a sync. Fix (cache modules at init; batch buffer inserts; minimize to_vec1) is safe specifically because Var::set does in-place copy_strided_src into shared storage (verified variable.rs:130) and VarBuilder.get returns a storage-sharing clone — cached modules see optimizer updates and stay correct; no API change.

title: Polyak (soft) target update gated behind target_update_interval instead of running every step
description: When config.tau < 1.0 the agent does a Polyak soft update, but only inside the if steps_since_target_update >= target_update_interval block. Standard soft-update (tau~0.005) is applied every gradient/env step; applying tau-blending only once per target_update_interval (default 10_000) means the target barely tracks the online net (e.g. one 0.005 blend per 10k steps), effectively freezing the target. Hard-update path (tau=1.0 default) is fine.
evidence: self.steps_since_target_update += num_envs; if self.steps_since_target_update >= self.config.target_update_interval { if self.config.tau < 1.0 { self.soft_update_target()? } else { self.hard_update_target()? } ... }
proposed_fix: Run soft_update_target every training step (decouple from the interval, which should only gate hard updates), matching SB3/SAC semantics.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at dqn.rs:418-426 and iqn.rs:733-741: soft_update_target() is only invoked inside the `steps_since_target_update >= target_update_interval` block, so a tau-blend (e.g. 0.005) is applied only once per interval (default 10_000) rather than every step, effectively freezing the target. Real but low: DQN/IQN default tau=1.0 (config.rs:441,1199) which takes the correct hard-update path; the bug only manifests when a user sets tau<1.0. Fix (run soft_update every training step, gate only hard updates by the interval) matches SB3/SAC semantics, is safe, and does not change the default-config behavior or API.

title: Epsilon decayed once per loop iteration, not per timestep — schedule scales with num_envs
description: update_epsilon() subtracts epsilon_decay once per while-loop iteration, but total_timesteps advances by num_envs each iteration. So the number of timesteps to anneal from epsilon_start to epsilon_end is (start-end)/decay iterations = that many * num_envs timesteps — the effective exploration schedule silently stretches proportionally to num_envs, making behavior depend on the vectorization width.
evidence: self.epsilon = (self.epsilon - self.config.epsilon_decay).max(self.config.epsilon_end); // called once per iteration; self.total_timesteps += num_envs;
proposed_fix: Decay by epsilon_decay*num_envs per iteration, or compute epsilon as a function of total_timesteps (linear fraction) independent of num_envs.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: update_epsilon() (dqn.rs:344-346, iqn.rs:654-656) subtracts epsilon_decay once per while-loop iteration (called at dqn.rs:429, iqn.rs:744) while total_timesteps advances by num_envs per iteration (dqn.rs:403). So the anneal takes (start-end)/decay iterations = that many * num_envs timesteps, stretching the exploration schedule proportionally to vectorization width. Real but low: identical to intended only when num_envs=1. Fix (decay by epsilon_decay*num_envs per iteration, or compute epsilon as a linear function of total_timesteps) is safe and API-compatible.

title: DQN Huber loss computes |u| via sqrt(sqr(.)) — infinite/NaN gradient at td_error == 0
description: The forward value of the Huber loss is mathematically correct, but linear = abs_error - quadratic.sqrt() recovers max(|u|-1,0) via sqrt of a squared/clamped quantity. d/dx sqrt(x) is infinite at x=0, so when td_error is exactly 0 the backward pass yields a NaN/inf gradient. Rare in f32 but a needlessly hazardous formulation.
evidence: let quadratic = abs_error.clamp(0.0, 1.0)?.sqr()?; let linear = (abs_error - &quadratic.sqrt()?)?; ((quadratic * 0.5)? + &linear)?.mean_all()?
proposed_fix: Compute linear directly as (abs_error - 1.0).clamp(0.0, f64::MAX) (the IQN file already does this at iqn.rs:485), avoiding the sqrt round-trip.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at dqn.rs:313-314: quadratic = clamp(|u|,0,1)^2, linear = |u| - sqrt(quadratic) = max(|u|-1,0). The forward value is the correct Huber, but sqrt's backward is 0.5/sqrt(x), infinite at x=0; the -sqrt(quadratic) term gives grad -inf w.r.t. quadratic at |u|=0, and chaining through quadratic = |u|^2 yields -inf*0 = NaN. So td_error exactly 0 produces NaN gradients (rare in f32 but a real hazard). The IQN file already avoids this at iqn.rs:485 via (|u|-kappa).clamp(0, MAX). Fix (compute linear directly as (abs_error-1.0).clamp(0.0, f64::MAX), keeping quadratic for the 0.5*min(|u|,1)^2 term) yields an identical forward value, removes the sqrt round-trip, and is safe with no API change.

title: Continuous-action log_std is a fixed constant (std=1), never trained, not in VarMap
description: For continuous action spaces, log_std is created with `Tensor::zeros(...)` as a plain Tensor, NOT as a candle Var and NOT inserted into the VarMap. The optimizer is built from `self.var_map.all_vars()` (a2c.rs:390 / ppg.rs:724), which therefore excludes log_std. Consequences: (1) std = exp(0) = 1 is fixed for the entire run, so exploration noise never adapts; (2) log_std receives no gradient (a plain Tensor is a constant leaf), so the entropy bonus and policy-gradient terms cannot widen/narrow the policy; (3) it is not persisted by save() (which iterates var_map.data()). This silently cripples all continuous-control training for both A2C and PPG.
evidence: a2c.rs:157 `self.log_std = Some(Tensor::zeros(&[self.act_dim], DType::F32, &candle_device)?);` -- a constant Tensor, not `var_map.get(...)`/a `Var`. Optimizer at a2c.rs:390 `AdamW::new(self.var_map.all_vars(), params)` never sees log_std. evaluate_actions uses it (a2c.rs:336 `.broadcast_sub(log_std)`) but no gradient path/optimizer slot exists. SB3 registers log_std as a learnable parameter (`nn.Parameter`).
proposed_fix: Register log_std via the VarBuilder/VarMap (e.g. `vb.get_with_hints(&[act_dim], "policy.log_std", init)`) so it is a trainable Var included in all_vars() and saved/loaded. Initialize from a config field (NetworkConfig.log_std_init exists at config.rs:366 but is unused here).
bench_to_confirm: ppo/env bench on a continuous (Box) action env: assert std changes across updates; unit test asserting log_std appears in var_map.all_vars()
verify_reasoning: Verified at a2c.rs:157 and ppg.rs:427: log_std = Some(Tensor::zeros(...)) creates a plain Tensor, not a candle Var, and it is never inserted into the VarMap via VarBuilder/var_map.get. Optimizers are built from var_map.all_vars() (a2c.rs:390) / policy_var_map.all_vars() (ppg.rs:724,911), which therefore exclude log_std. Consequences all hold: std=exp(0)=1 fixed; a non-Var leaf accumulates no gradient and isn't in the optimizer slot; save() iterates var_map.data() so log_std isn't persisted. This cripples continuous control for both A2C and PPG. NetworkConfig.log_std_init (config.rs:366) exists but is unused. Fix (register log_std as a Var in the VarMap, init from config) is the standard SB3 behavior, doesn't break the public API, and load() tolerates missing keys, so it's safe.

title: Environment is hard-reset at the start of every rollout, destroying episode continuity
location: a2c.rs:442 inside collect_rollout (called per-iteration from train, a2c.rs:515-517); identical in ppg.rs:631
description: collect_rollout begins with `let mut obs = self.env.reset(&self.device)?;` and is invoked once per training iteration. On-policy algorithms must reset the VecEnv once at the start of training and then continue stepping across rollouts; here every rollout re-resets ALL envs to the initial-state distribution. With A2CConfig default n_steps=5 (config.rs:225), the agent is reset every 5 steps, so it only ever experiences timesteps 0-4 of an episode and can never learn behavior with a horizon longer than n_steps. VecEnv auto_reset=true (envs/vecenv.rs) handles within-rollout dones, but the start-of-rollout hard reset still discards every in-flight episode. PPG (n_steps=256) is less catastrophic but still biased toward episode starts and wastes compute.
evidence: a2c.rs:442 `let mut obs = self.env.reset(&self.device)?;` at the top of collect_rollout; train() loop a2c.rs:515 calls collect_rollout each iteration with a freshly-allocated RolloutBuffer (a2c.rs:438). No persistent `obs` is carried between iterations.
proposed_fix: Reset the env once (store obs as agent state) and have collect_rollout continue from the carried obs; only reset on first call. This matches SB3 OnPolicyAlgorithm.collect_rollouts which keeps self._last_obs across rollouts.
bench_to_confirm: env/ppo bench on CartPole-like task: A2C mean_reward should be able to exceed ~5; with current code it plateaus at the n_steps horizon
verify_reasoning: Verified a2c.rs:442 and ppg.rs:631 call self.env.reset() at the top of collect_rollout, which train() invokes once per iteration (a2c.rs:517, ppg.rs:1009) with a freshly-allocated buffer and no persistent obs carried across iterations. With A2C default n_steps=5 (config.rs:264) the agent only ever experiences timesteps 0-4 of any episode, breaking learning of horizons longer than n_steps. This deviates from the standard on-policy pattern (SB3 keeps self._last_obs across rollouts). The fix (reset once, carry obs as agent state) corrects a real bug, matches SB3, and leaves the public train() API unchanged.

title: A2C ignores max_grad_norm: no gradient clipping despite config field and comment
description: The comment claims 'Backward pass with gradient clipping' but the code only calls `optimizer.backward_step(&total_loss)`. A2CConfig.max_grad_norm (config.rs:245, default 0.5) is validated/stored but never applied. A2C policy-gradient updates are high-variance and rely on global-norm clipping for stability (SB3 A2C default max_grad_norm=0.5). PPO in this same crate does clip (ppo.rs:508-509 via clip_gradients), so A2C is inconsistent and unstable. PPG similarly never applies max_grad_norm.
evidence: a2c.rs:410 `// Backward pass with gradient clipping` followed by a2c.rs:411 `optimizer.backward_step(&total_loss)?;` -- no call to any clip routine. Contrast ppo.rs:508 `let vars = self.var_map.all_vars(); clip_gradients(&vars, &mut grads, self.config.max_grad_norm)?;`.
proposed_fix: Use the manual-gradient path (loss.backward() -> GradStore -> clip_gradients(..., config.max_grad_norm) -> optimizer.step_with_grads) as PPO does, instead of optimizer.backward_step, so max_grad_norm is honored.
bench_to_confirm: ppo bench harness already exercises clip_gradients; add A2C unit test asserting grad norm <= max_grad_norm after update
verify_reasoning: Verified a2c.rs:410-411: comment 'Backward pass with gradient clipping' followed by optimizer.backward_step(&total_loss) with no clip. grep confirms max_grad_norm is never referenced in a2c.rs (it lives in config.rs and defaults to 0.5). PPO does clip (ppo.rs:507-510 backward()->clip_gradients->step). PPG also never applies max_grad_norm (grep shows it only in PPGConfig def at ppg.rs:92,127, never in the update paths which use backward_step). The fix (manual grad path with clip_gradients like PPO) aligns code with the documented intent and config field; doesn't break API.

title: A fresh AdamW optimizer is constructed every update, discarding moment state (A2C reduces to ~sign-SGD)
description: AdamW is re-created inside update()/policy_phase_update()/auxiliary_phase_update() on every call, so its first/second moment buffers (m, v) and timestep t reset to zero each time. A2C performs exactly ONE backward_step per rollout (a2c.rs:393-411), so every gradient step is an Adam t=1 step: with bias correction, m_hat=g and v_hat=g^2, giving update approx lr*g/(sqrt(g^2)+eps) ~= lr*sign(g). This negates Adam's running-average behavior and makes the effective step magnitude ~= lr regardless of gradient scale. PPG resets policy and value optimizer state on every iteration as well, losing momentum across rollouts.
evidence: a2c.rs:390 `let mut optimizer = AdamW::new(self.var_map.all_vars(), params)?;` constructed inside update(); the only optimizer.backward_step is a2c.rs:411 -> exactly one Adam step per fresh optimizer. ppg.rs:724-725 and ppg.rs:911-912 likewise build new AdamW per phase invocation.
proposed_fix: Construct the optimizer(s) once at agent construction and persist them on the struct (as long-lived fields), or otherwise persist Adam moment state across updates.
bench_to_confirm: Micro-bench: compare A2C learning curve with persistent vs per-update optimizer on a fixed seed; persistent should converge faster/more stably
verify_reasoning: Verified a2c.rs:390 constructs AdamW inside update(), and the only backward_step is a2c.rs:411 (exactly one Adam step per fresh optimizer per rollout). Adam's first step with bias correction gives m_hat=g, v_hat=g^2, update=lr*g/(|g|+eps) ~ lr*sign(g); without bias correction it is still a sign-SGD-like scaled step. Either way moment averaging never accumulates across rollouts. PPG (ppg.rs:724-725, 911-912) rebuilds optimizers per phase invocation, so momentum is lost across iterations (it does accumulate across minibatches within one call, but the across-rollout claim holds). Contrast: PPO persists self.optimizer (ppo.rs:108,178,510). Fix (persist optimizer on the struct as PPO does) is safe and non-breaking.

title: Per-batch-element GPU->CPU sync inside categorical sampling loop
description: Inside the per-sample loop, log-probs are pulled to the host one row at a time: `log_probs.get(b)?.flatten_all()?.to_vec1()?`. This is an O(batch) sequence of device->host transfers per sampling call, executed every environment step during rollout collection. On Metal/CUDA each .to_vec1() forces a separate device sync/stall. The full `probs` tensor is already materialized once (a2c.rs:235), so the log-probs should be too.
evidence: a2c.rs:262 `let lp_vec: Vec<f32> = log_probs.get(b)?.flatten_all()?.to_vec1()?;` inside `for b in 0..batch_size` (a2c.rs:242). One host transfer per batch row per step.
proposed_fix: Materialize log-probs once before the loop: `let lp_all: Vec<f32> = log_probs.flatten_all()?.to_vec1()?;` then index `lp_all[b*num_actions + action]`. Eliminates O(batch) syncs.
bench_to_confirm: gpu_benchmark: rollout step throughput on Metal with large num_envs/batch before vs after
verify_reasoning: Verified a2c.rs:262 and ppg.rs:534 do log_probs.get(b)?.flatten_all()?.to_vec1() inside the per-sample loop, an O(batch) sequence of device->host transfers per sample_action call, which runs every step during rollout collection (hot path). On Metal/CUDA each to_vec1 forces a separate sync. The full probs tensor is already materialized once (a2c.rs:235), and the proposed fix (materialize log_probs once, index lp_all[b*num_actions+action]) is correct since log_probs is row-major [batch,num_actions]. Real and the fix is safe/equivalent. Downgraded to low: the sampler already performs host-side sampling with a full sync, the default device is CPU (where to_vec1 is cheap), and batch size equals num_envs, so the marginal benefit is modest though genuine on GPU.

title: PPG auxiliary phase deviates from the paper: no auxiliary value head, BC target snapshotted at wrong time
description: Two deviations from Cobbe et al. PPG: (1) Policy and value are fully separate networks with no auxiliary value head on the policy, so the auxiliary phase only re-fits the separate value net (ppg.rs:943-957) and applies a behavioral-cloning loss to the policy; the core PPG benefit (distilling value into shared policy features) does not occur. (2) The BC target `old_policy_output` is captured in store_aux_rollout at COLLECTION time (ppg.rs:878), and store_aux_rollout runs BEFORE policy_phase_update each iteration (train: ppg.rs:1012 then 1015). Across the default num_aux_rollouts=32 iterations the targets are 32 different pre-update policies. When the aux phase finally runs, BC (ppg.rs:948-961) drags the just-improved policy back toward these older policies, partially undoing policy-phase progress. The reference algorithm snapshots pi_old from the CURRENT network at the start of the aux phase and only uses BC to preserve it during value distillation.
evidence: ppg.rs:878 `let policy_output = self.policy_forward(&obs_flat)?.detach();` stored at collection time; train() ppg.rs:1012 `self.store_aux_rollout(&buffer)?;` precedes ppg.rs:1015 `self.policy_phase_update(&buffer)?;`. aux update at ppg.rs:948 `let current_policy_output = self.policy_forward(&batch_obs)?;` vs stored `batch_old_policy` (ppg.rs:949).
proposed_fix: Recompute the BC/KL target from the current policy at the start of auxiliary_phase_update (snapshot per stored-obs batch just before the aux epochs), and either add an auxiliary value head to the policy network or document that this is a simplified PPG variant.
bench_to_confirm: ppo/ppg bench: compare return with aux phase enabled vs disabled; if aux hurts, the misalignment is confirmed
verify_reasoning: Both claims verified. (1) policy_var_map and value_var_map are fully separate networks (ppg.rs:303-306, 390-423) with no shared trunk/auxiliary value head, so the aux phase (ppg.rs:943-957) only re-fits the separate value net plus a BC loss on the policy; the PPG value-distillation-into-shared-features benefit cannot occur. (2) old_policy_output is captured at collection time (ppg.rs:878) and store_aux_rollout (ppg.rs:1012) runs before policy_phase_update (ppg.rs:1015); over num_aux_rollouts=32 iterations these are 32 different pre-update policies, so the BC loss (ppg.rs:948-950) drags the just-improved policy toward older policies, partially undoing policy-phase progress. The reference snapshots pi_old from the current network at the start of the aux phase. Fix (recompute BC target from current policy at aux start; add aux value head or document as simplified) is correct and non-API-breaking.

title: Vec::remove(0) used as ring buffer in RewardStats and ProgressTracker (O(n) shift)
description: Both windowed buffers evict the oldest element with `Vec::remove(0)`, which shifts all remaining elements (O(n)). RewardStats window default is 100 and update() is called per episode reward; ProgressTracker max_history is 1000. This is exactly the ring-buffer anti-pattern: once the window is full, every insert is O(window).
evidence: metrics.rs:151 `self.recent.remove(0);` inside update once `self.recent.len() >= self.window_size`. metrics.rs:262 `self.history.remove(0);` once `self.history.len() >= self.max_history`.
proposed_fix: Use std::collections::VecDeque with push_back/pop_front (O(1)) for `recent` and `history`.
bench_to_confirm: micro-bench RewardStats::update in a tight loop with window=100 (VecDeque vs Vec)
verify_reasoning: Verified metrics.rs:151 (RewardStats::update, window default 100) and metrics.rs:262 (ProgressTracker::update, max_history 1000) evict the oldest element with Vec::remove(0), which is O(n) shift once full. The anti-pattern is real. It is not a per-step hot path (update is per-episode-reward / per-iteration), so impact is minor, matching the low tag. Fix to VecDeque is safe: the private fields are only used via iter()/len()/push/clear (windowed_mean, windowed_std, best_reward), none require Vec-specific indexing, so VecDeque<push_back/pop_front> is a drop-in.

title: PPG KL early-stopping only breaks the minibatch loop, not the epoch loop
description: When approx_kl exceeds target_kl, the code `break`s out of the inner `for batch_idx` loop only; the outer `for _epoch in 0..policy_epochs` loop (ppg.rs:738) then continues and resumes policy updates in the next epoch. Early stopping should terminate all remaining epochs. Harmless at the default policy_epochs=1, but incorrect for any policy_epochs>1.
evidence: ppg.rs:808-815 `if approx_kl > target_kl { ... break; }` is lexically inside `for batch_idx in 0..n_batches` (ppg.rs:742); the enclosing epoch loop is ppg.rs:738.
proposed_fix: Use a labeled break (e.g. `break 'policy_epochs;`) or a boolean flag checked by the outer loop to stop all epochs on KL breach.
bench_to_confirm: unit test with policy_epochs=4 and a tiny target_kl asserting only one epoch of updates ran
verify_reasoning: Verified the KL break at ppg.rs:808-815 is lexically inside 'for batch_idx in 0..n_batches' (ppg.rs:742), while the enclosing 'for _epoch in 0..policy_epochs' loop starts at ppg.rs:738. The break only exits the inner batch loop, so with policy_epochs>1 the next epoch resumes updates. Harmless at the default policy_epochs=1 (matching low severity). Fix (labeled break or boolean flag) matches reference early-stopping semantics, is safe, and doesn't affect default behavior.

title: Categorical inverse-CDF sampling biases toward action 0 on float underflow
description: The sampler initializes `action = 0` and selects the first index where cumulative prob exceeds r. Due to softmax rounding the cumulative sum can finish slightly below 1.0; if the drawn r lands in that residual gap no branch fires and the default action 0 is returned. This adds a small systematic bias toward action index 0. The robust convention defaults to the LAST index.
evidence: a2c.rs:250 `let mut action = 0usize;` then a2c.rs:251-257 loop with `if r < cumsum { action = i; break; }`; if `r >= sum(probs)` no assignment occurs and action stays 0.
proposed_fix: Initialize `action = num_actions - 1` (the last index) so leftover probability mass maps to the final action instead of action 0.
bench_to_confirm: statistical test: sample N draws from a near-uniform categorical and check empirical freq of action 0 is unbiased
verify_reasoning: Verified a2c.rs:250 (and ppg.rs:522) init action=0, then loop selects first i where r<cumsum and breaks. r=rng.gen() yields [0,1); float32 softmax sums can finish slightly below 1.0, so if r lands in the residual gap [sum,1) no branch fires and action 0 is returned, a systematic (though tiny, ~1e-6 probability) bias toward action 0. Real but negligible in practice, consistent with low. Fix (init action=num_actions-1, the standard last-index convention) only changes the rare underflow case and is safe.

title: A2C dead/misleading config and metrics: rms_prop_eps unused, RMSprop comment, explained_variance always 0
description: A2CConfig.rms_prop_eps (config.rs:253, default 1e-5) is never read anywhere; the update() comment (a2c.rs:384) says 'RMSprop is traditionally used for A2C' but the code uses AdamW, so the eps field is misleading dead config. TrainMetrics.explained_variance is hard-coded to 0.0 for A2C (a2c.rs:424) and PPG (ppg.rs:860) even though the value targets/predictions are available to compute it, so any downstream dashboard reports a constant 0 for value-function fit.
evidence: config.rs:253 `pub rms_prop_eps: f32,` with no reader (grep shows no use in a2c.rs); a2c.rs:424 `explained_variance: 0.0,`; ppg.rs:860 `explained_variance: 0.0,`.
proposed_fix: Either implement RMSprop/use rms_prop_eps or remove the field and the comment; compute explained_variance = 1 - Var(returns - values)/Var(returns) where values/returns are already materialized.
verify_reasoning: All three verified: rms_prop_eps is only defined (config.rs:253, 271) with no reader anywhere in src; a2c.rs:384 comment says RMSprop is traditional while the code uses AdamW (a2c.rs:390), making the eps field misleading dead config; explained_variance is hardcoded 0.0 for A2C (a2c.rs:424) and PPG (ppg.rs:860) even though values/returns are materialized. Cleanup-level, matches low. fix_is_safe=true with a caveat: computing explained_variance from the already-materialized tensors and fixing the comment / wiring rms_prop_eps are all non-breaking, and the finding offers these safe options. The alternative 'remove the field' option would change the serialized public A2CConfig API, but a safe non-removal path is available, so the proposed fix can be applied safely.

title: Categorical::log_prob detaches autograd (host round-trip) — silent zero-gradient for the public distribution API
description: The Distribution::log_prob impl for Categorical gathers the selected log-probs by pulling tensors to the host (`actions.to_vec1::<u32>()`, `self.log_probs.flatten_all()?.to_vec1::<f32>()`), looping in Rust, then rebuilding a fresh tensor via `Tensor::from_slice(&selected_log_probs, ...)`. In candle, `from_slice` creates a non-variable leaf with no parent op, so backward() through this result yields no gradient w.r.t. the logits. Any policy-gradient training that backprops through this public method (the module docstring at distributions/mod.rs:1-4 advertises it 'during policy optimization', and it is re-exported in lib.rs:78,238) would silently learn nothing for the discrete policy. SCOPE: the internal PPO/PPG/A2C update path does NOT use this struct — it reimplements the gather with gradient-preserving `gather` (ppo.rs:357-359, ppg.rs:585-586), so the library's own training is unaffected; the defect is in the public-facing distribution API. The host round-trip is also a GPU->CPU->GPU sync in what is meant to be a hot per-update call.
proposed_fix: Replace the host loop with a tensor gather that preserves the graph and stays on-device: `let idx = actions.to_dtype(DType::I64)?.unsqueeze(1)?; self.log_probs.gather(&idx, 1)?.squeeze(1)`. This both restores gradient flow and removes the GPU<->CPU sync.
bench_to_confirm: 5-line autograd unit test: build logits as a candle `Var`, construct Categorical, call log_prob on fixed actions, `.backward()`, assert a non-None/non-zero grad exists for the logits Var (currently fails; passes after the gather fix).
verify_reasoning: Confirmed in src/distributions/categorical.rs:206-216: log_prob does actions.to_vec1::<u32>() and self.log_probs.flatten_all()?.to_vec1::<f32>(), loops in Rust, and rebuilds via Tensor::from_slice(&selected_log_probs, &[batch_size], device). Both the to_vec1 host pull and from_slice sever the autograd graph — from_slice produces a non-Var leaf with op=None, so backward() yields no gradient w.r.t. logits. The values are correct but gradient is silently zero. Scope claim verified: PPO (ppo.rs:357-359) and PPG (ppg.rs:585-588) reimplement the gather inline with gradient-preserving log_probs.gather(actions_i64.unsqueeze(1),1).squeeze(1); grep shows the Categorical/DiagGaussian/SquashedGaussian structs are never used by any algorithm/example/bench — they are public-API-only re-exports (lib.rs:78,238). So the library's own training is unaffected, but the module doc (distributions/mod.rs) explicitly demonstrates categorical.log_prob during policy optimization, so an external user following the docs gets silently zero gradients — a severe, silent correctness trap. Proposed fix (to_dtype(I64) -> unsqueeze(1) -> gather(dim=1) -> squeeze(1)) returns the identical [batch_size] values, preserves the graph, keeps the API/shape, and removes the host sync — safe.

title: SquashedGaussian::log_prob uses inconsistent clamps between the atanh inversion and the tanh Jacobian, biasing log-prob for confident actions
description: When called through the Distribution trait, `log_prob(actions)` passes `unsquashed = None` (line 337), so the unsquashed sample u is reconstructed by atanh with the action clamped to [-0.999, 0.999] (line 302). atanh(0.999) ≈ 3.8, so any |action| > 0.999 has its recovered u saturated, shrinking the Gaussian term `log μ(u)`. Meanwhile the Jacobian term uses the UNCLAMPED action with only +1e-8 (line 320), so for the same boundary action the two terms no longer correspond to the same u: the correction is computed for an action whose implied u disagrees with the u used in the base log-prob. The root cause is that `Distribution::sample()` (lines 331-334) discards the unsquashed sample, forcing the lossy atanh round-trip on the standard path. The numerically stable identity is also not used.
proposed_fix: Thread the unsquashed sample through the trait (e.g. cache it from sample_with_unsquashed, or have sample() store it) so log_prob never inverts; when inversion is unavoidable, clamp the action and the Jacobian with the same bound (and prefer the stable identity log(1 - tanh^2 u) = 2*(ln2 - u - softplus(-2u))) so both terms reference the same u.
bench_to_confirm: Unit test: pick mean/log_std and a sampled action with |tanh(u)|>0.999; compare log_prob computed via sample_with_unsquashed (exact u) vs the None/atanh path — they diverge today; should agree after fix.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed genuine inconsistency in gaussian.rs:297-321. Via the trait, log_prob passes unsquashed=None (line 337), so u is reconstructed from clamp(-0.999,0.999) then atanh (lines 302-308); the base log_prob is evaluated at that clamped-derived u (313). The Jacobian, however, uses the UNCLAMPED action: actions.sqr() with only +1e-8 (317-320). For |a|>0.999 the two terms reference different u: tanh(u)=clamped_a so 1-tanh^2(u)=1-clamped_a^2, but the code uses 1-a^2. For non-boundary actions the code is correct (1-a^2 == 1-tanh^2(u)), so the bias is confined to confident/saturated actions (|a|>0.999), where it can reach a few tenths of a nat. Downgraded from medium to low: the library's SAC never uses this struct (algorithms reimplement squashing inline), and the correct lossless path exists via log_prob_with_correction(actions, Some(unsquashed)). Fix safety: the minimal fix (clamp the Jacobian to the same bound + use the stable identity 2*(ln2 - u - softplus(-2u))) is mathematically equivalent for in-range actions and only corrects boundary actions, so no correct result is broken and the API is unchanged. NOTE the finding's alternative of 'threading the unsquashed sample through the trait' would require changing Distribution::sample's signature and WOULD break the public trait API — only the non-API variant is safe.

title: DiagGaussian sampling generates noise on the host and transfers via from_slice every call (per-step host alloc + device copy)
description: Every sample() call allocates a `Vec<f32>` of size batch*action_dim on the host using `rand::thread_rng()` element-by-element, then materializes a tensor with `Tensor::from_slice(&samples, ..., device)`. Regardless of device this is a fresh host allocation plus a host->device copy on each call; on Metal/CUDA it is a host-side serial RNG followed by an upload inside what is typically a per-rollout-step hot path. It is also non-seedable, so sampling is not reproducible.
proposed_fix: Use `Tensor::randn(0f32, 1f32, &[batch, action_dim], device)` to generate the reparam noise as a device-native constant (still detached, so the reparam trick is preserved). Verify candle's device randn support on Metal before asserting it removes the host->device hop; otherwise at minimum hoist/preallocate the buffer.
bench_to_confirm: gpu_benchmark or a micro-bench timing sample() in a loop on Metal vs CPU, comparing the from_slice path against Tensor::randn.
verify_reasoning: Code pattern confirmed (gaussian.rs:155-160): rand::thread_rng() sampled element-by-element into a Vec<f32> of size batch*action_dim, then Tensor::from_slice uploads it; non-seedable. However the 'per-rollout-step hot path' premise is NOT confirmed in this repo — grep shows DiagGaussian::sample() has no internal caller (every algorithm reimplements continuous sampling inline), so within the codebase this is effectively dead code, not a hot path. The verdict rests on two genuine but limited concerns: (1) non-seedability is a real reproducibility defect for a public sampling API regardless of call frequency; (2) the host->device copy only matters for external GPU users — on the default CPU build candle's Tensor::randn also generates on host, so the fix removes no copy there. Hence downgraded medium->low.
