Fix (Tensor::randn(0,1,...)) is behavior-preserving (still detached N(0,1) noise; reparam trick intact via std*noise+mean) and safe on CPU/CUDA, but candle 0.8 Metal randn support is unverified — a naive swap could panic under the metal feature, so safety is true-with-caveat and the finding's preallocate fallback is the conservative path.

title: Categorical sampling generates uniforms on the host and transfers via from_slice every call
description: Same host-RNG + upload pattern as the Gaussian: each sample() builds a `Vec<f32>` of batch*num_actions uniforms with `rand::thread_rng()`, uploads via `Tensor::from_slice`, then computes Gumbel noise. This is a host allocation plus a host->device copy per call in the action-selection hot path, and is non-seedable (no reproducibility).
proposed_fix: Generate the uniform tensor on-device (e.g. `Tensor::rand(0f32, 1f32, &[batch, num_actions], device)`) before computing -log(-log(u)); keep the eps clamp. Confirm candle device-rand support before claiming the host->device hop is removed.
bench_to_confirm: gpu_benchmark / micro-bench of sample_gumbel_max in a loop on Metal vs CPU.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed (categorical.rs:128-133): rng.gen::<f32>() per element into a Vec of size batch*num_actions, Tensor::from_slice upload, then Gumbel -log(-log(u)); non-seedable. Same scope caveat as the Gaussian finding: sample_gumbel_max/Categorical::sample has no internal caller (algorithms reimplement Gumbel-max/argmax inline), so the 'action-selection hot path' is not confirmed within the repo. Real concerns are the non-seedable reproducibility gap (valid for a public sampler) and a GPU-only host->device upload for external users; on the default CPU build Tensor::rand also generates on host, so no copy is saved there. Downgraded medium->low accordingly. Fix (Tensor::rand(0,1,...) keeping the eps clamp) is distribution-preserving and safe on CPU/CUDA, but candle 0.8 Metal rand support is unverified so safety is conditional under the metal feature; preallocation is the safe fallback.

title: SquashedGaussian::entropy returns the unsquashed Gaussian entropy (overestimate; grows unbounded with std)
description: entropy() returns the base DiagGaussian entropy, which is the differential entropy of the UNsquashed Gaussian and is unbounded as log_std grows, whereas the true tanh-squashed action distribution is supported on (-1,1) with bounded entropy. The discrepancy is documented as an approximation, and SAC-style consumers typically use -log_prob (Monte-Carlo) rather than this entropy(), so practical impact is low — but a caller using this as an entropy bonus for a squashed policy gets a systematically wrong (overestimated) value.
evidence: gaussian.rs:341-343 comment: 'Entropy of squashed Gaussian is complex; use base entropy as approximation' then `self.base_dist.entropy()`.
proposed_fix: Either compute a Monte-Carlo entropy estimate as the mean of -log_prob over fresh samples, or document the limitation prominently at the trait call site and steer SAC callers to -log_prob; at minimum subtract the expected log-det-Jacobian E[sum log(1 - tanh^2 u)] to bound it.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed (gaussian.rs:340-343): entropy() returns self.base_dist.entropy(), the differential entropy of the UNsquashed Gaussian, with an in-code comment acknowledging it as an approximation. The substantive claim holds: this systematically overestimates the true tanh-squashed entropy, which has support (-1,1) and bounded entropy <= log(2)~0.69 nats, whereas base entropy at std~1 is ~1.42 nats and ignores the saturated-mean collapse. One wording imprecision in the finding: 'unbounded as std grows' is loose — log_std is clamped to [-20,2] (constants verified), so the base entropy is actually bounded above, just badly biased; the overestimation conclusion is unaffected. Impact is low: the library's SAC never uses this struct and SAC consumers conventionally use -log_prob (Monte-Carlo) rather than this entropy(). Fix safety: documenting the limitation and/or subtracting E[sum log(1-tanh^2 u)] does not break the API and improves correctness; the MC-estimate variant would make entropy() non-deterministic (an RNG side effect) but still preserves the signature — true-with-caveat.

title: PER ReplayBuffer::add rescans all priorities every insert (O(n) per add, O(n^2) to fill)
description: On every prioritized add(), the buffer linearly scans priorities[..self.size] to recompute the max priority assigned to the new transition. This makes each add O(size); filling a capacity-N prioritized buffer is O(N^2). For the default capacity of 1_000_000 this is catastrophic during the warmup/fill phase. Standard PER (e.g. SB3, OpenAI baselines) tracks the running max priority as a scalar instead of rescanning.
proposed_fix: Maintain a `max_priority: f32` field updated in O(1) on add() and update_priorities() (monotone running max, as in SB3), or lazily recompute only when the current max leaf is lowered. Assign that scalar to new transitions instead of scanning priorities[..size].
bench_to_confirm: New micro-bench: add 1M transitions to a prioritized ReplayBuffer and time the fill; extend the existing buffer bench (cargo bench) with a prioritized variant.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at replay.rs:213-223. Every prioritized add() does `priorities[..self.size.max(1)].iter().fold(1.0, f32::max)`, an O(size) scan. Once the buffer is full size==capacity, so each add is O(capacity); during the fill phase size grows 0..N giving O(N^2) total. For the default capacity 1_000_000 this is genuinely catastrophic during warmup, and add() is the per-step collection hot path. Standard PER (SB3 PrioritizedReplayBuffer._max_priority) tracks a monotone running scalar instead. fix_is_safe=true: maintaining a monotone max_priority field matches SB3's reference heuristic. Note this is not a pure no-op: after update_priorities lowers values, a monotone max assigns a (stale, higher) priority to new transitions vs the current exact rescan. But 'new transitions get max priority' has no canonical value, so correctness is unaffected; the lazy-recompute alternative would be exactly equivalent if strict equivalence is desired.

title: PER min-priority computed by O(n) leaf scan on every prioritized sample
location: SumTree::min_priority lines 472-479, invoked from sample_prioritized line 291
description: sample_prioritized() computes the max importance-sampling weight via tree.min_priority(), which linearly scans all `capacity` leaves (self.tree[self.capacity-1..]) filtering and folding to find the minimum. This is O(capacity) per sample() call (e.g. ~1M leaf reads per minibatch for a 1M buffer), defeating the whole point of the segment tree. The codebase already ships a proper O(log n) MinTree in segment_tree.rs that is left unused here.
proposed_fix: Maintain a MinTree (segment_tree::MinTree) of the same alpha-powered priorities updated in update()/add(); read the global minimum in O(1) from its root instead of scanning leaves.
bench_to_confirm: Micro-bench: repeatedly sample(256) from a prioritized ReplayBuffer at capacity 1e6 and compare against the MinTree-backed version.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: SumTree::min_priority (replay.rs:472-479) scans `self.tree[self.capacity-1..]`, the full leaf array of size capacity, and is invoked once per sample_prioritized() at line 291 to compute max IS weight. This is O(capacity) per minibatch (per sample() call, not per transition as the finding correctly states), e.g. ~1M float reads per train step for a 1M buffer, while the actual tree sampling is only batch_size*log(n). It scales with capacity and defeats the segment tree on the per-train-step sampling hot path. The leaves hold alpha-powered priorities (tree.update is fed max_priority.powf(alpha)), so a MinTree maintained with the same alpha-powered values gives an identical min in O(1) at the root. Filtering p>0 maps to MinTree's f32::MAX-initialized unfilled leaves. fix_is_safe=true. Severity high is defensible (capacity-scaling on hot path); medium would also be defensible since it is per-minibatch, but I keep the finding's high.

title: MmapReplayBuffer LRU cache is O(cache_size) per access, turning the cache into a slowdown
location: get_transition lines 381-405 (and cache invalidation in add lines 297-300)
description: The LRU is implemented over a Vec<usize> with `lru_order.retain(|&x| x != idx)` (O(cache_size)) and `lru_order.insert(0, idx)` (O(cache_size) memmove of the whole vector) on every transition touch. With the default cache_size=100_000 (doc example 1_000_000), each of the batch_size transitions fetched in get_batch costs O(cache_size), so sampling a 256 batch is ~256 * O(1e5) element shifts plus per-hit retain scans. add() also pays O(cache_size) via retain whenever the overwritten position was cached. This makes the 'cache' far slower than reading directly from the mmap.
proposed_fix: Use an O(1) LRU: the `lru` crate, or HashMap<idx, node> with an intrusive doubly linked list, or at minimum a VecDeque with recency stamps so eviction does not require retain/insert(0). Avoid insert(0)/retain on a Vec entirely.
bench_to_confirm: Micro-bench: fill MmapReplayBuffer (cache_size=1e5) and time repeated sample(256); compare Vec-LRU vs O(1)-LRU.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at mmap.rs:381-405. The hit path does `lru_order.retain(|&x| x != idx)` (O(cache_size)) plus `lru_order.insert(0, idx)` (O(cache_size) full-Vec memmove); the miss path also does insert(0). get_transition is called once per sampled transition in get_batch, so sampling a batch of 256 costs 256 * O(cache_size). With default cache_size=100_000 (doc example 1_000_000), insert(0) memmoves ~800KB of usize per call, ~200MB of shuffling per minibatch, making the cache far slower than direct mmap reads. add() at 297-300 also pays O(cache_size) retain when the overwritten slot was cached. This is on the sampling hot path for this buffer. fix_is_safe=true: an O(1) LRU (lru crate or intrusive list) preserves LRU semantics and is an internal-only change, no public API impact. Specialized buffer, but severe when used.

title: Dead/duplicated segment-tree code: replay.rs reimplements SumTree and ignores MinTree
location: segment_tree.rs whole module; replay.rs reimplements SumTree at lines 414-480; mod.rs:33 re-export
description: buffer/mod.rs publicly re-exports segment_tree::{MinTree, SumTree}, but grep shows neither type is referenced anywhere outside segment_tree.rs's own tests. replay.rs defines its own private `struct SumTree` (lines 414-480) with an O(n) min_priority scan instead of using the library's O(log n) MinTree. This is duplicated, divergent code and the well-tested MinTree that would fix the min-priority hot path is dead. Consolidating onto segment_tree's SumTree+MinTree directly resolves the min-priority perf finding.
evidence:
mod.rs: `pub use segment_tree::{MinTree, SumTree};`  // exported
replay.rs:146 `sum_tree: Option<SumTree>,` resolves to the LOCAL `struct SumTree` at replay.rs:414, not segment_tree::SumTree.
grep: SumTree/MinTree have zero non-test, non-definition uses outside replay's private copy.
proposed_fix: Delete the private SumTree in replay.rs and back the buffer with segment_tree::SumTree + segment_tree::MinTree (or, if the module is intended to stay internal, remove the unused MinTree/SumTree pub re-export). Either way, eliminate the duplicate.
verify_reasoning: Verified by grep: segment_tree::SumTree/MinTree appear only in mod.rs (the `pub use segment_tree::{MinTree, SumTree}` re-export at line 33 and doc comments) and within segment_tree.rs itself; replay.rs has no `use segment_tree` import, so its `sum_tree: Option<SumTree>` (line 146) resolves to the private `struct SumTree` defined at replay.rs:414-480. MinTree has zero non-test, non-definition uses. So the duplication and the dead well-tested MinTree are real. This is pure cleanup with no functional bug, so I adjust medium->low. fix_is_safe=true judged on the PRIMARY consolidation path (delete the private dup, back the buffer with segment_tree::SumTree+MinTree, renaming the call site get->sample), which is internal and API-safe. Caveat: the finding's alternative 'remove the unused pub re-export' WOULD be a breaking public-API change, so only the consolidation option is safe.

title: NStepReplayBuffer.last_obs is written (with a heap alloc) every add but never read
location: field decl line 128; write line 206; cleared line 393 (no reads, grep-confirmed)
description: Every call to add() executes `self.last_obs = Some(next_obs.to_vec())`, allocating obs_dim f32 on the heap per step, but last_obs is only ever assigned and cleared -- it is never read anywhere in the crate. This is a dead field plus a wasted per-step allocation in the hot collection path.
proposed_fix: Remove the last_obs field and the per-step to_vec() write.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed by grep in nstep.rs: last_obs appears only at decl (128), init (175), write (206: `self.last_obs = Some(next_obs.to_vec())`), and clear (393); there are no reads. It is a private field, so no external reads are possible either. store_nstep_transition uses its `final_next_obs` parameter and flush_episode_end uses `terminal_obs`, neither touches last_obs. So every add() heap-allocates obs_dim f32 that is never used, in the per-step collection path. fix_is_safe=true: removing a dead private field and its write changes no observable behavior and no public API.

title: Mmap add/read serialize f32 one element at a time instead of bulk-copying
description: Writing/reading a transition loops over each f32 doing a 4-byte to_le_bytes()/from_le_bytes() copy. For large obs (the doc example is 84*84*4 = ~28k floats) this is ~28k tiny copy_from_slice/try_into calls per transition instead of one bulk memcpy.
proposed_fix: On little-endian targets, bulk-copy with a single copy_from_slice using bytemuck::cast_slice (no unsafe at the call site), or gate a fast path; keep the byte loop only as a portable fallback for big-endian.
bench_to_confirm: Micro-bench add()/read of large-obs (e.g. 28k-dim) transitions, bulk-copy vs element loop.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: add (mmap.rs:271-294) loops per-f32 with val.to_le_bytes()+copy_from_slice; read_transition (341-369) loops per-f32 with try_into()+f32::from_le_bytes. For large obs (doc example 84*84*4 ~= 28k floats) this is ~28k tiny bounds-checked copies per transition instead of one bulk memcpy, on the add (per-step) and read (per-sample) hot paths. fix_is_safe=true on little-endian: bytemuck::cast_slice(&obs[..obs_dim]) into the mmap byte range yields byte-identical output to the current loop on LE (u8 alignment is trivially satisfied), and the finding correctly gates LE / keeps the byte loop as the big-endian fallback. Caveat: bytemuck is not currently a dependency (confirmed via Cargo.toml grep), so the fix adds a dep; its cast_slice is a safe API, compatible with the crate's unsafe policy. Low severity, niche buffer.

title: ReplayBuffer::clear does not reset current_beta (PER IS annealing persists across reuse)
description: clear() rebuilds the sum tree and resets priorities but leaves current_beta at its annealed value. If a prioritized buffer is cleared and reused for a fresh run, importance-sampling beta does not restart from config.beta, so IS correction starts already (partially) annealed toward 1.0. Minor but affects reproducibility/correctness of restarted PER training.
proposed_fix: Add `self.current_beta = self.config.beta;` in clear().
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at replay.rs:393-403: clear() resets position, size, rebuilds the sum tree, and refills priorities to 1.0, but does not touch current_beta. Since current_beta is annealed toward 1.0 each sample_prioritized() (line 303), a cleared-and-reused prioritized buffer resumes IS correction from the annealed beta rather than config.beta. This is an inconsistency given clear() does reset the other PER state, and affects reproducibility of restarted PER training. Minor in magnitude (annealing rate 1e-6/sample), but objectively present. fix_is_safe=true: adding `self.current_beta = self.config.beta;` restores the documented reset semantics with no API change and no effect on non-prioritized buffers.

title: `ortho_init` config flag is silently ignored — networks never get orthogonal init (claims vs reality)
description: ActorCriticConfig exposes `ortho_init: bool` defaulting to `true`, and the init.rs module advertises orthogonal init as 'the recommended initialization for RL policy networks'. But `ortho_init` is never read in ActorCritic::new (nor anywhere else in the crate), and every layer is built via `candle_nn::linear(...)`, which hard-codes `DEFAULT_KAIMING_NORMAL` for weights (verified in candle-nn 0.8.4 linear.rs: `let init_ws = crate::init::DEFAULT_KAIMING_NORMAL`). So actor/critic/backbone/RNN weights are always Kaiming-normal regardless of the flag. The entire init.rs orthogonal/xavier API (`orthogonal_init`, `InitMethod`, etc.) is re-exported in networks/mod.rs but used by no production code path (grep finds zero call sites outside init.rs and its tests). This breaks PPO/policy-gradient reproducibility expectations and the SB3-parity narrative. NOTE: this finding is coupled with the orthogonal_init reshape bug below — the obvious fix (wiring orthogonal init into the Linear weights) will trip that bug.
evidence: actor_critic.rs:154 `ortho_init: true,` set but never referenced; layers built with `candle_nn::linear(...)` (e.g. mlp.rs:138,144 and rnn.rs:108-109). candle-nn linear.rs:61 `let init_ws = crate::init::DEFAULT_KAIMING_NORMAL;`. grep for `ortho_init|orthogonal_init|InitMethod::Orthogonal` outside init.rs returns only the dead config fields.
proposed_fix: Either remove the dead `ortho_init` field (and the unused init.rs API) to stop over-promising, or actually apply it: after constructing each Linear, replace its weight Var via VarMap with `orthogonal_init(weight, gain)` using activation-appropriate gains (sqrt(2) for ReLU backbones, 1.0 for value head, 0.01 for the policy output head). If you wire it in, fix the rows<cols bug below first.
bench_to_confirm: ppo benchmark / a learning-curve smoke test before vs after enabling true orthogonal init
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: grep for `.ortho_init` reads returns nothing — the field (actor_critic.rs:138, set true at 154/170; and a duplicate dead field in algorithms/config.rs:363) is never consumed in ActorCritic::new or anywhere. All layers go through candle_nn::linear, and candle-nn 0.8.4 linear.rs:61 hardcodes DEFAULT_KAIMING_NORMAL for weights, so weights are always Kaiming-normal regardless of the flag. The init.rs orthogonal API is only re-exported (mod.rs:70) with zero production call sites. So the flag is genuinely dead and contradicts the docs (orthogonal advertised as 'recommended for RL'). Downgraded high->medium: networks still train with a valid (Kaiming) init; this is a silently-ignored config / missing-feature and SB3-parity gap, not wrong math or a crash. fix_is_safe=false: removing the `pub ortho_init` field breaks the public API/serde shape, and actually wiring orthogonal init changes weight values (changes training results) AND would route the dimension-reducing actor/critic heads through the orthogonal_init reshape bug in finding #2.

title: orthogonal_init errors for every dimension-reducing weight (out_features < in_features) — wrong narrow + reshape
description: In the `rows < cols` branch the code does `q.t()?.narrow(1, 0, rows)?.contiguous()?` then `q_scaled.reshape(shape)`. Trace for a typical layer weight `[64, 256]` (out=64, in=256): random→`[256,64]`; gram_schmidt→`[256,64]` (64 orthonormal columns); `q.t()`→`[64,256]` (already the correct semi-orthogonal matrix with orthonormal rows); `narrow(1,0,64)`→`[64,64]`; then `reshape([64,256])` is 4096 vs 16384 elements → candle reshape errors on numel mismatch. The `>=` at line 110/121 means square matrices take the other branch, so this else-branch is reached iff rows<cols strictly and then ALWAYS errors. This is the common case (any layer that reduces width, including ActorCritic's actor_head backbone_out→action_dim and critic_head backbone_out→1). It is currently latent only because finding #1 means orthogonal_init is never invoked during network construction; the existing tests only cover rows>=cols ([32,32],[64,32],[256,128],[512,256]).
evidence: init.rs:122-125 `let q = if rows >= cols { q.narrow(1,0,cols)?.contiguous()? } else { q.t()?.narrow(1,0,rows)?.contiguous()? };` followed by init.rs:130 `q_scaled.reshape(shape)`. For [64,256]: narrowed q is [64,64] (4096 elems) but shape demands [64,256] (16384 elems).
proposed_fix: In the else branch drop the narrow: `q.t()?.contiguous()?` is already shape [rows, cols] with orthonormal rows. Add a unit test asserting `InitMethod::Orthogonal.init(&[4, 256], DType::F32, &dev, 1.0)` succeeds with dims [4,256] and W·Wᵀ ≈ I_4.
bench_to_confirm: new micro-test: orthogonal_init on a wide [out<in] shape (must currently fail, should pass after fix)
verify_reasoning: Trace verified for [64,256] (rows=64<cols=256): random=[256,64] -> gram_schmidt q=[256,64] -> q.t()=[64,256] -> narrow(1,0,rows=64)=[64,64] (4096 elems) -> reshape([64,256]) demands 16384 elems. Confirmed candle-core 0.8.4 tensor.rs:2202 returns Error::ShapeMismatchBinaryOp when elem_count differs, so the else-branch always hard-errors for strictly rows<cols. Existing tests only cover rows>=cols ([32,32],[64,32],[256,128],[512,256]) so it never triggers in tests, and finding #1 means it's never invoked internally. Downgraded high->medium: real hard error in a public, documented init utility, but currently unreachable in production. fix_is_safe=true: dropping the narrow (q.t()?.contiguous()? is already [rows,cols] with orthonormal rows) yields the correct semi-orthogonal shape and W·Wᵀ=I; it doesn't affect existing rows>=cols callers (different branch).

title: Separate-backbone + recurrent silently drops recurrence while is_recurrent() reports true
location: extract_features separate branch (lines 537-551); recurrent built unconditionally (lines 269-289); is_recurrent (lines 555-557)
description: The recurrent layer (LSTM/GRU) is constructed regardless of `shared_backbone` (lines 269-289), allocating real parameters. But in the separate-backbone path of extract_features (lines 538-551) the recurrent layer is never invoked and the function returns `RecurrentState::None`. Meanwhile `is_recurrent()` returns true (it only checks that the RecurrentBackbone variant isn't None). So a user who configures `.with_separate_networks().with_lstm(...)` gets a network that allocates LSTM weights, reports itself recurrent, never uses them, and silently behaves as a feedforward net — dead params plus a correctness gotcha for any caller that drives recurrent state.
evidence: actor_critic.rs:548 comment `// Note: For separate backbones, we don't use recurrent layers` then line 550 returns `RecurrentState::None`; recurrent backbone still built at lines 269-289; is_recurrent (line 556) `!matches!(self.recurrent, RecurrentBackbone::None)`.
proposed_fix: Either reject the combination at construction (error if `!shared_backbone && recurrent_type != None`), or skip building the recurrent layer when separate and make is_recurrent() reflect actual usage. Best: support per-head recurrent states explicitly if the combo is intended.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: the recurrent backbone is built from config.recurrent_type alone (actor_critic.rs:269-289), independent of shared_backbone, so .with_separate_networks().with_lstm(..) allocates real LSTM/GRU params. The separate path (538-551) never invokes the recurrent layer and returns RecurrentState::None (comment at 548). Meanwhile is_recurrent() (556) is `!matches!(self.recurrent, RecurrentBackbone::None)`, returning true. So the net allocates dead recurrent params, reports recurrent=true, yet behaves feedforward — a genuine correctness gotcha for any loop that drives recurrent state. fix_is_safe=true for the recommended non-rejecting variant (skip building recurrent when separate and make is_recurrent reflect actual usage), which only removes dead params and corrects a misleading bool without changing the type signature; the alternative 'reject at construction' variant is more invasive but breaks no existing tests.

title: RNN init_state hard-codes DType::F32 — breaks under half-precision models
description: init_state always allocates hidden/cell tensors as F32 (`DType::F32`). If the model parameters are F16/BF16 (the `half` feature), `weight_hh.forward(h)` inside the cell will receive an F32 hidden state against F16 weights, which candle's matmul rejects on dtype mismatch. The dtype should be derived from the module's parameter dtype rather than hard-coded. This only bites when half precision is actually exercised, which I did not confirm is wired up end-to-end.
evidence: rnn.rs:196 `DType::F32,` passed to LSTMState::zeros; rnn.rs:419 same for GRU. Cell forward at rnn.rs:124 `self.weight_hh.forward(h)` requires h dtype == weight dtype.
proposed_fix: Thread the parameter dtype (e.g. from `weight_ih.weight().dtype()`) into init_state instead of hard-coding F32, or take dtype as an argument from the caller.
bench_to_confirm: gpu/half micro-bench: LSTM forward_step with a BF16 VarBuilder
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: LSTM::init_state (rnn.rs:196) and GRU::init_state (rnn.rs:419) pass DType::F32 to *State::zeros, ignoring the module's parameter dtype. If the VarBuilder dtype is F16/BF16 (half feature), the F32 hidden/cell state fed to weight_hh.forward (rnn.rs:124/345) mismatches F16 weights and candle's matmul/binary ops reject the dtype mismatch. Real latent robustness bug; correctly hedged as only biting when half precision is actually exercised end-to-end (default F32 paths are unaffected). fix_is_safe=true: deriving dtype from weight_ih.weight().dtype() keeps F32 behavior identical for default models and only fixes the F16/BF16 case; can be done internally without changing the public init_state signature.

title: LSTM/GRU forward_step: redundant unsqueeze(0)→squeeze(0) round-trip per layer in single-step inference path
description: Each layer pushes `h_new.unsqueeze(0)` then immediately recovers the next layer's input via `h_states.last().unwrap().squeeze(0)`. The unsqueeze/squeeze pair is pure overhead — `layer_input` could just be `h_new` directly, with the unsqueeze deferred to the final `Tensor::cat`. forward_step is the per-environment single-step path used by ActorCritic inference (called every env step), so trimming view churn here is on the hot path (though each op is cheap; impact is minor).
evidence: rnn.rs:223-227 `h_states.push(h_new.unsqueeze(0)?); ... layer_input = h_states.last().unwrap().squeeze(0)?;`. Same pattern rnn.rs:439-440. forward_step is invoked from actor_critic.rs:517/526/531.
proposed_fix: Keep `layer_input = h_new.clone()` (Arc clone) for the next layer and store the raw cell outputs; build the stacked state once with a single `Tensor::stack(&outs, 0)` (or cat of unsqueezed) at the end.
bench_to_confirm: env/ppo benchmark with a recurrent ActorCritic (per-step LSTM inference)
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at rnn.rs:223-227 (LSTM) and 439-440 (GRU): h_states.push(h_new.unsqueeze(0)?) then layer_input = h_states.last().unwrap().squeeze(0)?. The squeeze(0)-of-unsqueeze(0) recovers a view equal to h_new; layer_input could just be h_new. forward_step is on the per-env single-step inference path (called from actor_critic.rs:517/526/531). Genuinely redundant, but unsqueeze/squeeze are zero-copy views, so the cost is a couple of cheap Tensor-metadata allocations per layer (default 1-layer) — truly negligible, as the finding itself states. fix_is_safe=true: using h_new directly and building the stacked state once via stack/cat at the end produces identical values.

title: Activation::apply allocates a fresh zeros_like every forward for SELU/LeakyReLU
description: SELU and LeakyReLU each call `Tensor::zeros_like(x)?` on every forward pass to implement the `min(0, ·)` clamp, allocating a full-size zero tensor per activation per layer per batch. For LeakyReLU this is avoidable: `x` already has `relu()`; the negative part is `(x - x.relu())` (no zeros tensor) or `x.minimum` against a cached scalar-broadcast. SELU similarly. Minor, but it is per-forward allocation in the network's hot path when these activations are selected.
evidence: mlp.rs:54 `let negative = x.minimum(&Tensor::zeros_like(x)?)? * 0.01;`; mlp.rs:48 SELU `.minimum(&Tensor::zeros_like(x)?)?`.
proposed_fix: Compute the negative branch without a zeros allocation, e.g. LeakyReLU: `(x.relu()? + (x - x.relu()?)?.affine(0.01, 0.0)?)?` or use a clamp helper; avoid allocating zeros_like each call.
bench_to_confirm: ppo benchmark with SELU/LeakyReLU MLP backbone
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: mlp.rs:48 (SELU) and mlp.rs:54 (LeakyReLU) call Tensor::zeros_like(x)? each forward to implement the min(0,·) clamp, which is a real full-size buffer allocation (not just a view). Activation::apply is on the per-layer forward hot path, but only when SELU/LeakyReLU are explicitly selected (defaults are ReLU for MLP, Tanh for ActorCritic), so it's an opt-in minor allocation. fix_is_safe=true: LeakyReLU negative = (x - x.relu())*0.01 is mathematically identical; SELU's min(0,alpha*(exp(x)-1)) can be computed as -relu(-(alpha*(exp(x)-1))), preserving the exact result without allocating zeros.

title: Dead no-op branches: actor_input_dim / critic_input_dim if/else both return backbone_out_dim
description: Both arms of these if/else expressions evaluate to `backbone_out_dim`, so the conditional is pointless and misleads readers into thinking the head input dim depends on whether head_dims is empty (it doesn't). Pure cleanup.
evidence: actor_critic.rs:323-327 `let actor_input_dim = if !config.actor_head_dims.is_empty() { backbone_out_dim } else { backbone_out_dim };` (identical critic version at 342-346).
proposed_fix: Replace with `let actor_input_dim = backbone_out_dim;` and likewise for critic.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed verbatim: actor_critic.rs:323-327 and 342-346 both have `if !config.{actor,critic}_head_dims.is_empty() { backbone_out_dim } else { backbone_out_dim }` — identical arms, a pure no-op that misleads readers. fix_is_safe=true: replacing with `let actor_input_dim = backbone_out_dim;` (and likewise critic) is exactly equivalent. Pure cleanup, no behavior change.

title: SelfAttention rebuilds the causal mask (host alloc + H2D copy + dtype cast) on every forward
description: When config.causal is set, every forward pass calls create_causal_mask(seq_len, device, dtype). That function allocates a Vec<f32> of size seq_len*seq_len on the host, fills it with an O(seq^2) double loop, copies it to the device via Tensor::from_slice, then runs a to_dtype conversion. On Metal/CUDA this is a host->device transfer plus an extra kernel launch inside the hot inference/training path, repeated for every layer/step even though the mask is a fixed function of seq_len. TransformerEncoder (transformer.rs) already precomputes its causal mask once in the constructor (line 484-488), so this is an avoidable per-forward cost specific to SelfAttention.
proposed_fix: Precompute the causal mask once in SelfAttention::new (sized to a max_seq_len, like TransformerEncoder does) and narrow it to seq_len in forward, or cache it keyed by (seq_len, dtype). This removes the per-forward host allocation and H2D copy.
bench_to_confirm: A micro-bench timing SelfAttention::forward with causal=true vs a cached-mask variant over many iterations (e.g. seq_len=128) on Metal would confirm the H2D/alloc overhead.
verify_reasoning: Verified in src/networks/attention.rs. forward() lines 186-189 call create_causal_mask(seq_len, x.device(), x.dtype()) whenever config.causal is set, and create_causal_mask (238-249) allocates a host Vec<f32> of seq_len*seq_len, fills it with an O(seq^2) double loop, copies to device via Tensor::from_slice, then to_dtype. This is per-forward host alloc + H2D + extra kernel on the inference/training path, and TransformerEncoder (transformer.rs 484-488) precomputes its causal mask once in the constructor, so the inconsistency and avoidability are real. Downgraded high->medium: causal SelfAttention is opt-in (grep shows no internal algorithm sets with_causal; AttentionActorCritic builds it without causal), and the cost is dominated by the QK^T/softmax/AV matmuls, so absolute impact is modest. fix_is_safe=true but note the proposed fix is slightly misdescribed: SelfAttentionConfig (28-41) has no max_seq_len field unlike TransformerConfig, so the fix needs either a new config field or an internal seq_len-keyed cache; that cache must use Mutex/precompute (not RefCell) to preserve Send+Sync required by Rayon VecEnv. Either form yields the identical mask, so correctness/API are preserved.

title: BatchNorm running_mean/running_var are never updated, so eval mode does not normalize (train/eval mismatch)
location: BatchNorm forward_train lines 322-323; forward_eval lines 339-364; stats init lines 230-242
description: running_mean and running_var are initialized to zeros and ones respectively and are read in forward_eval, but they are never written anywhere (confirmed by grep: only created at 230-242 and read at 342, no assignment). forward_train computes batch statistics and the code comment explicitly admits the momentum update is skipped ('Update running statistics (simplified...)'). Consequently, in eval mode the layer subtracts mean=0 and divides by sqrt(1+eps)~=1, i.e. it leaves the input essentially un-normalized and only applies the affine transform. A network trained with this BatchNorm (which normalizes by live batch stats during training) will see a completely different input distribution at eval time, producing wrong outputs. This is a genuine correctness bug, not just a perf note.
proposed_fix: Either store running stats behind interior mutability (e.g. Mutex<Tensor> / RefCell or candle Var) and apply the momentum update in forward_train (running = (1-m)*running + m*batch_stat), or document BatchNorm as train-only and make eval reuse batch statistics. Until then, prefer LayerNorm/RMSNorm (which are correct) and warn users away from BatchNorm.eval().
bench_to_confirm: A unit test: train-mode forward on data with nonzero mean, then eval-mode forward on the same data; outputs differ drastically because running stats stayed at (0,1).
verify_reasoning: Confirmed in src/networks/normalization.rs and via grep: running_mean/running_var are only created in new() (230-242, zeros and ones) and read in forward_eval (342), with no assignment anywhere. forward_train (322-323) explicitly comments that the momentum update is skipped, and the fields are plain Option<Tensor> with forward taking &self, so they cannot be updated. Consequently eval subtracts mean=0 and divides by sqrt(1+eps)~=1, leaving input essentially un-normalized while training normalizes by live batch stats -- a genuine train/eval distribution mismatch. Severity medium rather than high because the module itself documents recommending LayerNorm/RMSNorm and BatchNorm is rarely used in RL. fix_is_safe=true with the constraint that the interior-mutability path must use Mutex/atomic/precompute, NOT RefCell as the finding suggests as one option, since RefCell breaks Sync required by Rayon VecEnv; the high-level fix (momentum-tracked running stats or documented train-only) corrects an already-incorrect result and does not break the public API.

title: DecisionTransformer double-encodes position: learned timestep embeddings + unconditional sinusoidal PE over interleaved tokens
description: DecisionTransformer::forward adds learned timestep embeddings to the return/state/action embeddings (lines 748-751), then interleaves them into a 3*seq_len token sequence and feeds them to self.transformer.forward(&tokens, None) (line 758). But TransformerEncoder::forward unconditionally adds sinusoidal positional encoding at line 518 (there is no config flag to skip it). So DT tokens receive BOTH a learned per-timestep embedding AND sinusoidal PE. Worse, the sinusoidal PE indexes the flat interleaved position (3t, 3t+1, 3t+2), which does not respect the 'R, S, A share timestep t' structure that the timestep embedding encodes — the two positional signals conflict. The reference Decision Transformer (Chen et al. 2021) deliberately uses timestep embeddings instead of GPT-style positional encoding for exactly this reason.
proposed_fix: Add a config flag (e.g. TransformerConfig.use_positional_encoding) and gate line 518 on it; construct the DT backbone with positional encoding disabled so position comes solely from the learned timestep embedding, matching the reference DT.
bench_to_confirm: No bench needed; trace DT.forward -> transformer.forward -> pos_encoding.forward. A correctness check would compare DT outputs/learning curves with PE on vs off against a reference DT implementation.
verify_reasoning: Verified: DecisionTransformer::forward (transformer.rs 748-751) adds learned timestep embeddings to return/state/action embeddings, interleaves into a 3*seq_len token sequence, and calls self.transformer.forward(&tokens, None) (758). TransformerEncoder::forward unconditionally adds sinusoidal positional encoding at line 518 with no config flag to disable it (PositionalEncoding is always built at 457). So DT tokens receive both signals, and the sinusoidal PE indexes flat positions (0..3*seq_len-1) rather than the per-timestep grouping that the timestep embedding encodes. The reference DT (Chen et al. 2021) deliberately omits transformer positional embeddings in favor of timestep embeddings (the official implementation explicitly strips GPT2's wpe), so this is a real deviation. The airtight grounds are 'PE added unconditionally with no way to disable + canonical DT omits PE'; the finding's 'the two positional signals conflict' is slightly overstated (they are redundant/complementary, not contradictory), but that does not affect is_real. fix_is_safe=true: adding a use_positional_encoding flag defaulting to current behavior and gating line 518 is backward-compatible (add serde default) and makes DT match the reference.

title: dropout configured throughout transformer/attention but never applied in any forward pass
location: TransformerConfig.dropout (line 41, with_dropout 87-90); DecisionTransformerConfig.dropout (621); attention.rs SelfAttentionConfig.dropout (36) and CrossAttentionConfig.dropout (263)
description: Both modules expose dropout fields and with_dropout builders (DecisionTransformer even defaults to 0.1 and forwards it via .with_dropout(config.dropout) at transformer.rs:706), but grep shows no Dropout module is ever instantiated and the dropout value is never read inside any forward(). Users who set dropout expecting regularization get none, which silently degrades training behavior versus expectation.
evidence: grep 'Dropout|::dropout' over src/networks/ returns only doc-comment and config-field hits; no candle_nn::Dropout usage in attention.rs/transformer.rs forwards. config.dropout flows into TransformerConfig and is then unused.
proposed_fix: Either wire candle_nn::Dropout into the attention/FFN/residual paths (active only in training mode) using the configured probability, or remove the dropout fields/builders and document that these modules are dropout-free to avoid the silent no-op.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed via grep: there is no candle_nn::Dropout anywhere in src, and the only references to dropout in attention.rs/transformer.rs are the config fields and the with_dropout builders (self.dropout = dropout); no forward() reads or applies the value, and DecisionTransformer forwards config.dropout into the backbone (706) where it is still ignored. So setting dropout is a silent no-op. Severity low is appropriate (regularization is silently absent but no crash/incorrect-shape). fix_is_safe=true via the recommended path (wire candle_nn::Dropout active only in training): dropout is identity at eval, so it does not change correct inference results, though it requires adding a training-mode flag to these modules (additional plumbing, not a breaking side effect). The alternative offered (removing the public dropout fields/builders) would break the public API, but the primary wiring fix does not.

title: Redundant first .contiguous() on K before it is immediately transposed and re-materialized
location: SelfAttention::forward lines 172-182 (k); same pattern in transformer.rs MultiHeadAttention lines 190-200 and CrossAttention lines 385-395
description: K is reshaped, transposed(1,2), and made contiguous to [batch, heads, seq, head_dim], then immediately transposed again (Minus2,Minus1) and made contiguous a second time to form k_t. The first .contiguous() materializes a layout that is discarded by the very next transpose+contiguous, wasting one full tensor allocation and copy per attention call. Candle transpose is a stride/metadata op that composes on non-contiguous tensors, so the two transposes can be fused into a single materialization.
evidence:
let k = k.reshape(...)?.transpose(1, 2)?.contiguous()?;   // materialize #1 (wasted)
let k_t = k.transpose(D::Minus2, D::Minus1)?.contiguous()?; // materialize #2
proposed_fix: Drop the .contiguous() on k after transpose(1,2) and compute k_t = k.reshape(...).transpose(1,2).transpose(D::Minus2,D::Minus1).contiguous(), materializing once. (Q and V still need their contiguous() for matmul.)
bench_to_confirm: Micro-bench attention forward on a large head_dim/seq to confirm one fewer allocation/copy per call.
verify_reasoning: Verified in attention.rs SelfAttention (172-182): k is reshaped, transpose(1,2), .contiguous() (materialize #1), then k is used ONLY at line 182 to form k_t = k.transpose(Minus2,Minus1).contiguous() (materialize #2). Same pattern in transformer.rs MultiHeadAttention (190-200) and CrossAttention (385-395), where k is likewise used solely to build k_t. Candle transpose is a stride/metadata op that composes on non-contiguous tensors, so the first contiguous materializes a layout immediately discarded by the next transpose+contiguous -- one wasted alloc+copy per attention call. Real but low: the wasted copy is O(batch*seq*d_model) versus the dominant O(seq^2*d) matmuls. fix_is_safe=true: reshape().transpose(1,2).transpose(Minus2,Minus1).contiguous() produces the identical [batch,heads,head_dim,seq] contiguous tensor (k is not used elsewhere), so the matmul input and numeric result are unchanged.

title: BatchNorm allocates a 1-element eps tensor via Tensor::from_slice on every forward
description: Each forward builds Tensor::from_slice(&[eps as f32], &[1], device) to add epsilon before sqrt, incurring a host allocation and a tiny H2D copy per call. Scalar add (Tensor + f64) or a cached eps tensor avoids the per-forward allocation/transfer.
evidence: let std = (var.broadcast_add(&Tensor::from_slice(&[self.config.eps as f32], &[1], x.device())?))?.sqrt()?;
proposed_fix: Use (var + self.config.eps)?.sqrt()? (candle supports Tensor + f64 scalar add, as already done in LayerNorm at line 118), eliminating the per-forward tensor construction.
verify_reasoning: Verified in normalization.rs: forward_train (326-331) and forward_eval (349-354) each build Tensor::from_slice(&[eps as f32], &[1], device) before sqrt, a per-forward host alloc plus a tiny H2D copy/kernel. LayerNorm at line 118 already uses the scalar form (var + self.config.eps)?.sqrt()? in this same crate, proving candle supports Tensor + f64 and that the fix compiles. Severity low (1-element allocation, marginal). fix_is_safe=true: Tensor + f64 applies eps to every element via affine, numerically equivalent to broadcast_add of the eps tensor; result identical, no API change.

title: FrameStack produces wrong tensor shape for multi-dimensional observations (cat vs stack)
location: FrameStack::new lines 47-53 (declared shape) and stack_frames lines 85-95 (actual concat)
description: For non-1D base observations (e.g. images), FrameStack declares an observation space of shape [n_stack, ...base_shape] but stack_frames concatenates the n_stack frames with Tensor::cat(&frames, 0), which concatenates along the EXISTING leading dimension instead of creating a new one. For a base obs of shape [C,H,W] the result is [n_stack*C, H, W], not the advertised [n_stack, C, H, W]. The canonical Atari case base [84,84] -> declared [4,84,84] actually yields [4*84,84]=[336,84]. The mismatch propagates: VecEnv::step then Tensor::stack's these per-env obs into a batch whose shape disagrees with what a CNN expecting [N,4,84,84] needs. The 1D branch is correct (declared [n_stack*dim], cat along dim 0 gives [n_stack*dim]); only the multi-dim branch is broken.
evidence: new(): `let stacked_shape = if base_shape.len() == 1 { vec![n_stack * base_shape[0]] } else { let mut shape = vec![n_stack]; shape.extend_from_slice(base_shape); shape };`  then stack_frames(): `let frames: Vec<&Tensor> = self.frames.iter().collect(); Tensor::cat(&frames, 0)`. Each frame has shape == base_shape, so cat-dim-0 of n_stack frames of shape [C,H,W] = [n_stack*C,H,W] != declared [n_stack,C,H,W].
proposed_fix: Keep Tensor::cat(&frames, 0) for the 1D base-shape branch (correct there), but for multi-dim base shapes use Tensor::stack(&frames, 0) to introduce the new leading n_stack dimension. Select cat vs stack based on base_shape.len()==1, matching the shape chosen in new().
bench_to_confirm: new micro-bench/unit test: FrameStack over a dummy env with obs shape [3,4]; assert stacked obs dims()==[n_stack,3,4]
verify_reasoning: Confirmed in src/envs/wrappers.rs. new() (lines 47-53) declares stacked_shape=[n_stack, ...base_shape] for base_shape.len()>1, but stack_frames() (lines 90-94) does Tensor::cat(&frames, 0) over frames each of shape==base_shape. For base [84,84] that yields [4*84,84]=[336,84], not declared [4,84,84]; obs_space.shape disagrees with the actual returned tensor's dims. The 1D branch is correct (cat dim0 of [dim] frames = [n_stack*dim] = declared). Note the underlying data is recoverable via reshape (cat-then-reshape == stack, same memory order), so this is a shape-contract violation producing a loud downstream shape mismatch (e.g. a CNN built from observation_space().shape()=[4,84,84] receiving [N,336,84]) rather than silent numeric corruption. Real bug; downgraded from high to medium because only multi-dim/image observations are affected, this trading-focused repo has no multi-dim FrameStack usage, and the failure is loud and recoverable. Fix is safe: selecting cat for len==1 and Tensor::stack(&frames,0) for multi-dim matches the shape chosen in new(), preserves the correct 1D path, and produces a tensor matching the declared space.

title: Auto-reset discards the terminal observation, making truncation bootstrapping impossible
location: step() sync path line 423; worker path line 375; step_async lines 488 and 554; VecStepResult struct lines 168-180
description: On a done step with auto_reset, VecEnv replaces the real terminal observation with the freshly-reset observation via `auto_reset_obs.unwrap_or(step_result.observation)` and the terminal observation is preserved nowhere (VecStepResult has no terminal-obs field, and the per-env StepInfo only carries f32 scalars). This is correct Gym-style auto-reset for the next-state, but it irreversibly loses s_terminal. The rollout buffer's GAE (src/buffer/mod.rs:408-418, labeled 'CRITICAL FIX') deliberately bootstraps truncated transitions with V(s'), but the only s' it can ever see for a truncated step is the reset observation of the NEW episode, so the truncation bootstrap value is computed from the wrong state. The data needed to do it correctly is destroyed at the VecEnv layer.
evidence: vecenv.rs:423 `let obs = auto_reset_obs.unwrap_or(step_result.observation);` (step_result.observation is the terminal obs and is dropped). Same at :375 and :488/:554. VecStepResult{observations,rewards,terminated,truncated,infos} has no terminal-observation field. StepInfo.extra is `HashMap<String,f32>` (traits.rs:44) so it cannot carry a Tensor.
proposed_fix: Preserve the terminal observation when auto-resetting: add a tensor-carrying field, e.g. `terminal_observations: Vec<Option<Tensor>>` on VecStepResult (or a `Tensor` field on StepInfo), populated with step_result.observation whenever auto_reset replaces it. Have the rollout collector use that tensor to compute the bootstrap value V(s_terminal) for truncated steps. Note this is a small API addition, not a one-line change, because StepInfo cannot currently hold tensors.
bench_to_confirm: ppo_benchmark on a TimeLimit-truncated env; compare value-loss/return curves before vs after preserving terminal obs
verify_reasoning: Fully confirmed across all paths. vecenv.rs:423 (sync), :375 and :488/:554 (worker/async) all do auto_reset_obs.unwrap_or(step_result.observation), dropping the terminal obs when done. VecStepResult (168-180) has no terminal-obs field; StepInfo.extra is HashMap<String,f32> (traits.rs:44) and StepInfo derives Default while Tensor has no Default, so it cannot carry a tensor. The PPO rollout loop confirms the consequence: ppo.rs:600-630 stores values[t]=V(obs_t) then sets obs=step_result.observations (the reset obs for done steps); buffer GAE (buffer/mod.rs:408-418) bootstraps truncated transitions with next_value=values.get(step+1)=V(reset_obs) since the not_terminated mask is 1 for truncations. So the explicit 'CRITICAL FIX' truncation-bootstrap operates on the wrong state, defeating its purpose; a2c.rs/ppg.rs share the same pattern. No terminal_observation handling exists anywhere (grep confirms only an unrelated nstep.rs usage). High severity is warranted: truncation via TimeLimit is common and the bootstrap bias occurs at every episode boundary. Fix is safe because it is additive: adding Vec<Option<Tensor>> to VecStepResult (or Option<Tensor> on StepInfo, which preserves the Default/Clone/Debug derives) populated with step_result.observation when auto-reset replaces it, plus a rollout-collector change to bootstrap from V(s_terminal). It doesn't break existing fields or change currently-correct results.

title: RunningMeanStd Welford combination accumulates in f32, freezing variance after ~16M samples
description: The parallel-variance (Chan) combination is algebraically correct, but the intermediate sums-of-squares are accumulated in f32 while the sample count is f64. m_a = var*count grows ~linearly with total count; once count exceeds ~2^24 (~16.7M), adding m_b (order of a single batch's variance) to m_a rounds away to zero in f32, so var stops updating and effectively freezes. Long PPO/continuous-control runs that normalize observations across tens of millions of steps will see the running variance silently stop tracking the data. Reference implementations (SB3 RunningMeanStd) accumulate these moments in f64.
evidence: `let m_a = self.var.iter().map(|v| v * self.count as f32).collect::<Vec<_>>();` and `let m2 = m_a[j] + m_b[j] + delta[j]*delta[j]*(self.count*batch_count/total_count) as f32; self.var[j] = m2 / total_count as f32;` — m_a, m_b, m2 are all f32 but self.count is f64 and can reach >1e7.
proposed_fix: Store mean/var (or at least the M2 accumulators) and perform the combine in f64, converting to f32 only when exposing normalize()/std(); or store M2 directly in f64 and derive var = M2/count on read.
bench_to_confirm: micro-bench: feed 5e7 samples from N(0,1) in small batches; assert reported std stays ~1.0 instead of drifting/freezing
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: m_a, m_b, m2 are all f32 while self.count is f64 (wrappers.rs:303-318). The Chan combination is algebraically correct but the precision claim holds and the failure mode is worse than a benign freeze. Tracing the single-sample recurrence used by NormalizeObservation (batch_count=1 => batch_var=0 => m_b=0): once delta^2 falls below ULP(var*count) near count~2^24, m2=m_a=var*count exactly, so var_new=var*count/(count+1); telescoping gives var_n=var_0*count_0/count_n -> 0, i.e. the variance slowly COLLAPSES toward zero rather than freezing at the true value. A correct f64 accumulation holds the variance. After collapse, normalize() divides by sqrt(var)->0 and observations saturate to +/-clip_obs. SB3's RunningMeanStd accumulates these moments in float64, so this is a genuine divergence from the reference. Kept at low (not the medium claimed) purely on the trigger: with per-env single-sample updates it requires ~16M+ steps per env to reach 2^24. Fix is safe: keep M2/var accumulators in f64 and derive f32 on read; mean()/std() public signatures can be preserved by retaining an f32 mean cache, so no API break and the result only becomes more accurate.

title: NormalizeObservation/NormalizeReward keep independent per-env running stats when vectorized
location: NormalizeObservation lines 347-405; via traits.rs make_vectorized lines 82-94 and vecenv.rs::new lines 233-235
description: make_vectorized/VecEnv::new clone the (already-wrapped) template environment num_envs times, so each parallel env owns its own RunningMeanStd. Normalization statistics therefore diverge across the batch (each env normalizes with stats estimated from only its own 1/num_envs slice of the data), unlike the standard SB3 VecNormalize which maintains a single shared running stat over all parallel envs. Consequences: inconsistent normalization across the observation batch fed to the network, and num_envs-times-slower warmup of the statistics. This is a semantic divergence from VecNormalize rather than a math error, and depends on composition order (wrap-then-vectorize), so it may be partly intended.
evidence: traits.rs make_vectorized: `VecEnv::new(vec![self], num_envs)`; vecenv.rs:233-235 `let envs: Vec<E> = (0..num_envs).map(|i| template_envs[i % template_envs.len()].clone()).collect();` — NormalizeObservation derives Clone, so obs_rms is cloned independently per env with no shared aggregation.
proposed_fix: For shared normalization, apply NormalizeObservation/Reward as a VecEnv-level wrapper that owns a single RunningMeanStd updated from the full [num_envs,...] batch each step, rather than wrapping per-env before vectorization. Alternatively document that per-env wrapping yields per-env stats.
verify_reasoning: Mechanism verified: traits.rs:88/92 make_vectorized calls VecEnv::new(_async)(vec![self], num_envs); vecenv.rs:233-235 clones the already-wrapped template per env; NormalizeObservation/NormalizeReward derive Clone (wrappers.rs:347/444) so each env owns an independent RunningMeanStd with no shared aggregation. This is a real semantic divergence from SB3 VecNormalize, which maintains a single shared running statistic over the full [num_envs,...] batch; consequences are per-env normalization (inconsistent stats across the observation batch fed to the network) and ~num_envs-times-slower stat warmup. Anchored against the SB3 shared-stats reference so this is not merely flagging a design choice. Not a hard correctness bug (each env still normalizes and stats converge across envs eventually), and the finding itself notes it depends on wrap-then-vectorize composition order and may be partly intended, so severity is low rather than the claimed medium. Fix is safe: applying normalization as a VecEnv-level wrapper with one shared RMS aligns with the reference and is an additive usage pattern (the documentation-only alternative is trivially safe); it does not break a currently-canonical correct result.

title: normalize_obs does a GPU<->CPU round-trip and two heap allocations every step
description: Every step (and reset) flattens the observation to a host Vec via to_vec1(), then rebuilds a Tensor via from_slice. On Metal/CUDA, to_vec1() forces a device synchronization per env per step, and from_slice re-uploads. It also allocates at least two Vecs per call (the normalize() output and the clipped output) on top of the to_vec1 allocation. Inside VecEnv's rayon parallel step this serializes many small device syncs. For CPU envs it is pure alloc churn in the hot loop.
evidence: `let data: Vec<f32> = obs.flatten_all()?.to_vec1()?;` ... `let normalized = self.obs_rms.normalize(&data);` (alloc) ... `let clipped: Vec<f32> = normalized.into_iter().map(...).collect();` (alloc) ... `Tensor::from_slice(&clipped, shape, &candle_device)`.
proposed_fix: Fuse normalize+clip into a single pass writing into one preallocated/reused buffer (avoid the second Vec), and reuse a per-wrapper scratch buffer instead of allocating each step. Where feasible, perform the affine normalize on-device as a tensor op ((obs-mean)/std then clamp) to avoid the to_vec1/from_slice round-trip entirely.
bench_to_confirm: env_benchmark with NormalizeObservation wrapper on CPU and Metal; compare steps/sec before/after
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at wrappers.rs:388-404: obs.flatten_all()?.to_vec1()? (host copy / device sync + alloc), obs_rms.normalize(&data) (alloc Vec), then a clipped collect (second alloc Vec), then Tensor::from_slice (re-upload). normalize_obs is called every step and reset (lines 421/426), so these are genuinely on the hot path. The round-trip/sync claim only bites for GPU-resident envs (uncommon: envs typically run on CPU even when the policy trains on GPU), and trading observations are small vectors, so the dominant real cost is per-step alloc churn rather than device sync. Real but low rather than medium. Fix is safe: fusing normalize+clip into a single pass over a reused per-wrapper scratch buffer yields identical numeric results with fewer allocations; an optional on-device affine normalize+clamp would also preserve the math.

title: FrameStack re-concatenates all n_stack frames into a new tensor every step
description: Each step collects all frames and calls Tensor::cat over the full window, copying every one of the n_stack frames into a freshly allocated tensor on every step even though only one frame changed. The ring buffer itself is fine (VecDeque pop_front/push_back are O(1), not the .remove(0) antipattern), but the per-step full re-concat is O(n_stack * frame_size) of pure copy.
evidence: step(): pop_front/push_back then `let stacked_obs = self.stack_frames(device)?;` which does `Tensor::cat(&frames, 0)` over all n_stack frames each call.
proposed_fix: Maintain a preallocated stacked buffer and shift/overwrite only the newest slot (or keep a single contiguous tensor and roll it), avoiding a full n_stack copy per step. For GPU tensors consider an on-device slice-assign.
bench_to_confirm: env_benchmark with FrameStack(n_stack=4) wrapper; steps/sec before/after
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: step() (wrappers.rs:126-131) pops front / pushes back (VecDeque O(1), correctly avoiding the .remove(0) antipattern) then calls stack_frames() which Tensor::cat's all n_stack frames into a fresh tensor (lines 90-94), copying every frame each step though only one changed: O(n_stack*frame_size) copy per step. Real but minor, correctly self-labeled low; FrameStack is only re-exported and not used in this repo's examples/benches. Fix is safe in correctness terms: a carefully implemented preallocated rolling buffer produces an identical tensor. Caveat noted: candle tensors are immutable, so an on-device slice-assign would still allocate, meaning the proposed optimization may yield limited GPU benefit, but it would not break correctness or the API.

title: RunningMeanStd::std() allocates a Vec every call, invoked per-step in NormalizeReward
location: RunningMeanStd::std lines 341-343; called from NormalizeReward::normalize_reward line 503
description: std() maps var.iter() into a freshly allocated Vec<f32> and is called once per step inside reward normalization purely to read element [0]. For dim=1 (the reward RMS) this is a heap allocation each step to fetch a single scalar.
evidence: `pub fn std(&self) -> Vec<f32> { self.var.iter().map(|v| v.sqrt()).collect() }` and in normalize_reward: `let std = self.return_rms.std()[0];`.
proposed_fix: Add a `std_at(i)` / `var_at(i)` accessor (or `std_scalar()` for dim==1) that returns the value without allocating, and use it in normalize_reward.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: std() collects var.iter().map(sqrt) into a fresh Vec<f32> (wrappers.rs:341-343) and normalize_reward reads only [0] from it (line 503), so for the dim==1 return RMS this is a heap allocation per step to fetch a single scalar. Real, minor, correctly low. Fix is safe: adding a non-allocating std_at(i)/var_at(i) accessor (or std_scalar()) returns the identical value and is purely additive to the API.

title: BoxSpace::sample uniform [-1,1] for unbounded dims contradicts comment and ignores one-sided finite bounds
description: For any dimension where either lo or hi is infinite, sample returns rng.gen::<f32>()*2-1 (uniform on [-1,1]), but the comment says 'Standard normal-ish'. It is uniform, not normal. Additionally, if only one side is infinite (e.g. low=0, high=+inf), the finite bound is ignored entirely and the sample can be negative. Minor since sampling random actions/obs from spaces is rarely on a hot path.
evidence: `if lo.is_infinite() || hi.is_infinite() { rng.gen::<f32>() * 2.0 - 1.0 // Standard normal-ish for unbounded }`
proposed_fix: Fix the comment to say 'uniform [-1,1]', or draw from a standard normal to match it; and handle the half-bounded case (sample relative to the finite side) instead of discarding it.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed at space.rs:96-100: when either lo or hi is infinite it returns rng.gen::<f32>()*2.0-1.0 (uniform on [-1,1]) with a comment 'Standard normal-ish' (it is uniform, not normal), and a one-sided-infinite dim (e.g. low=0, high=+inf) discards the finite bound entirely so samples can violate it (e.g. go negative). Real cleanup/low. Fix is safe when grounded in the safe options: correcting the comment and handling the half-bounded case (sample relative to the finite side) preserve or improve correctness without API change. Note the alternative uniform->standard-normal swap is NOT result-preserving (it changes bounded [-1,1] output to unbounded and could surprise callers assuming bounded samples), so safety rests on the comment + half-bounded fixes, and space sampling is off the hot path anyway.

title: simd::compute_gae / compute_gae_avx2 use a single `dones` mask for both bootstrap and GAE trace — no value bootstrap on truncation
location: x86.rs compute_gae_avx2_impl lines 1189-1263; mod.rs compute_gae lines 1077-1177 (FFI gae_compute_with_returns_neon) and scalar fallback lines 1159-1174
description: There are two divergent GAE APIs in the simd module. gae.rs::compute_gae_simd(_inplace) correctly takes separate `terminated`/`truncated` arrays: bootstrap_mask = 1-terminated, trace_mask = 1-min(terminated+truncated,1) — this is what rollout.rs actually calls. But the older public exports simd::compute_gae (mod.rs, both the aarch64 FFI path and the non-aarch64 scalar fallback) and simd::compute_gae_avx2 (x86.rs) take a single `dones` array and use `mask = 1 - dones` for BOTH the bootstrap term and the gamma*lambda trace decay. That means on a time-limit truncation (where `dones`=1) they zero the bootstrap (delta = r - V instead of r + gamma*V_next - V), reintroducing exactly the value-collapse bug that rollout.rs's docstring says was fixed. These functions are publicly re-exported (mod.rs lines 84-87) so any external caller / future rewiring silently gets truncation-incorrect GAE that disagrees with the internal path.
evidence: x86.rs:1224 `let mask = _mm256_sub_ps(one_vec, done);` then 1227-1229 `gamma_next = gamma*next_value; gamma_next_masked = gamma_next*mask; delta = (reward+gamma_next_masked)-value;` and 1232-1233 `gae_decay = gamma_lambda*mask; last_gae = fmadd(gae_decay,last_gae,delta);` — same `mask` (=1-done) gates both bootstrap and trace. Contrast gae.rs:279-295 which derives `bootstrap_mask = 1-terminated` and a separate `trace_mask = 1-min(terminated+truncated,1)`.
proposed_fix: Either delete the single-`dones` compute_gae / compute_gae_avx2 / FFI gae paths, or change their signatures (and the C kernel) to accept separate terminated/truncated and apply bootstrap_mask=1-terminated vs trace_mask=1-max(terminated,truncated), matching gae.rs. At minimum, mark them deprecated and document that they treat truncation as termination.
bench_to_confirm: micro-bench: feed a rollout with a truncation flag set and assert simd::compute_gae_avx2 advantages != gae::compute_gae_simd advantages; ppo_benchmark would not catch it since PPO uses the gae.rs path
verify_reasoning: Confirmed from code. gae.rs::compute_gae_simd/_inplace correctly uses separate masks (bootstrap_mask=1-terminated at scalar_range:602, trace_mask=1-min(term+trunc,1) at :603) and is the path actually wired into production (rollout.rs:273 calls compute_gae_simd_inplace, with its own scalar fallback). The single-`dones` variants are genuinely divergent: mod.rs compute_gae scalar fallback (1165-1168) and x86.rs compute_gae_avx2_impl (1224-1233) both apply mask=1-done to BOTH the gamma*next_value bootstrap and the gamma*lambda trace, so on done=1 (used for truncation) delta collapses to r-V instead of r+gamma*V_next-V. They are pub and re-exported (mod.rs:84-87, top-level compute_gae). So the factual and mathematical claim is accurate. Severity downgraded to low: grep shows compute_gae and compute_gae_avx2 have no production callers (only their own test modules at mod.rs:1418, x86.rs:1419); this is a latent public-API footgun, not an active hot-path defect, and a single-`dones` GAE is a legitimate (older SB3-style) contract for callers without truncation. fix_is_safe=false: the primary proposed fixes (delete the pub re-exported fns, or change their signatures plus the C FFI kernel) are breaking public-API changes; only the secondary 'mark deprecated' option is safe but it does not fix correctness.

title: AVX2 log-prob kernels use polynomial fast_exp/fast_log/fast_tanh on SIMD lanes but exact libm on the remainder lanes (not equivalent to scalar/NEON)
location: gaussian_log_prob_avx2 lines 447-495; squashed_gaussian_log_prob_avx2 503-561; squashed_gaussian_log_prob_from_squashed_avx2 569-635; fast_log_avx2 748-779; (mirrored in x86.rs sample_with_log_prob_avx2 496-536)
description: The AVX2 log-probability paths compute std=exp(log_std) via fast_exp_avx2, log(std)/atanh/jacobian via fast_log_avx2, and tanh via fast_tanh_avx2 — all low-order minimax polynomials — for the first chunks*8 elements, then fall back to EXACT std.exp()/ln()/tanh() for the tail remainder (e.g. lines 490-494, 551-560, 626-634). Consequences: (1) the AVX2 result is not bit- or even ~1e-4-equivalent to the documented closed form `-0.5*(log2pi + 2*log_std + z^2)`; (2) the NEON gaussian path (lines 824-835 uses exact .exp()) and the scalar path are exact, so AVX2 disagrees with NEON/scalar; (3) within ONE call, two elements with identical inputs get different log-probs depending on whether they land in a full SIMD lane or the tail. fast_log_avx2 error is ~6e-4 absolute (verified: at m=1.5 the poly gives 0.40521 vs ln(1.5)=0.40547). For a summed multi-dim action log-prob this compounds. If a PPO/SAC ratio exp(logp_new-logp_old) ever mixes this kernel with an exact path, it injects a systematic bias.
evidence: log_prob.rs:468 `let std = fast_exp_avx2(ls);` for the vector body, vs remainder log_prob.rs:491 `let std = log_stds[i].exp();`. fast_log_avx2 is a 6-coef Horner poly (752-778) with no Newton refinement; hand-eval at m=1.5 yields 0.40521 vs exact 0.40547 (abs err 2.6e-4, and ~6e-4 elsewhere on [1,2)).
proposed_fix: Either (a) make the SIMD lanes and the remainder use the same math (drop fast_* and store/scalar-exp the lanes like the NEON gaussian path already does), or (b) add a unit test asserting AVX2 == scalar within a tight tolerance and replace fast_exp/fast_log with a higher-accuracy (Newton-refined) version. Do NOT mix approximate body with exact tail.
bench_to_confirm: new micro-test: compare gaussian_log_prob_simd (AVX2) vs gaussian_log_prob_scalar on random inputs with n not a multiple of 8; assert max abs diff < 1e-5 (currently fails on body vs tail)
verify_reasoning: Confirmed structurally. gaussian_log_prob_avx2 body uses fast_exp_avx2 (468) while remainder uses std.exp() (491); squashed_gaussian_log_prob_avx2 body uses fast_exp/fast_tanh/fast_log (526-541) vs exact tail (552-557); from_squashed_avx2 body uses fast_log/fast_exp (601-617) vs exact tail (627-632). NEON gaussian path uses exact .exp() (line 833) and scalar paths (915-944) use exact .exp()/.tanh()/.ln(). So AVX2 body diverges from AVX2 tail, NEON, and scalar — accurate. fast_log_avx2 is a 6-coef minimax Horner poly (748-779) with no Newton refinement; its ~1e-4 absolute error claim is consistent with such a polynomial. Severity downgraded to low: grep confirms gaussian_log_prob_simd/squashed_gaussian_log_prob_simd and the batch wrappers have NO non-test callers in the crate (production log-probs go through candle Tensor ops in ppo.rs/distributions). The body/tail inconsistency is a genuine defect-smell but currently latent. fix_is_safe=true: making the lanes use the same exact math as the tail/NEON (NEON already does scalar-exp-in-lanes) preserves the API and improves correctness.

title: fast_sincos_avx2 / fast_sin/cos_avx512 use Taylor series over full [-pi,pi]; large error near ±pi distorts Box-Muller Gaussian samples
location: fast_sincos_avx2 lines 362-407; fast_sin_avx512 734-755; fast_cos_avx512 760-777; used by sample_standard_normal_avx2 279-281 and _avx512 665-669
description: Box-Muller computes theta = 2*pi*u2 with u2 in [-0.5,0.5), so theta spans the full [-pi,pi). The sin/cos are evaluated by range-reducing to [-pi,pi] (lines 369-370) and then a fixed Taylor polynomial (sin to x^7, cos to x^6). Taylor truncation over the whole [-pi,pi] is very inaccurate near the endpoints: at x=pi the degree-7 sin gives -0.0752 (true 0, err ~0.075) and the degree-6 cos gives -1.2114 (true -1, err ~0.21). Worse, cos^2+sin^2 ≈ 1.47 at x=pi instead of 1, so the (cos,sin) point leaves the unit circle and the Box-Muller radius is mis-scaled — the produced 'standard normals' are distorted in both shape and variance for samples whose theta is near ±pi. The unit test only checks mean within 0.5 and var within 0.5, so it passes despite the distortion.
evidence: x86.rs:369-370 reduces only to [-pi,pi]: `n = round(x/(2pi)); x_reduced = x - n*2pi;` then 394-404 apply Taylor directly. Hand-eval at x=pi: sin = pi - pi^3/6 + pi^5/120 - pi^7/5040 = -0.0752; cos = 1 - pi^2/2 + pi^4/24 - pi^6/720 = -1.2114; cos^2+sin^2 = 1.47.
proposed_fix: Range-reduce to [-pi/4, pi/4] (with quadrant selection) before the Taylor polynomial, or add 2 more Taylor terms, or normalize by 1/sqrt(sin^2+cos^2). Tighten the sampler test to a KS/moment test on the tails.
bench_to_confirm: new test: sample 1e6 from GaussianSamplerAvx2, check 3rd/4th moments and a chi-square on histogram tails; the AVX2 sampler is standalone (not used by core algorithms, which sample via Candle), so existing benches won't catch it
verify_reasoning: Confirmed. fast_sincos_avx2 (362-407) range-reduces only to [-pi,pi] via round(x/2pi) (368-370) then applies fixed Taylor (sin to x^7, cos to x^6); fast_sin/cos_avx512 (734-777) are identical in structure. theta=2*pi*u2 with u2=cvtepi32_ps(signed)*2^-32 ∈ [-0.5,0.5) (AVX2 line 264, AVX512 line 654), so theta ∈ [-pi,pi) — endpoints are exercised. Hand-check at x=pi reproduces the claim: degree-7 sin = -0.0753, degree-6 cos = -1.2114, cos^2+sin^2 ≈ 1.47, so the (cos,sin) point leaves the unit circle and mis-scales the Box-Muller radius for samples whose theta nears ±pi. Math is correct. Severity downgraded to low: the AVX2/AVX512 GaussianSampler structs have no production callers (grep finds GaussianSamplerAvx2/sample_standard_normal_avx2 only inside src/simd and tests; production sampling uses candle Tensor ops). fix_is_safe=true: range-reducing to [-pi/4,pi/4] with quadrant selection, adding terms, or renormalizing by 1/sqrt(sin^2+cos^2) all improve accuracy without changing the API.

title: SIMD dispatchers silently no-op (zeros / un-normalized) if compiled with +avx2 but runtime AVX2 detection fails
description: In compute_gae_impl the AVX2 arm (cfg target_feature=avx2+fma) runs only `if is_x86_feature_detected!("avx2") && ...{ ...; return; }`, and the scalar fallback block is `#[cfg(not(any(neon, avx2+fma)))]` — so it is NOT compiled into an +avx2 build. If the static cfg is true but the runtime check returns false, no branch executes and `advantages`/`returns` are left as the all-zero pre-fill from compute_gae_simd (lines 141-142), silently corrupting training. normalize_advantages_impl has the same structure (lines 658-666 return-guarded, scalar block cfg-excluded) leaving advantages un-normalized. This is mostly theoretical (running a +avx2 binary on a non-AVX2 CPU is already UB), but the failure mode is silent wrong-data rather than an error.
evidence: gae.rs:480-497 `if is_x86_feature_detected!("avx2") && is_x86_feature_detected!("fma") { unsafe { compute_gae_avx2(...) } return; }` with the scalar block at 501-523 gated `#[cfg(not(any(... x86_64 avx2 fma)))]` — no else path remains in an +avx2 build.
proposed_fix: Make the scalar fallback always-compiled (remove it from the cfg(not(...)) exclusion or add an explicit `else` calling compute_gae_scalar) so a failed runtime feature check degrades to correct scalar output instead of leaving zeros.
bench_to_confirm: N/A (build/runtime-feature edge; covered by adding an unconditional scalar fallback + a test that forces the non-SIMD branch)
verify_reasoning: The code structure is exactly as described: in compute_gae_impl the AVX2 arm (gae.rs:480-497) is guarded by `if is_x86_feature_detected!(avx2)&&fma { ...; return; }` with no else, and the scalar block (501-523) is cfg(not(any(neon, avx2+fma))), so it is NOT compiled into an +avx2 build; a runtime-false result therefore leaves advantages/returns as the all-zero prefill (141-142). normalize_advantages_impl (650-679) has the same shape. is_real=true because the structural fallback gap genuinely exists. Severity kept low (a structural robustness gap with a cheap defensive fix), NOT medium: the harmful consequence cannot manifest in a well-formed program — in a +avx2 build is_x86_feature_detected!(avx2) is effectively always true on any CPU that can execute the binary, and the only path to false is running on non-AVX2 hardware, which already SIGILLs/UB elsewhere. The finding itself acknowledges this is theoretical. fix_is_safe=true: adding an explicit else calling the always-compiled compute_gae_scalar (or un-gating the scalar block) degrades safely and changes no public behavior on supported CPUs.

title: normalize_advantages_simd computes mean/variance with naive sequential f32 summation over the full advantage array
description: mean = advantages.iter().sum::<f32>()/n and var_sum = sum((x-mean)^2) are naive sequential f32 reductions. For PPO rollouts the array is num_steps*num_envs (e.g. 2048*64 = 131072) elements; naive f32 accumulation loses precision (error grows ~ n*eps), so the computed mean/std (and thus the normalized advantages feeding the policy loss) can drift relative to a pairwise/f64 reduction. The two-pass population variance (divide by n, eps inside sqrt) is otherwise a fine match to the SB3 reference; only the accumulation precision is the issue.
evidence: gae.rs:637 `let sum: f32 = advantages.iter().sum();` and 641 `let var_sum: f32 = advantages.iter().map(|&x| (x - mean).powi(2)).sum();` — both default sequential f32 fold over up to ~1.3e5 elements.
proposed_fix: Accumulate the sum and sum-of-squares in f64 (or use a pairwise/Kahan reduction) before casting back to f32; this is cheap relative to the per-element normalize and removes the precision concern.
bench_to_confirm: micro-test comparing mean/std from naive f32 sum vs f64 accumulation on 131072 O(1)-magnitude values; difference is observable at ~1e-3
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: gae.rs:637 `let sum: f32 = advantages.iter().sum()` and :641 `var_sum: f32 = ...map(|x|(x-mean).powi(2)).sum()` are sequential f32 folds; Iterator::sum for f32 does no pairwise/Kahan reduction, so accumulation error does grow with n (worst-case ~n*eps) over PPO-scale arrays. The two-pass population variance formula itself is a correct SB3 match; only accumulation precision is at issue, as claimed. Severity low: the practical impact is negligible (std error ~1e-4 relative on a quantity that is normalized to unit scale, to which the policy loss is robust) and grep confirms normalize_advantages_simd has no production callers (PPO uses normalize_advantages on a candle Tensor at ppo.rs:440; buffer/mod.rs:723 uses its own Tensor path). It is an accurate but minor precision nit. fix_is_safe=true: accumulating sum and sum-of-squares in f64 (or Kahan) before casting back is cheap and changes neither the API nor any observably-correct result.

title: GAE kernel over-dispatch aliases and corrupts real outputs (no env_id bounds guard)
location: gae_compute kernel lines 286-314; dispatch lines 744-747 in compute_gae()
description: compute_gae() dispatches ceil(num_envs/64) threadgroups of size 64.min(num_envs), i.e. ceil(num_envs/64)*64 >= num_envs total threads, but the gae_compute kernel has NO `if (env_id >= num_envs) return;` guard. Because the output index is 2D (idx = step*num_envs + env_id), a phantom env_id >= num_envs does NOT write past the buffer end harmlessly — it aliases a VALID slot belonging to a different step's real column and overwrites it. Triggers whenever num_envs > 64 && num_envs % 64 != 0 (e.g. 96, 100, 200 — all realistic vec-env counts; 128/256 happen to be safe). This is silent results-corruption of advantages/returns, not just UB. Latent: MetalContext is only re-exported (simd/mod.rs:67) and never called by any algorithm/bench, so it is not in the active training path, but it is public API.
evidence: Kernel: `uint idx = uint(step) * num_envs + env_id; ... advantages[idx] = last_gae; returns[idx] = last_gae + values[idx];` with no env_id range check. Dispatch: `let thread_group_size = MTLSize::new(64.min(num_envs as u64), 1, 1); let thread_groups = MTLSize::new((num_envs as u64 + 63) / 64, 1, 1);`. With num_envs=100, num_steps=128: group=64, groups=2 -> 128 threads; phantom env_id=100,step=0 writes advantages[100] which is the real (step=1,env=0) slot.
proposed_fix: Add `if (env_id >= num_envs) return;` as the first line of gae_compute (num_envs is already a kernel constant). Alternatively switch the host call from dispatch_thread_groups to dispatch_threads (non-uniform threadgroups), which launches exactly num_envs threads.
bench_to_confirm: New metal-feature unit test: compute_gae with num_envs=100 compared against the CPU reference simd::gae::compute_gae_simd; outputs will diverge until the guard is added.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed from code. dispatch_thread_groups(ceil(num_envs/64), 64.min(num_envs)) launches ceil(num_envs/64)*64 threads, and gae_compute (lines 286-314) has no env_id>=num_envs guard. Because the write index is 2D (idx = step*num_envs + env_id), a phantom env_id>=num_envs aliases a VALID slot of another step's real column, e.g. num_envs=100: env_id=100,step=0 -> idx=100 = real (step=1,env=0). This is silent corruption of advantages/returns, not benign OOB. Triggers for num_envs in {96,100,200,...} (num_envs>64 && %64!=0); 128/256 safe. Reachable via public MetalContext::compute_gae. Fix (add `if (env_id >= num_envs) return;`) only suppresses phantom threads and leaves valid env_ids untouched, and changes only the internal kernel, not the public Rust signature, so it is safe.
