title: Element-wise/argmax kernels over-dispatch with no bounds check (out-of-bounds GPU read/write, UB)
location: kernels gaussian_log_prob(92-107), gaussian_entropy(111-117), ppo_policy_loss(127-146), ppo_value_loss(150-172), ppo_combined_loss(175-204), argmax_row(259-278); dispatch sites e.g. 489-491, 561-563, 684-686, 778-780
description: All element-wise kernels dispatch ceil(n/256) groups of 256 threads (argmax: ceil(batch/64) groups of 64), i.e. more threads than elements when n is not a multiple of the threadgroup size, yet none of the kernels guard `if (id >= n) return;`. Phantom threads id in [n, ceil(n/256)*256) read inputs[id] and write outputs[id] past the end of the exactly-sized (n*4 byte) buffers. Unlike the GAE kernel these write only their own out-of-range slot, so valid [0,n) outputs are untouched (which is why the n=3/n=4 tests pass) — but it is still out-of-bounds memory access (undefined behavior). It is masked because StorageModeShared MTLBuffers are page-rounded (~16KB), so a small overrun lands in slack; it CAN fault for an n whose n*4 sits just under a page boundary.
evidence: `let thread_group_size = MTLSize::new(256, 1, 1); let thread_groups = MTLSize::new((n as u64 + 255) / 256, 1, 1); encoder.dispatch_thread_groups(thread_groups, thread_group_size);` paired with kernels that index `log_probs[id]`, `losses[id] = ...` with no `id < n` check. n=3 -> 1 group of 256 -> threads 3..255 write losses_buf[id] though losses_buf is 12 bytes.
proposed_fix: Pass element count n as a `constant uint&` to each kernel and early-return `if (id >= n) return;`, or replace dispatch_thread_groups with dispatch_threads (dispatchThreads:threadsPerThreadgroup:) so the grid is exactly n.
bench_to_confirm: metal-feature unit test calling e.g. gaussian_log_prob with n=300 (not a multiple of 256) under an address sanitizer / Metal validation layer; demonstrates the benign OOB write.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: element-wise kernels dispatch ceil(n/256)*256 threads, argmax dispatches ceil(batch/64)*64, none guard id/batch_id. Because these are 1D-indexed, phantom threads only touch their own out-of-range slot, so valid [0,n) outputs are never corrupted (why n=3/n=4 tests pass) — claim is accurate. With 16KB shared-buffer page rounding the element-wise overrun (<=255 extra floats) always lands within the page, so it effectively never faults; that part is mostly benign UB. However argmax phantom batch_id reads offset=batch_id*num_classes, i.e. up to a full extra row past the buffer, which CAN exceed the allocation and fault for moderate num_classes (e.g. batch=100, num_classes=100). Real OOB access; no valid-output corruption and masked for small buffers, so medium not high. Fix passes element count as a constant and early-returns, changing only kernel internals (not the public API) and not altering valid results — safe.

title: Per-call Metal buffer (re)allocation with no pooling or reuse
location: create_buffer(425-433)/create_empty_buffer(436-441) called fresh in every op: gaussian_log_prob(475-478), ppo_policy_loss(543-548), ppo_value_loss(592-597), ppo_loss(652-665), compute_gae(716-727)
description: Every invocation allocates brand-new GPU buffers for all inputs, outputs, and even the tiny scalar constants (clip_range, vf_coef, num_steps, etc. each become a separate device buffer). Nothing is cached across calls. In a PPO update these helpers would be called per-minibatch per-epoch, so allocation + driver bookkeeping dominates over the trivial element-wise arithmetic. Scalar constants in particular should be passed with set_bytes (inline) rather than a heap-allocated MTLBuffer each call.
evidence: `let clip_range_buf = self.create_buffer(&[clip_range])?; let vf_coef_buf = self.create_buffer(&[vf_coef])?; let ent_coef_buf = self.create_buffer(&[ent_coef])?;` (lines 663-665) — three GPU allocations for three scalars, repeated every ppo_loss call; same pattern in compute_gae (724-727).
proposed_fix: Maintain a size-keyed buffer pool on MetalContext (reuse output/input buffers across calls), and pass scalar constants via encoder.set_bytes instead of allocating a Buffer per scalar.
bench_to_confirm: ppo_benchmark / a micro-bench looping ppo_loss N times: compare wall time with pooled+set_bytes vs current per-call allocation.
verify_reasoning: Code confirmed: every op calls create_buffer/create_empty_buffer fresh, and scalars (clip_range, vf_coef, ent_coef, num_steps, etc.) each become a separate device buffer (e.g. lines 663-665, 724-727); nothing is cached. The pattern is a real inefficiency. However the perf premise ('called per-minibatch per-epoch in a PPO update, allocation dominates') is NOT on any active hot path: grep confirms MetalContext is never invoked by any algorithm/bench/example, so the claimed 'medium' impact is hypothetical -> low. Fix (size-keyed buffer pool + encoder.set_bytes for scalars) is internal, produces identical results, and because every call blocks on wait_until_completed the reuse is safe; it does not change the public API.

title: Blocking CPU<->GPU sync per call plus mandatory triple host round-trip
location: commit()+wait_until_completed() at 494-495, 519-520, 566-567, 615-616, 689-690, 750-751, 783-784; API signatures take &[f32]/return Vec<f32> via read_buffer(444-448)
description: Each operation ends with command_buffer.commit() immediately followed by wait_until_completed() — a full pipeline flush + CPU stall — for kernels that are pure element-wise arithmetic. There is no batching of multiple kernels into one command buffer (gaussian_log_prob, gaussian_entropy and ppo_loss each commit+wait separately, so they cannot pipeline). Worse, the API consumes CPU &[f32] and returns Vec<f32>: to use it with Candle Metal tensors the caller must copy GPU->CPU (Tensor::to_vec), then this code copies CPU->GPU (create_buffer), then GPU->CPU again (read_buffer). That triple copy plus the blocking sync makes the GPU path slower than just doing the op in Candle on-device.
evidence: `command_buffer.commit(); command_buffer.wait_until_completed(); Ok(self.read_buffer(&output_buf, n))` (494-497). read_buffer does `slice::from_raw_parts(ptr,count).to_vec()` (446-447), a host copy on every call.
proposed_fix: Accept/return Candle Metal tensors (or raw MTLBuffer handles) to keep data resident on-device; batch dependent kernels into a single command buffer and avoid per-op wait_until_completed (let the caller synchronize once). Where a host result is unavoidable, return without an intermediate Vec copy.
bench_to_confirm: gpu_benchmark comparing on-device Candle PPO-loss vs this host-round-trip path for batch sizes ~2k-64k.
verify_reasoning: Code confirmed: every op ends with commit()+wait_until_completed() (e.g. 494-495), no batching of kernels, and the API consumes &[f32] and returns Vec<f32> via read_buffer (host copy, 444-448), forcing the GPU->CPU/CPU->GPU/GPU->CPU triple copy described. The description is accurate. But, as with finding 3, MetalContext is never called internally (grep-confirmed), so the per-call sync/host-roundtrip is not on an active hot path -> low, not medium. fix_is_safe=false: the proposed remedy (accept/return Candle Metal tensors or raw MTLBuffer handles, drop the intermediate Vec) changes the public method signatures (e.g. gaussian_log_prob/ppo_loss), which breaks the public API.

title: Metal GAE kernel ignores truncation — bootstraps incorrectly on time-limit cutoffs
description: The Metal GAE kernel receives only `dones` and computes mask = 1 - done, so any episode end (including time-limit truncation) zeros the bootstrap term gamma*next_value*mask. For truncation you must still bootstrap the value. The project's own canonical rollout buffer keeps a separate `truncated` flag and explicitly bootstraps on it (src/algorithms/rollout.rs:82-83 'When truncated=1.0, we still bootstrap the value'; compute_gae_scalar_with_truncation), so the Metal kernel is a correctness regression versus the CPU reference whenever environments are truncated by a time limit.
evidence: Kernel: `float mask = 1.0f - dones[idx]; float delta = rewards[idx] + gamma * next_value * mask - values[idx]; last_gae = delta + gamma * gae_lambda * mask * last_gae;` — no truncation input exists in the signature (rewards, values, dones, last_values only).
proposed_fix: Add a `truncated` buffer; use a termination-only mask for the value bootstrap (bootstrap when truncated) while still cutting the GAE recursion at episode boundaries, mirroring rollout.rs:compute_gae_scalar_with_truncation.
bench_to_confirm: env/PPO test with a time-limited env: compare Metal compute_gae vs rollout.rs truncation-aware GAE on a rollout containing a truncation.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: gae_compute takes only `dones` and uses mask=1-dones for BOTH the value bootstrap (gamma*next_value*mask) and the GAE trace (gamma*lambda*mask*last_gae), so it cannot bootstrap-on-truncation-but-reset-trace. The CPU reference rollout.rs explicitly keeps separate `terminated`/`truncated` flags and compute_gae_scalar_with_truncation bootstraps on truncation while resetting the trace at any episode boundary (lines 78-83, 300-343), fixing a 'value function collapse' bug. The Metal kernel is therefore a genuine regression versus the CPU reference for time-limited envs. Reduced to medium because the kernel is unreachable internally and lacks any truncation input. fix_is_safe=false: a correct fix must add a `truncated` buffer to the kernel AND a truncated parameter to the public compute_gae() signature, which is a breaking public-API change as written.

title: normalize_obs kernel hardcodes obs_dim via `% 1024` — wrong for any other obs_dim
description: normalize_obs indexes the per-feature mean/var with `id % 1024`, with the comment 'Assuming max obs_dim of 1024'. For a flattened batch of observations the feature index of element id is `id % obs_dim`, not `id % 1024`; the kernel only produces correct normalization when obs_dim == 1024. For any other obs_dim it reads the wrong mean/var entry. The kernel has no Rust wrapper, so it is currently unreachable even via the public API (latent), but it is a real bug if wired up.
evidence: `float std = sqrt(var[id % 1024] + EPSILON); float norm = (obs[id] - mean[id % 1024]) / std;` (lines 342-343).
proposed_fix: Pass obs_dim as a `constant uint&` and index with `id % obs_dim` (and bound-check id against batch*obs_dim).
verify_reasoning: Confirmed lines 342-343: `var[id % 1024]`, `mean[id % 1024]` with comment 'Assuming max obs_dim of 1024'. For a flattened batch the per-feature index is id % obs_dim, so this is only correct when obs_dim==1024; otherwise it reads the wrong mean/var entry. Real bug, but normalize_obs has a compiled pipeline yet NO public wrapper method, so it is currently unreachable via the API -> low. Fix (pass obs_dim as a constant, index id % obs_dim, bound-check) touches only the unused kernel, doesn't change any reachable behavior, and breaks nothing -> safe.

title: Combined PPO loss kernel and ppo_loss() entry point cannot apply value clipping
description: The standalone ppo_value_loss kernel supports clipped value loss, but ppo_combined_loss always computes unclipped `0.5*diff*diff`, and the public ppo_loss() wrapper takes no `old_values` argument, so value-function clipping is impossible through the main combined entry point. This is an inconsistency between the two value-loss paths; if the intent is SB3-style PPO with clip_range_vf, the combined path silently omits it. (Entropy/total-loss signs are correct: total = policy_loss + vf_coef*value_loss - ent_coef*entropy.)
evidence: Combined kernel: `float diff = values[id] - returns[id]; float value_loss = 0.5f * diff * diff;` (197-198) with no old_values/clip handling, while ppo_value_loss kernel (150-172) does `clamp(value - old_value, -clip_range, clip_range)` and takes max(loss1, loss2).
proposed_fix: Either document that the combined kernel is unclipped-value-only, or add old_values + use_clipping params to ppo_combined_loss and thread old_values through ppo_loss().
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: ppo_combined_loss computes unclipped 0.5*diff*diff (197-198) and ppo_loss() (638-648) takes no old_values, while the standalone ppo_value_loss kernel does support clipped value loss (150-172). The inconsistency between the two value-loss paths is real, and the finding correctly notes total-loss signs are right (policy + vf_coef*value - ent_coef*entropy). It is a limitation, not a math error (unclipped value loss is SB3's default since clip_range_vf defaults to None), hence low. fix_is_safe=true because the finding offers a documentation-only option (mark combined path unclipped-value-only), which changes nothing and is correctness-preserving.

title: gaussian_log_prob applies EPSILON to std but not to the 2*log_std normalization term
description: The kernel regularizes the z-score denominator as `(std + EPSILON)` but the normalization constant uses the raw `2.0f*log_std`. The regularization is numerically inconsistent: as log_std -> -inf (std -> 0) the z term is bounded by EPSILON but the `2*log_std` term still -> -inf, so the supposed numerical guard does not actually prevent a blow-up; meanwhile for normal-magnitude std the +EPSILON (1e-8) introduces a tiny systematic bias vs the exact log-prob. Minor, but the eps is neither necessary for typical inputs nor sufficient for the degenerate one.
evidence: `float std = exp(log_std); float z = (action - mean) / (std + EPSILON); log_probs[id] = -0.5f * (LOG_2PI + 2.0f * log_std + z * z);` (103-106).
proposed_fix: Drop the EPSILON in the denominator (rely on clamping log_std to a min like -20 upstream as DiagGaussian does), so z and the normalization term use the same std consistently.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed lines 103-106: z uses (std + EPSILON) but the normalization term uses raw 2*log_std. The regularization is inconsistent — for std->0 the 2*log_std term still diverges so EPSILON doesn't prevent blow-up, and for normal std the 1e-8 introduces a tiny systematic bias versus the exact log-prob. Real but trivial; finding's own 'low' is correct. Fix (drop EPSILON, rely on upstream log_std clamping as DiagGaussian does) makes the kernel match the exact reference formula, changes results only by ~1e-8 toward more-correct, and doesn't change the public API -> safe.

title: softmax_row kernel runs fully serial on thread 0 with three no-op barriers
description: Despite launching a full threadgroup and the comment about parallel reduction, every phase (max, exp+sum, normalize) is gated on `local_id == 0`, so all work is serial O(num_classes) on a single lane while the rest of the threadgroup idles. The three threadgroup_barrier(mem_device) calls synchronize threads that do nothing, adding overhead with no benefit. Also uses mem_flags::mem_device for a threadgroup-local synchronization. The kernel has no Rust wrapper and is currently unreachable (latent).
evidence: `if (local_id == 0) { float m = input[offset]; for (...) m = max(...); max_val[batch_id] = m; } threadgroup_barrier(mem_flags::mem_device); if (local_id == 0) { ... }` (225-256).
proposed_fix: Either dispatch one thread per row (drop the barriers and the threadgroup) or implement a genuine threadgroup parallel reduction over num_classes using threadgroup memory.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed lines 212-256: all three phases gated on local_id==0 (serial O(num_classes) on one lane), with three threadgroup_barrier(mem_flags::mem_device) synchronizing idle threads, and mem_device used where mem_threadgroup is appropriate. Real inefficiency, and the kernel has no public wrapper so it is unreachable -> low. Fix (one thread per row, or a real threadgroup reduction) is internal to the unused kernel and produces the same numerical softmax, so it is safe.

title: create_buffer never checks for nil allocation; BufferCreationFailed variant is dead
description: Both buffer constructors wrap the metal-rs return value unconditionally in Ok(...) and never check whether the underlying MTLDevice returned a nil buffer (Metal returns nil on allocation failure, e.g. zero-length data or OOM). Consequently the BufferCreationFailed error variant is never constructed anywhere, and an allocation failure would surface later as a null-pointer dereference in read_buffer/contents rather than a clean error. Also note an empty input slice produces a size-0 buffer with no validation.
evidence: `let buffer = self.device.new_buffer_with_data(...); Ok(buffer)` (427-432) — no nil check; grep shows MetalError::BufferCreationFailed has no construction site.
proposed_fix: Check the returned buffer length/handle and return Err(MetalError::BufferCreationFailed) on nil or when data.is_empty(); this also removes the dead-variant warning.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: create_buffer/create_empty_buffer (425-441) wrap the return value unconditionally in Ok with no nil/length check, and grep confirms MetalError::BufferCreationFailed is only defined (line 54), never constructed — a dead variant. An allocation failure or empty-slice (size-0 buffer) would surface later in read_buffer/contents rather than as a clean error. Real cleanup-level issue -> low. Fix (validate buffer handle/length, reject empty data, return BufferCreationFailed) leaves the happy path unchanged and only adds error handling -> safe.

title: Entire MetalContext GPU path is public-only and never exercised internally
description: MetalContext (and all its kernels) is only re-exported from simd/mod.rs and is not called by any algorithm, bench, or example (grep over src/benches/examples finds zero call sites besides the re-export). The kernels gaussian_entropy/argmax/compute_gae/ppo_* have wrappers but no internal user; softmax_row/normalize_obs/update_running_stats have no wrapper at all. The tests are gated on (test, target_os=macos, feature=metal) so they only run on Metal hardware with the feature enabled, meaning none of the OOB/correctness issues above are caught in default CI. This is dead-but-public surface: its perf claims are unverified and its latent bugs ship in the public API.

proposed_fix: Either wire MetalContext into the PPO/GAE paths behind the metal feature (and add a non-hardware-gated correctness test that runs the kernels via a CI Metal runner), or mark the module clearly experimental/doc(hidden) until the OOB and obs_dim bugs are fixed.

This is dead-but-public surface whose perf claims are unverified and latent bugs ship in the public API — accurately described -> low (packaging). Fix (doc(hidden)/mark experimental, or wire into the metal path with a Metal-runner test) is non-breaking; doc(hidden) keeps the API usable while hiding it -> safe.

title: Reward double-counts position P&L: unrealized_pnl added on top of marked-to-market position (every step)
location: portfolio_value() L797-801; reward block L1198-1217 (esp. L1205-1217)
description: The per-step reward adds the position's full lifetime unrealized PnL on top of the marked position value, every single step. portfolio_value() = balance + position*price*IB + unrealized_pnl, but portfolio_before (L1214) is hand-built WITHOUT the unrealized_pnl term while portfolio_after = portfolio_value() (L1216) includes it. Working through the algebra (unrealized_pnl = (new_price-entry)*|pos|*IB*sign(pos) = (new_price-entry)*pos*IB), the realized reward becomes reward = pos*(new_price-price_before) + pos*(new_price-entry). The correct mark-to-market step return is only the first term; the second term re-adds the entire gain-from-entry on EVERY timestep. This is path-dependent, accumulates, and strongly biases the agent toward holding winners indefinitely (it is rewarded repeatedly for the same unrealized gain). Root cause: balance is never debited for the position notional (execute_order L946 only deducts commission), so position*price*IB is pure exposure and adding unrealized_pnl is the actual double-count. The sibling MultiAssetEnv does this correctly: it debits cash on each trade (multi_asset.rs L649) and computes total_value = cash + Sum(pos*price) with unrealized_pnl computed but NEVER added (multi_asset.rs L97).
evidence: L1214-1217: `let portfolio_before = self.balance + self.position_state.position * price_before * self.config.initial_balance; let portfolio_after = self.portfolio_value(); let reward = (portfolio_after - portfolio_before) / self.config.initial_balance;` where portfolio_value() (L800) returns `self.balance + position_value + self.position_state.unrealized_pnl`. Contrast multi_asset.rs L97 `self.total_value = self.cash + total_position_value;` (no unrealized_pnl) with cash debited at L649.
proposed_fix: Make the reward symmetric and avoid double counting: either drop the `+ self.position_state.unrealized_pnl` term from portfolio_value() (since position*price*IB already marks the position) so reward = pos*(new_price-price_before)*IB, OR properly debit cash for the notional on entry (balance -= fill_qty*execution_price) and value the position purely at market without the separate pnl add — matching MultiAssetEnv. Compute portfolio_before via the same function used for portfolio_after.
bench_to_confirm: Micro-test: hold a fixed position across N steps on a monotonically rising price series and assert cumulative reward equals total mark-to-market change, not ~2x or an entry-relative accumulation.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed against code. portfolio_value() (env.rs L797-801) = balance + position*price*IB + unrealized_pnl, while portfolio_before (L1214-1215) is hand-built WITHOUT the unrealized_pnl term. execute_order (L946) only deducts commission, never debits balance for the notional, so position*price*IB is pure exposure and the separate unrealized_pnl add is a true double count. Algebra holds: with unrealized_pnl=(p1-entry)*|pos|*IB*sign(pos)=(p1-entry)*pos*IB, reward = pos*(p1-p0) + pos*(p1-entry). The second term re-adds the full lifetime gain-from-entry every step, biasing the policy toward holding winners. The sibling MultiAssetEnv is verified correct: cash debited at multi_asset.rs L649, total_value=cash+position_value at L97 with unrealized_pnl computed but never added. fix_is_safe=true: portfolio_value() is private (no API break), and because portfolio_before reads the POST-execution position, round-trip rewards telescope correctly with the drop-term fix (entry q*(p1-p0), holds telescope, close=0, summing to the realized gain); the cash-debit variant also works. The residual total_return/balance gap (realized PnL never credited to balance) is pre-existing and unchanged by the fix.

title: Reward is exactly 2x the true step return (unrealized_pnl delta double-counts the marked position move)
location: portfolio_value() L511-517; step() value_before L688, value_after L724, reward L725
description: Both value_before (L688) and value_after (L724) call portfolio_value() = balance + position*price*IB + unrealized_pnl. Between them, unrealized_pnl is recomputed for the new price (L715). For a held position with no trade, value_after - value_before = pos*IB*(price_{t+1}-price_t) [position reprice] + (upnl_new - upnl_old) [= pos*IB*(price_{t+1}-price_t)], i.e. exactly 2*pos*IB*delta_price. The marked-position term and the unrealized_pnl term capture the SAME price move, so the reward is twice the true mark-to-market return. Unlike the env.rs case this is a bounded constant 2x factor (RL can largely absorb a uniform reward scaling), but it is still an incorrect accounting model. MultiAssetEnv (multi_asset.rs L97) shows the correct pattern: do not add unrealized_pnl to a value that already marks the position at current price.
evidence: L513-517 `self.balance + self.position_state.position * price * self.config.initial_balance + self.position_state.unrealized_pnl`; L715-718 sets unrealized_pnl = (new_price-entry)*|pos|*IB*dir; L725 `let reward = (value_after - value_before) / self.config.initial_balance;`
proposed_fix: Remove the `+ self.position_state.unrealized_pnl` term from portfolio_value() (the position is already valued at market via position*price*IB), or adopt the cash-debit model from MultiAssetEnv. Either makes the step reward equal the single mark-to-market change.
bench_to_confirm: Same micro-test as the env.rs finding; assert reward == pos*(price_{t+1}-price_t)*IB/IB rather than 2x.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed. multi_timeframe.rs portfolio_value() (L511-517) = balance + position*price*IB + unrealized_pnl. value_before (L688) is computed via portfolio_value() with the OLD unrealized_pnl; value_after (L724) after unrealized_pnl is recomputed for the new price (L715-718). For a held position with no trade, value_after-value_before = pos*IB*(p1-p0) [reprice] + pos*IB*(p1-p0) [unrealized delta] = 2*pos*IB*(p1-p0), so reward is exactly 2x the true mark-to-market return on held/no-trade steps. 'Exactly 2x' holds for the held-position case; on trade steps the structure differs and commission appears once (not doubled), so the distortion also alters the relative weight of P&L vs costs (not a pure uniform scale) — supporting medium. Fix (remove +unrealized_pnl) makes reward=pos*(p1-p0); portfolio_value() is private, so fix_is_safe=true.

title: current_prices()/current_volatilities() allocate a fresh Vec on every call, multiple times per step
location: current_prices() L585-588, current_volatilities() L591-594; callers: execute_rebalance L598, step L816-817, build_observation L698
description: current_prices() and current_volatilities() each allocate and return a new Vec<f32> via .map().collect(). Within a single step() these are invoked at least 3 times (execute_rebalance L598 -> current_prices; step L816 new_prices, L817 new_vols; build_observation L698 current_prices), each allocating num_assets-length heap vectors that are immediately discarded. This is per-step heap churn in the hot loop that scales with num_assets and step count.
evidence: L587 `self.data.assets.iter().map(|a| a.prices[idx][3]).collect()` and L593 `...prices[idx][7]).collect()`; called again at L698, L816, L817.
proposed_fix: Compute prices/vols once at the top of step() into a reusable buffer (or a struct-owned scratch Vec cleared each step) and pass slices to execute_rebalance/update_values/build_observation, avoiding repeated allocation and repeated index math.
bench_to_confirm: env_benchmark with MultiAssetEnv (large num_assets, long episode); compare allocations/step before vs after.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed. current_prices() (L585-588) and current_volatilities() (L591-594) each allocate a new Vec<f32> via .map().collect(). Within step() (the hot path): execute_rebalance calls current_prices() (L598), step calls current_prices() (L816) and current_volatilities() (L817), and build_observation calls current_prices() (L698) — at least 3 current_prices allocations plus 1 current_volatilities per step, each num_assets-length and immediately discarded. Genuinely on the per-step hot loop. Low severity is appropriate (small Vecs). Proposed fix (compute once into a reusable buffer / struct-owned scratch) is safe; note new_prices is read after the current_step increment so it needs a separate compute from the pre-increment prices, but the redundant ones (execute_rebalance, build_observation) can reuse cached slices.

title: regime_persistence has inverted semantics: counts steps toward a change and resets while regime is stable
location: update() persistence logic L426-435; exposed as regime_persistence L448 and feature L138-139; doc L128-129
description: `persistence` is documented/exposed as 'how long in current regime' (RegimeObservation.regime_persistence, L128-129, fed to the observation feature vector at L138-139 as persistence/100). But the update logic does the opposite: when a DIFFERENT regime is detected it increments persistence (L428) and switches once it crosses min_persistence; when the SAME (current) regime is detected it resets persistence to 0 (L434). So while the market stays in one regime, persistence is pinned at 0 — the exact opposite of 'time in regime'. The observation feature is therefore ~0 throughout any stable regime and only briefly nonzero during a pending switch. Additionally the increment does not require the same candidate regime to persist: it accumulates across different non-current regimes, so the eventual switch can be to whichever regime happened to be detected on the threshold step.
evidence: L427-435: `if new_regime != self.current_regime { self.persistence += 1; if self.persistence >= self.config.min_persistence { self.current_regime = new_regime; self.persistence = 0; } } else { self.persistence = 0; }` then L448 `regime_persistence: self.persistence`. Doc L129 says 'how long in current regime'.
proposed_fix: Track two counters: a stability counter incremented each step the regime is unchanged (this is the true 'time in regime' to expose), and a separate change-candidate counter that only accumulates when the SAME new regime is detected consecutively (reset if the candidate changes) before committing the switch.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed. update() L427-435: when new_regime != current_regime it increments persistence and switches at min_persistence (resetting to 0); when SAME it resets persistence to 0. So persistence is pinned at 0 while a regime is stable — the exact opposite of the documented 'how long in current regime' (doc L128-129), and the observation feature persistence/100 (L139) is ~0 throughout any stable regime. Also confirmed: the increment accumulates across DIFFERENT non-current candidate regimes (no requirement that the same candidate persist), so the committed switch goes to whichever regime was detected on the threshold step — this corrupts current_regime (actual classification/dynamics), not just the exposed feature, justifying medium. Fix (separate stability counter for time-in-regime + a consistent-candidate change counter) is a behavior change to a private/internal counter and a pub field's semantics; type unchanged, no API break, fix_is_safe=true.

title: HMM forward step can underflow to all-zero and freeze the filtered state probabilities
description: emission_prob computes a raw Gaussian density exp(-0.5*z^2)/(std*sqrt(2pi)). The observation is the GARCH volatility (sqrt of variance). When volatility is far from all emission_means (e.g. a spike: obs=0.1, mean=0.005, std=0.002 gives z~47, exp(-1128)=0 in f32), every state's emission underflows to 0. Then in update_hmm new_probs are all 0, sum==0 (L477-478), and the code skips the assignment (L478 `if sum > 0.0`), leaving hmm_state_probs frozen at the previous value indefinitely. Also, emission_prob divides by std with no guard, so user-supplied emission_stds containing 0 yields inf/NaN that then propagate. Both are edge cases (defaults are safe) but degrade the regime filter silently under volatility spikes. update_hmm is on the per-step hot path (called from update()).
evidence: L205-206 `let z = (observation - mean) / std; (-0.5 * z * z).exp() / (std * (2.0 * std::f32::consts::PI).sqrt())`; L477-483 `let sum: f32 = new_probs.iter().sum(); if sum > 0.0 { ... self.hmm_state_probs = new_probs; }` (no else / no fallback).
proposed_fix: Compute emissions in log-space (log N) and subtract the max before exponentiating (log-sum-exp normalization), or add a small epsilon floor to each emission and to std (std.max(1e-6)) so the forward recursion never collapses to all-zeros.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed. emission_prob (L205-206) computes raw f32 Gaussian density exp(-0.5*z^2)/(std*sqrt(2pi)); for an observation far from all emission_means (a volatility spike) every state's emission underflows to 0 in f32 (exp beyond ~-88 underflows). In update_hmm, new_probs become all 0, sum==0, and the guard `if sum > 0.0` (L478) has no else branch, so hmm_state_probs stays frozen at the previous value. Verified defaults (means [0.005,0.015,0.035], stds [0.002,0.005,0.015]) keep the highest state nonzero for moderate spikes (e.g. obs=0.1) but collapse for larger spikes (obs>~0.27), so it is a real edge case, consistent with the finding. Separately, emission_prob divides by std with no guard, so user-supplied emission_stds containing 0 yields inf/NaN. update_hmm is on the per-step hot path (called from update() L416). Fix (log-sum-exp normalization or epsilon floor + std.max(1e-6)) is standard and only alters the degenerate already-broken case; fix_is_safe=true.

title: GarchParams::unconditional_variance lacks stationarity guard (alpha+beta>=1 -> negative/inf)
description: unconditional_variance returns omega/(1 - alpha - beta) with no check that alpha+beta<1. GarchParams::new accepts arbitrary alpha,beta with no validation, so a non-stationary parameterization (alpha+beta>=1) yields a negative variance or a divide-by-zero (inf). Confirmed by grep that the function is never called internally (not in the hot path) — it is a public-API robustness issue only. Defaults (alpha=0.1, beta=0.85 -> denom 0.05) are safe.
evidence: L244 `self.omega / (1.0 - self.alpha - self.beta)`; grep over src/ shows the only occurrence of `unconditional_variance` is its definition (no internal callers).
proposed_fix: Validate in GarchParams::new that alpha>=0, beta>=0, alpha+beta<1 (return Result or clamp), and in unconditional_variance guard the denominator: `let d = 1.0 - alpha - beta; if d > eps { omega/d } else { f32::INFINITY or omega/eps }`.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed. unconditional_variance (L243-245) returns omega/(1-alpha-beta) with no stationarity check, and GarchParams::new (L233) accepts arbitrary alpha,beta with no validation, so alpha+beta>=1 yields negative variance or inf. grep confirms the only occurrence of unconditional_variance in src/ is its own definition — no internal callers, so this is purely a public-API robustness issue (defaults alpha=0.1,beta=0.85 are safe), matching the low rating. fix_is_safe=true scoped to the safe variants: guarding the denominator in unconditional_variance, or clamping in new(), is non-breaking. Note the finding's alternative 'return Result from new()' WOULD break the public signature (currently returns Self), so that specific variant should be avoided; the denominator-guard/clamp path is safe.

title: build_observation pads via obs.insert(0, 0.0) (O(n) front shift); multi_timeframe pad condition cannot pad per-timeframe
location: multi_timeframe.rs L616-621; cf. env.rs L995-998, multi_asset.rs L723-728
description: All three trading envs pad insufficient-history observations using obs.insert(0, 0.0) in a while loop, which shifts the entire (growing) observation vector right on each insert — O(n) per element, O(n^2) if many pads were needed. I verified these loops are effectively DEAD under the constructors' length validation (env.rs forces exactly `lookback` rows; multi_timeframe's min_start_idx guarantees current_tf_idx>=lookback), so this is latent, not a live hot-path cost. The more concrete issue is in multi_timeframe.rs: the pad guard `while obs.len() < expected_len_for_tf` compares the CUMULATIVE obs length (all prior timeframes already pushed) against a single timeframe's expected length, so for the 2nd+ timeframe it can never pad even when that timeframe genuinely lacks bars, and for the 1st timeframe an insert(0,..) would prepend zeros before ALL data rather than within the timeframe block. If the constructor guard were ever relaxed, this produces a wrong-length / misordered observation instead of correct padding.
evidence: multi_timeframe.rs L617-621 `let features_per_bar = 5 + ...; let expected_len_for_tf = lookback * features_per_bar; while obs.len() < expected_len_for_tf { obs.insert(0, 0.0); }` — obs is accumulated across all timeframes before this point (L593-614).
proposed_fix: Track each timeframe's block length locally and pad by extending at the correct block offset (or pre-zero a fixed-size obs buffer and overwrite slices). Replace insert(0,..) with computing the needed count and using splice/resize-at-offset; never front-insert into a cumulative buffer.
verify_reasoning: Code structure confirmed, but the perf framing is refuted and must not be asserted as a live cost. The obs.insert(0,0.0) front-shift loops are DEAD under the constructor invariants (env.rs forces exactly lookback rows; multi_timeframe min_start_idx guarantees current_tf_idx>=lookback so the inner range yields exactly lookback bars and per-bar padding at L606-613 already handles None), so the claimed O(n^2) front-insert is NOT on a live hot path. The verdict rests solely on the latent correctness defect: the multi_timeframe pad guard (L616-621) compares the CUMULATIVE obs.len() (all prior timeframes already pushed) against a single timeframe's expected_len_for_tf, so for the 2nd+ timeframe it can never pad, and insert(0,..) for the 1st would prepend before all data — verified real in the code and would produce wrong-length/misordered observations if the constructor invariant were ever relaxed. Latent, hence low. Fix (track per-tf block length, pad at the correct offset, avoid front-insert) is a safe improvement; fix_is_safe=true.

title: metrics/trading.rs: std-dev / downside-dev take sqrt of a possibly-negative variance (NaN poisoning)
location: return_std() L349-352, return_variance() L339-347, downside_deviation() L355-361
description: return_std() = return_variance().sqrt() with NO non-negativity clamp. return_variance() uses the one-pass form (returns_sq_sum - n*mean^2)/(n-1) = (returns_sq_sum - returns_sum^2/n)/(n-1), and both returns_sq_sum and returns_sum are maintained by additive subtraction across the rolling window (L221-239). By Cauchy-Schwarz the numerator is >=0 only in exact arithmetic; with subtraction-maintained running sums and near-constant returns (true variance ~0) floating-point catastrophic cancellation can make it slightly negative, so sqrt() yields NaN. The same unguarded pattern is in downside_deviation() L357 ((downside_returns_sq_sum/(n-1)).sqrt()). A single NaN propagates into sharpe_ratio, sortino_ratio, annualized_volatility and var_parametric, and into TradingMetrics. The sibling module proves the fix is expected: src/risk/rewards.rs clamps at the analogous spots (L117 self.variance().max(0.0).sqrt(); L126 (self.sum_neg_sq/n).max(0.0).sqrt()).
evidence: trading.rs L350-352: `fn return_std(&self) -> f64 { self.return_variance().sqrt() }` (no clamp) vs rewards.rs L117: `self.variance().max(0.0).sqrt()`. trading.rs L357: `(self.downside_returns_sq_sum / (self.n_returns - 1) as f64).sqrt()` vs rewards.rs L126: `(self.sum_neg_sq / n).max(0.0).sqrt()`.
proposed_fix: Clamp before sqrt: `self.return_variance().max(0.0).sqrt()` and `(self.downside_returns_sq_sum/(n-1) as f64).max(0.0).sqrt()`. Optionally switch to Welford for the rolling sums to bound drift.
bench_to_confirm: micro-bench: feed ~1e5 near-identical returns (e.g. 0.001 repeated) through MetricsCalculator with rolling_window set, then assert sharpe_ratio()/annualized_volatility() are finite
verify_reasoning: Confirmed in code. return_std() (L350-352) = return_variance().sqrt() with no clamp; return_variance() (L339-347) uses the one-pass form (returns_sq_sum - n*mean^2)/(n-1), classic catastrophic cancellation that can go slightly negative for near-constant returns. downside_deviation() (L355-361) sqrts downside_returns_sq_sum/(n-1) with no clamp; that running sum is maintained by additive subtraction on eviction (L222-228), so after all in-window negatives are evicted it can drift to a tiny negative, also reachable. I empirically verified (-1e-18).sqrt()==NaN and that NaN==0.0 is false, so the sharpe_ratio std_ret==0.0 guard (L372) does NOT catch a NaN std — excess_return/NaN propagates into sharpe, sortino, annualized_volatility, var_parametric and the whole TradingMetrics snapshot. The sibling rewards.rs uses .max(0.0).sqrt() at L117/L126, proving the guard is the intended behavior. Fix .max(0.0).sqrt() only changes the (mathematically should-be-zero) negative-variance case to 0 — no API change, no change to any correct result. High is justified by the broad downstream NaN contamination of public, exported metrics.

title: metrics/trading.rs: recalculate_drawdown() is O(window) on every add_return in rolling mode, defeating the streaming design
description: The struct is documented as an online/streaming calculator ('avoiding the need to store and reprocess all historical data', L162-165). All scalar sums (returns_sum, returns_sq_sum, gross_profit, ...) are maintained incrementally, but once the rolling window fills, every single add_return() call triggers recalculate_drawdown(), which iterates the entire equity_curve (O(window)) to rebuild peak_equity/max_drawdown/drawdown_squared_sum from scratch. So per-step cost is O(window) instead of O(1) (e.g. 252 passes/step for a 1-year daily window). This is the hot path when streaming windowed metrics during training/backtest.
evidence: L249-253: `if self.config.rolling_window > 0 && self.equity_curve.len() >= self.config.rolling_window { self.equity_curve.pop_front(); self.recalculate_drawdown(); }` and L309 `for &equity in &self.equity_curve { ... }` (full re-scan each call).
proposed_fix: Maintain rolling drawdown incrementally (e.g. monotonic deque for the running peak, and subtract the popped point's dd_pct^2 from drawdown_squared_sum on eviction) so add_return stays O(1) amortized; only fall back to full recompute when the running peak is evicted.
bench_to_confirm: micro-bench add_return throughput with rolling_window=252 vs rolling_window=0 over 1e6 steps
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: once rolling_window>0 and the equity_curve is full, every add_return() (L249-253) calls recalculate_drawdown() which does a full O(window) rescan (L309 loop). All scalar sums are otherwise maintained incrementally and the struct is documented as online/streaming (L162-165), so this is a genuine O(window)-per-step regression on the documented hot path — but only when a user opts into rolling_window>0 (default is 0/cumulative, where this never fires). fix_is_safe=false: the proposed incremental scheme ('subtract the popped point's dd_pct^2') has a subtle side effect on drawdown_squared_sum (Ulcer index). recalculate_drawdown computes each point's dd_pct relative to the running peak from the window front; the evicted front always contributes dd_pct=0, and the new front's dd_pct must be recomputed against a new running peak. A naive subtract-on-eviction would diverge from the full recompute, changing the Ulcer index. A correct incremental version is possible but is not the mechanism proposed.

title: metrics/trading.rs: var_historical/cvar clone+sort O(n log n) per call; IR/Treynor allocate per call
location: var_historical() L531-542, cvar() L565-582, information_ratio() L598-610, treynor_ratio() L633-645
description: var_historical() and cvar() each allocate a fresh Vec<f64> from the returns deque and full-sort it (O(n log n)) on every invocation; compute_metrics() (L664-697) calls both, so a single snapshot sorts the return history twice and allocates twice. information_ratio() and treynor_ratio() also allocate a tracking_errors Vec each call. For a calculator advertised as streaming and likely snapshotted frequently, this is the dominant per-snapshot cost. No incremental order statistic is kept.
evidence: L536-537: `let mut sorted_returns: Vec<f64> = self.returns.iter().copied().collect(); sorted_returns.sort_by(...)`; duplicated at L570-571 in cvar(); L598-603 builds `tracking_errors: Vec<f64>` each call.
proposed_fix: Compute VaR and CVaR together from one sort (select_nth_unstable for the cutoff index instead of full sort), or maintain an order-statistic structure; reuse a scratch buffer. For IR/Treynor, fold the covariance/tracking-error sums in a single pass without the intermediate Vec.
bench_to_confirm: micro-bench compute_metrics() call cost at n=1000 returns, before/after single-sort refactor
verify_reasoning: The dominant claim is accurate: var_historical() (L536-537) and cvar() (L570-571) each clone the returns deque into a fresh Vec and full-sort O(n log n), and compute_metrics() (L680,L682) calls both, sorting the same data twice per snapshot; in the default cumulative mode the deque grows unbounded so n is the full history. information_ratio() (L598-603) does allocate a tracking_errors Vec each call. However the finding's prose overstates by lumping in Treynor: treynor_ratio() (L633-645) computes covariance and bench_var with .map(...).sum::<f64>() directly — no collect(), no tracking_errors Vec — so that sub-claim is inaccurate. is_real stays true on the var/cvar double-sort and IR Vec. Severity downgraded to low: compute_metrics is a periodic snapshot, not a per-step hot path, so the cost is amortized. fix (single partial sort via select_nth_unstable shared between VaR cutoff and CVaR tail-sum, scratch reuse, single-pass folds for IR) preserves the computed values and the public API.

title: rewards.rs: downside deviation divides squared-negatives by count-of-negatives instead of total N (Sortino inconsistency)
location: RollingStats::downside_deviation() L121-127, used by compute_sortino() L350-366
description: downside_deviation() = sqrt(sum_neg_sq / n_neg) where n_neg = count of strictly-negative values. This is the RMS of negative returns, not the target semivariance used in the standard Sortino ratio, which divides by the TOTAL number of periods N: DD = sqrt((1/N) * sum_{r<target} (r-target)^2). Dividing by the smaller n_neg overstates downside deviation by a factor sqrt(N/n_neg) and therefore understates the reported Sortino ratio (e.g. 40% of periods negative => DD overstated ~1.58x). The sibling module disagrees: src/metrics/trading.rs L355-361 divides downside_returns_sq_sum by (n_returns-1) (~ standard target semivariance). Two modules, two conventions; the rewards.rs one deviates from the common definition.
evidence: rewards.rs L122-126: `let n = self.values.iter().filter(|&&v| v < 0.0).count() as f64; ... (self.sum_neg_sq / n).max(0.0).sqrt()` vs trading.rs L357: `(self.downside_returns_sq_sum / (self.n_returns - 1) as f64).sqrt()`.
proposed_fix: Divide by total window count (self.values.len()) rather than the negative count to match the standard Sortino downside deviation, and align the two modules on one convention.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: downside_deviation() (L121-127) divides sum_neg_sq by n = count of strictly-negative values, i.e. the RMS of negatives, not the standard Sortino target downside deviation which divides the sum of squared below-target shortfalls by total N. This is the well-known 'divide by downside count' error and produces a systematic overstatement of DD by ~sqrt(N/n_neg), understating the Sortino used to shape rewards (compute_sortino L350-366). The sibling trading.rs divides by (n_returns-1), the standard convention, so the cross-module inconsistency is real. Severity reduced to low: it lives in a reward shaper (affects shaping magnitude, not a reported performance metric) and the code documents its choice as 'std dev of negative values only' (L120); both conventions appear in practice. fix (divide by self.values.len()) changes the shaped-reward magnitude as intended, no API break.

title: metrics/trading.rs: powf on possibly-negative (1+total_return) yields NaN for returns below -100%
description: annualized_return and calmar use (1.0 + total_return).powf(periods_per_year / n_returns). total_return() = final_equity - 1.0 where final_equity is the running product of (1.0 + ret) (L242-255). add_return accepts arbitrary f64, so a single ret < -1.0 (a below-(-100%) leveraged loss) drives the cumulative equity product negative, making (1.0 + total_return) < 0; powf with a fractional exponent then returns NaN, which propagates into annualized_return, calmar_ratio and the TradingMetrics snapshot. Same NaN class as the unclamped sqrt finding but via a different path.
evidence: L409: `(1.0 + total_return).powf(self.config.periods_per_year / self.n_returns as f64) - 1.0`; total_return from L426-430 `final_equity - 1.0`; equity built at L242-245 `... * (1.0 + ret)` with no guard on ret <= -1.0.
proposed_fix: Guard ret > -1.0 on input (or clamp), and short-circuit annualization when (1.0 + total_return) <= 0 (return a sentinel / saturate) instead of calling powf on a negative base.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: annualized_return() (L440) and calmar_ratio() (L409) compute (1.0+total_return).powf(fractional). total_return() = final_equity-1.0 (L427), final_equity is the cumulative product of (1.0+ret) (L242-245) with no guard on ret<=-1.0, and add_return accepts arbitrary f64. A net-negative number of sub-(-100%) returns makes final_equity<0, so (1+total_return)<0; I empirically verified (-0.5).powf(0.1)==NaN, which then propagates into annualized_return, calmar, and the snapshot. Edge case (requires a below-(-100%) leveraged/PnL-style return), hence low. fix_is_safe=true via the safe variant: short-circuit annualization to a sentinel when (1.0+total_return)<=0 instead of calling powf — replacing a NaN with a defined value changes no correct result and no API. (The alternative input-clamp variant would alter the equity curve and is riskier, but the short-circuit path is safe.)

title: metrics/trading.rs: recovery_factor/calmar use full-history total_return numerator over a window-only max drawdown in rolling mode
location: total_return() L424-431, recovery_factor() L506-519, calmar_ratio() L401-422
description: In rolling-window mode the equity_curve is a cumulative product from t=0 that is never renormalized when old points are popped (L249-255), so total_return() = final_equity - 1.0 reflects the entire history, not the window. recalculate_drawdown() (L303-327) however rebuilds peak_equity/max_drawdown over the window only. recovery_factor() = total_return / max_dd_pct and calmar_ratio() thus divide a full-history return by a window-scoped drawdown ratio, mixing two horizons. (The drawdown ratios themselves are scale-invariant and fine; the defect is the horizon mismatch in the numerator.)
evidence: L426-430 returns `final_equity - 1.0` (full cumulative product) while L508-511 computes `max_dd_pct = self.max_drawdown / self.peak_equity` from the window-recomputed peak; combined at L515 `self.total_return() / max_dd_pct`.
proposed_fix: In rolling mode compute total_return relative to the window's starting equity (front of equity_curve) so numerator and drawdown share the same horizon, or document that these ratios are cumulative-only.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: in rolling mode equity_curve holds absolute cumulative levels from t=0 (L242-245) and is never renormalized when the front is popped (L250), so total_return() (L426-430) = back-1.0 reflects the entire history, while recalculate_drawdown() (L303-327) rebuilds peak_equity/max_drawdown over the window only; recovery_factor() (L515) and calmar_ratio() then divide a full-history return by a window-scoped drawdown ratio — a genuine horizon mismatch (calmar additionally annualizes a full-history return over the window-length n_returns). Low: rolling-mode-only, semantic rather than crash. fix_is_safe=false: the proposed fix (compute total_return relative to the window's front equity) would have to change the public total_return() method, which also feeds TradingMetrics.total_return (L690) and may be intentionally full-history; that blast radius could change a result other callers rely on. A window-local helper used only by recovery/calmar would be safe, but that is not what is proposed.

title: rewards.rs: RollingStats::variance() is biased (divides by n) while metrics uses unbiased (n-1)
description: variance() = (sum_sq/n) - mean^2 is the biased (population, /n) estimator, exposed as public API and used for the Sharpe shaping/metric. src/metrics/trading.rs return_variance() (L339-347) uses the unbiased (/n-1) estimator. The two modules report different Sharpe/volatility for the same return stream. Minor (convention), but it is an undocumented inconsistency across the public API.
evidence: rewards.rs L111-112: `let mean = self.sum / n; (self.sum_sq / n) - (mean * mean)` (/n) vs trading.rs L342-343: `(self.returns_sq_sum - self.n_returns as f64 * mean * mean) / (self.n_returns - 1) as f64` (/n-1).
proposed_fix: Pick one convention (typically unbiased /n-1 for Sharpe sample std) and apply it consistently across both modules, or document the difference.
verify_reasoning: Factually confirmed: rewards.rs variance() (L106-113) = (sum_sq/n)-mean^2 is the population/biased estimator (/n), while trading.rs return_variance() (L342-343) uses the unbiased (/n-1) estimator, so the two modules report slightly different std/Sharpe for the same stream. Marked is_real=true as the cross-module inconsistency in the public API is genuine, but it is the weakest finding: both /n and /(n-1) are legitimate estimators, neither is wrong, and for a 252 window the gap is ~0.4%; it borders on convention-nit (invalid) rather than a defect. The reward-shaper choice is internally self-consistent. fix (standardize on /(n-1), or document) is a minor value change with no API break.

title: rewards.rs: downside_deviation() re-scans the whole deque each call despite maintaining sum_neg_sq
description: sum_neg_sq is maintained incrementally in push() (L91-93, L81-83), but downside_deviation() still iterates the entire VecDeque every call just to count the negatives (n). Since compute_sortino() / shape_reward() call this per step, it is O(window) per step that could be O(1) by also tracking a running negative-count alongside sum_neg_sq.
evidence: L122: `let n = self.values.iter().filter(|&&v| v < 0.0).count() as f64;` — full O(window) scan each call.
proposed_fix: Maintain a `neg_count: usize` incrementally in push()/eviction (mirroring sum_neg_sq) and use it directly, making downside_deviation O(1).
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: sum_neg_sq is maintained incrementally in push()/eviction (L81-83,L91-93) but downside_deviation() (L122) recounts negatives with a full O(window) scan (self.values.iter().filter(...).count()), and SortinoRewardShaper::shape_reward (L370-390) calls it twice per step (once via compute_sortino, once directly), so it is O(window) per env step where O(1) is achievable. Low: the per-step reward-shaping path is called frequently but is cheap relative to network forward/backward, so the absolute cost is small. fix_is_safe=true: tracking a neg_count usize incrementally alongside sum_neg_sq is a clean mirror with identical results and no API change.

title: metrics/trading.rs: profit_factor returns f64::INFINITY, which serde_json serializes to null on the Serialize-derived TradingMetrics
description: profit_factor() returns f64::INFINITY when gross_loss == 0 and gross_profit > 0. This value flows into TradingMetrics.profit_factor (L678). TradingMetrics derives Serialize/Deserialize (L79); serde_json cannot represent Infinity and emits null, and a subsequent Deserialize of null into f64 fails — silent data loss / round-trip break for the JSON export the metrics module advertises.
evidence: L462-464: `} else if self.gross_profit > 0.0 { f64::INFINITY }`; struct attr L79 `#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)]`.
proposed_fix: Return a large finite sentinel (or Option<f64>/None) for the no-losses case, or serialize with a custom serializer that maps non-finite to null/0 consistently and tolerates it on deserialize.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed: profit_factor() (L462-464) returns f64::INFINITY when gross_loss==0 && gross_profit>0, flowing into TradingMetrics.profit_factor (L678); TradingMetrics derives Serialize/Deserialize (L79). serde_json serializes non-finite floats as null and rejects null when deserializing into f64, so a round-trip breaks. This is a latent issue: a grep shows only TradeEntry has an actual JSON export call site (journal.rs L422); there is no current TradingMetrics JSON serialization path, so the 'JSON export the metrics module advertises' framing is overstated — the defect is the derive + INFINITY combination, which justifies low. fix_is_safe=true via the non-breaking variant: a custom serializer mapping non-finite to null and tolerating it on deserialize (or a large finite sentinel) keeps the API; the Option<f64> variant the finding also lists would break the API, so only the safe variant should be applied.

title: Crate is not built as a Python extension module (no cdylib / extension-module / abi3)
location: lines 1-25, 66-67 (no [lib] section; pyo3 optional under gym/wandb)
description: The crate has no `[lib] crate-type` section, so it compiles only as an rlib. A maturin/uv wheel requires a `[lib]` with `crate-type = ["cdylib", "lib"]` and a `pyo3` dependency built with the `extension-module` feature (otherwise the wheel links libpython and is non-portable). Today pyo3 0.24.1 is `optional = true` gated behind the `gym`/`wandb` features only, with no `extension-module`/`abi3` and no `#[pymodule]` entry point anywhere in src/. This is the foundational packaging gap before any type can be exposed.
evidence: Cargo.toml line 67: `pyo3 = { version = "0.24.1", optional = true }`; features `gym = ["dep:pyo3"]`, `wandb = ["dep:pyo3"]`. grep for `[lib]`/`cdylib`/`pymodule` finds nothing. No `#[pymodule] fn ...` exists in the codebase.
proposed_fix: Add `[lib] name = "octane_rs"  crate-type = ["cdylib", "lib"]`, add a `python = ["dep:pyo3", "dep:numpy"]` feature with `pyo3 = { version="0.24", features=["extension-module", "abi3-py39"] }`, a `pyproject.toml` with `[build-system] requires=["maturin"]`, and a `#[pymodule]` registering the wrapper classes.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed against repo: Cargo.toml has no [lib] section/crate-type, pyo3 0.24.1 is optional under gym/wandb only with no extension-module/abi3, and grep finds no #[pymodule]/#[pyclass] in src/ and no pyproject.toml. The 'a wheel requires cdylib + extension-module' part is PyO3/maturin semantics (correct, not repo-verifiable). Without this no wheel builds at all, so it is foundational; in the packaging-goal frame this is high (the underlying library is unaffected — purely additive). Fix is additive: crate-type = ["cdylib","lib"] keeps the existing rlib, and the new python feature is opt-in, so no existing consumer breaks.

title: All RL agents are generic over E: Environment and cannot be #[pyclass] directly
location: ppo.rs:94 PPOAgent<E>; sac.rs:25; a2c.rs:24; td3.rs:23; dqn.rs:27; ddpg.rs:63; ppg.rs:292; redq.rs:261; iqn.rs:66; agent.rs:72 (CQLAgent at cql.rs:65 is the only concrete one)
description: PyO3 `#[pyclass]` requires a concrete, non-generic, 'static, Send type. Every public agent except CQLAgent is declared `pub struct XAgent<E: Environment + Clone + 'static>`, so none can be exported as a pyclass as-is. You must monomorphize each agent onto a concrete env type and wrap it: `#[pyclass] struct PyPPO(PPOAgent<ConcreteEnv>)`. The cleanest strategy is to pick ONE concrete bridge env type (see next finding) and generate one wrapper per algorithm over it. CQLAgent (cql.rs:65) is already concrete (offline, takes a dataset) so it can be wrapped without monomorphization.
evidence: grep `pub struct .*Agent`: `PPOAgent<E: Environment + Clone + 'static>`, `SACAgent<E: Environment + Clone + 'static>`, ... `CQLAgent {` (no generic). PyO3 rejects generic pyclasses at compile time.
proposed_fix: Define one concrete bridge env type and emit `#[pyclass]` newtype wrappers `PyPPO(PPOAgent<BridgeEnv>)`, `PySAC(...)`, etc., forwarding `new/train/predict/save/load`. Wrap CQLAgent directly since it is non-generic.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed from repo: every agent struct is `XAgent<E: Environment + Clone + 'static>` (ppo/sac/a2c/td3/dqn/ddpg/ppg/redq/iqn/agent), and CQLAgent is the only concrete one. The 'pyclass rejects generics / requires concrete 'static Send' part is PyO3 semantics (correct). It is a hard blocker for exposing agents, but the fix is a routine newtype-over-concrete-env wrapper, so I downgrade the claimed 'critical' to high. Fix is additive (new wrapper structs forwarding methods); no existing API changes.

title: Python-env bridge is impossible under the current VecEnv Clone design (struct-level bound + clone-the-template construction)
location: vecenv.rs:204 (struct bound `E: Environment + Clone`), vecenv.rs:228-235 (new clones template to reach num_envs); gym.rs:493-496 (GymEnv is explicitly NOT Clone)
description: To expose a Python-defined environment you need a Rust bridge `PyEnv(Py<PyAny>)`. But `pub struct VecEnv<E: Environment + Clone>` puts `Clone` on the STRUCT, so `VecEnv<PyEnv>` is only a nameable type if PyEnv: Clone; and the existing `GymEnv` is explicitly non-Clone (`unsafe impl Send/Sync` only), so `VecEnv<GymEnv>` is not even constructible today, and `PPOAgent<GymEnv>` is impossible (agent requires `E: Clone`). Worse, `VecEnv::new(template_envs, num_envs)` reaches num_envs by cloning the template (`template_envs[i % len].clone()`); a `Py<PyAny>` 'clone' only refcount-bumps, so all N 'parallel' envs would share ONE Python object -> data races / wrong rollouts. The entire documented Gym path therefore cannot feed the agents.
evidence: vecenv.rs:204 `pub struct VecEnv<E: Environment + Clone>`; vecenv.rs:233-235 `let envs: Vec<E> = (0..num_envs).map(|i| template_envs[i % template_envs.len()].clone()).collect();`; gym.rs:493 comment `GymEnv cannot be Clone due to Python GIL requirements` + only `unsafe impl Send/Sync`.
proposed_fix: Relax the struct bound at vecenv.rs:204 to `VecEnv<E: Environment>` (keep `Clone` only on `fn new`), and add a non-cloning constructor `VecEnv::from_envs(Vec<E>)` that takes N independently constructed instances. For Python, build N independent `PyEnv` objects (one `Py<PyAny>` each, like `make_vec_gym_envs`) rather than cloning a template. Drop the `E: Clone` bound from the agents that only need to construct, not duplicate.
verify_reasoning: Confirmed from repo: vecenv.rs:204 `pub struct VecEnv<E: Environment + Clone>` puts Clone on the struct; new() reaches num_envs by cloning the template (lines 233-235); GymEnv is explicitly non-Clone with only `unsafe impl Send/Sync` (gym.rs:492-496), and agents require E: Clone, so VecEnv<GymEnv>/PPOAgent<GymEnv> are unnameable/unconstructible today. The Py<PyAny>-clone-is-refcount-bump data-race reasoning is correct PyO3 semantics about a hypothetical bridge. Downgraded claimed 'critical' to high because the recommended primary architecture (finding 7) sidesteps this path entirely. Fix is safe: relaxing a trait bound is backward-compatible and from_envs is additive — but note it is a multi-site refactor (struct bound + new/new_async + optional agent bounds + new constructor), not a one-liner.

title: candle Tensor saturates the public I/O surface; no numpy crate for marshaling
location: traits.rs:8-11,61-64 (reset/step return Tensor); ppo.rs:707 predict(&Tensor)->Tensor; sac.rs:600; vecenv.rs:168-180 VecStepResult holds Tensor
description: Every user-facing input/output is a candle `Tensor` (observations, actions, predict result, VecStepResult fields). candle `Tensor` is a foreign type, so it cannot be `#[pyclass]`-ed (orphan rule) and should not be exposed raw to Python. A wheel needs numpy.ndarray<->Tensor marshaling at every boundary. The `numpy`/`rust-numpy` crate is NOT a dependency; the existing gym.rs converts via slow Python-side `.flatten().tolist()` (gym.rs:237-254, 257-277), which is unacceptable for the hot predict/step path.
evidence: traits.rs:8 `pub type ObsType = Tensor;`, traits.rs:61 `fn reset(...) -> Result<ObsType>`; ppo.rs:707 `pub fn predict(&mut self, obs: &Tensor, ...) -> Result<Tensor>`; vecenv.rs:170-177 `observations/rewards/terminated/truncated: Tensor`. Cargo.toml has no `numpy` dep.

title: No From<OctaneError> for PyErr conversion exists

description: `OctaneError` has `From` impls for candle/io/serde but none for `pyo3::PyErr`. `#[pymethods]` return `PyResult<T>`; without `impl From<OctaneError> for PyErr` the `?` operator won't convert internal errors and every fallible wrapper must hand-map errors. OctaneError is local and PyErr is foreign, so the impl is allowed by the orphan rule and should map variants to idiomatic Python exceptions.

title: Long-running train()/learn() needs GIL release, but the FnMut callback and per-step Python envs fight allow_threads
description: `train(total_timesteps, callback)` runs for minutes; if called from Python without `py.allow_threads(...)` it freezes the interpreter. But two tensions: (1) the Rust `callback: FnMut(&TrainMetrics)` must re-acquire the GIL (`Python::with_gil`) to invoke a Python progress callback, which is fine only with a native env; (2) if the env is a PyEnv, every `env.step` re-enters Python, so the GIL cannot be released for the duration -> no concurrency and the design's whole premise is lost. This makes the wrapping strategy load-bearing.
evidence: ppo.rs:643 `pub fn train<F>(&mut self, total_timesteps: usize, mut callback: F) -> Result<()> where F: FnMut(&TrainMetrics)`; inner loop steps env (ppo.rs:596) and updates for the full horizon. sac.rs:486 same shape.
proposed_fix: Cleanest strategy: expose `.train()` over NATIVE Rust envs (TradingEnv/AdvancedTradingEnv) and run the entire loop inside `py.allow_threads(|| agent.train(...))`, re-acquiring the GIL only to fire an optional Python callback (channel/queue) or just return aggregated TrainMetrics. Treat PyEnv per-step FFI as an explicit slow path that keeps the GIL.
verify_reasoning: Repo-confirmed: train<F: FnMut(&TrainMetrics)>(total_timesteps, callback) at ppo.rs:643 and sac.rs:486; the loop steps the env and updates for the whole horizon. The GIL reasoning (must wrap in py.allow_threads or freeze the interpreter; a PyEnv re-enters Python every step and prevents GIL release) is correct PyO3 semantics, not repo-verifiable. This is a real design consideration but has a standard pattern (allow_threads over native envs), so I downgrade the claimed 'high' to medium. The 'fix' is an architectural recommendation, not a code change that alters results.

title: Cleanest overall strategy: wrap native Rust envs, not per-step Python callbacks

description: Synthesis of the blockers above: the trading-focused, Apple-Silicon-accelerated value proposition is only preserved if the env loop stays in Rust. The recommended packaging architecture is: (a) expose the native Rust envs (TradingEnv/AdvancedTradingEnv/MultiAssetEnv) to Python, constructed from numpy market data once at __init__; (b) expose agent wrappers monomorphized over those concrete envs; (c) run `.learn()` entirely in Rust under allow_threads; (d) marshal numpy only at construction and at predict() for inference. The Python/Gym per-step bridge should be a documented compatibility fallback, not the primary path, because it collides with both the Clone bound and the GIL/Rayon parallelism in VecEnv (vecenv.rs:396 par_iter_mut).

This is a synthesis/strategy recommendation rather than a concrete defect, so I downgrade the claimed 'medium' to low. It is advisory; nothing to fix that would change results.

title: Device is trivially wrappable but its Metal/Cuda variants are cfg-gated by build features
description: `Device` is `Clone+Copy+PartialEq+Eq+Serialize+Default` POD with no candle handle stored (it lazily builds the candle device via `to_candle()`), so it maps cleanly to a small `#[pyclass]` enum or string constructor. The only wrinkle: `Metal` and `Cuda(usize)` variants and their constructors are `#[cfg(feature=...)]`, so the Python-visible device set depends on which features the wheel was built with; constructing `Device.metal()` in a CPU-only wheel must fail gracefully rather than not exist.

proposed_fix: Expose `Device.cpu()`, `Device.metal()`, `Device.cuda(i)` staticmethods that return a clear PyErr ('feature not compiled') when the cfg is absent, so the Python API surface is stable across wheel variants.

The observation that the Python-visible device set varies per wheel and CPU-only wheels should fail gracefully is accurate. Low; fix (stable staticmethods returning a clear PyErr when the cfg is absent) is additive.

title: Configs are serde-ready but use a Rust builder chain unergonomic in Python
location: config.rs:10-72 PPOConfig (Debug,Clone,Serialize,Deserialize), builder setters 96-120; SACConfig at 524
description: All configs (PPOConfig, SACConfig, etc.) derive `Clone + Serialize + Deserialize` with a fluent builder (`.learning_rate(x).n_steps(y)`), which is awkward from Python where `PPOConfig(learning_rate=3e-4, n_steps=2048)` is expected. Because they are serde-serializable, the lowest-effort bridge is a `from_dict`/`from_json` round-trip; a richer bridge is `#[pyclass]` with a `#[new]` signature of optional kwargs plus getters/setters.
evidence: config.rs:10 `#[derive(Debug, Clone, Serialize, Deserialize)] pub struct PPOConfig`; builder methods consume/return self (103-120). save() already does `serde_json::to_string_pretty(&self.config)` (ppo.rs:764).
proposed_fix: Expose configs as `#[pyclass]` with `#[new](**kwargs)` defaulting to `Default::default()`, plus `from_json`/`to_json` reusing the existing serde impls.
verify_reasoning: Repo-confirmed: PPOConfig (config.rs:10) derives Debug+Clone+Serialize+Deserialize with consuming builder setters (learning_rate/n_steps/...), and ppo.rs save() already serde_json-serializes the config. The ergonomics point (Python expects kwargs/from_dict, not a fluent chain) is accurate. Low; proposed #[pyclass] with #[new](**kwargs) + from_json/to_json reuses existing serde impls and is additive.

title: Buffers hold a Device field and emit Tensor-bearing batches; mostly internal but leak Tensors if exposed

title: Wrapped types must be Send; verify candle Tensor/VarMap stay Send under metal
location: ppo.rs:94-139 (PPOAgent holds VarMap, Option<Tensor>, AdamW); sac.rs:25-68 (multiple VarMap + log_alpha Tensor)

The agents hold `VarMap`, candle `Tensor` (`log_std`, `log_alpha`), `AdamW`, and `StdRng`. These are Send by candle's design on CPU and Metal, so wrapping is fine, but it is worth an explicit compile check when building the wheel with `--features metal` so the Send requirement is confirmed rather than assumed. No evidence of `Rc`/`RefCell`/non-Send members in these structs.
