The claim that #[pyclass] requires Send and that candle Tensor/VarMap are Send is PyO3/candle semantics — Send is an auto-trait depending on candle internals and cannot be fully confirmed from this repo, which is exactly why the finding recommends an explicit _assert_send compile check under --features metal. is_real=true (a sound, low-severity recommendation); adding a compile-time assertion is safe.

title: Rollout policy/value forward is the strongest mlx-rs side-path candidate (weights static across rollout), but win is conditional and partly recoverable in Candle
location: policy_forward 207-235, value_forward 238-266; called per-step in collect_rollout 588-633 (sample_action->policy_forward + value_forward)
description: During rollout collection the network weights do NOT change (collect_rollout only does inference, the AdamW step happens later in update()). So an mlx-rs side-path could marshal the policy+value weights into mlx ONCE per rollout, then run n_steps=2048 forward-only passes ([num_envs,obs]->256->256->act/1 with tanh) paying only obs-in / logits+value-out f32 marshaling per step. mlx's lazy graph fuses the linear+tanh chain into far fewer Metal dispatches than Candle's op-by-op dispatch. Required ops (matmul, tanh) are present in mlx-rs. This is the only forward path with a clean 'marshal weights once, reuse many steps' structure. SKEPTICAL CAVEAT 1 (symmetric to the categorical finding): the Candle baseline here is artificially inflated because policy_forward/value_forward RECONSTRUCT every Linear via candle_nn::linear(vb.pp(format!("{}.layer_{}", ...))) on EVERY call -- a per-step format!+VarBuilder lookup+module build. Much of the apparent mlx win is just 'stop rebuilding layers', recoverable in pure Candle by caching Linear/MLP modules. The true mlx-only delta is kernel fusion / dispatch-count reduction, which is smaller. SKEPTICAL CAVEAT 2: the env runs CPU-side (VecEnv::step does actions.get(i) then env.step on the CPU), so actions must return to CPU and obs originate CPU-side every step -- the forward cannot stay resident on-device across the rollout. The win therefore only materializes when num_envs AND obs/hidden dims are large enough that batched-matmul compute exceeds fixed per-step marshaling+launch overhead; for small num_envs with hidden_sizes=[256,256] marshaling likely dominates. Rank: #1, but 'measure, do not assume'.
evidence: ppo.rs:214-234 rebuilds layers every call: `for i in 0..num_layers { let linear = candle_nn::linear(..., vb.pp(format!("{}.layer_{}", self.policy_prefix, i)))?; x = linear.forward(&x)?; x = x.tanh()?; }` -- invoked from collect_rollout:590 `self.sample_action(&obs)` and :593 `self.value_forward(&obs)`, looped `for _step in 0..n_steps` (n_steps default 2048, config.rs:78). Weights are only mutated later in update() (ppo.rs:510 optimizer.step), confirming they are static during the whole rollout.
proposed_fix: Prototype an mlx-rs forward-only MLP behind a feature flag: copy policy+value weights into mlx Arrays at the start of collect_rollout, then per step marshal obs f32 -> mlx, eval fused (matmul,tanh)* graph, marshal logits/value back. Gate it on a measured num_envs/obs/hidden threshold. FIRST do the free Candle win (cache the Linear modules instead of rebuilding via format! every call) so the comparison is honest.
bench_to_confirm: New micro-bench (or extend gpu_benchmark): mlx fused MLP forward vs candle-metal forward across num_envs in {8,256,1024,4096} x obs_dim x hidden=256, measuring per-step latency including f32 marshaling both directions.
verify_reasoning: Verified: policy_forward (ppo.rs:207-235) and value_forward (238-266) rebuild every Linear via candle_nn::linear(vb.pp(format!("{}.layer_{}"))) on each call, and collect_rollout (588-633) calls sample_action+value_forward inside `for _step in 0..n_steps` while AdamW optimizer.step is only invoked later in update() (510). So weights are genuinely static across the rollout, exactly as claimed. The two skeptical caveats are accurate: (1) the Candle baseline is inflated by per-step layer reconstruction, recoverable without mlx; (2) VecEnv steps CPU-side, so obs/actions round-trip every step and the forward cannot stay device-resident. The mlx-only delta (kernel/dispatch fusion) is real but small and conditional, which the finding honestly states. Severity low matches a speculative, conditional perf candidate. fix_is_safe: the only default-path change is caching the Linear modules; candle Var::set updates storage in place and VarBuilder::get returns storage-sharing clones, so cached modules still observe weight updates and autodiff still maps grads to the Vars; the mlx prototype is gated behind a feature flag. No correctness or API break.

title: Discrete categorical sampling does a per-step GPU->CPU sync plus a to_vec1 inside the per-batch loop; on-device mlx categorical/gumbel could remove it, but the existing Candle gumbel path already can
location: sample_action discrete branch 272-314 (probs.to_vec1 at 278; log_probs.get(b)?.flatten_all()?.to_vec1() INSIDE the per-batch loop at 305)
description: The PPO discrete sampler pulls the full probs tensor to CPU (to_vec1, line 278), then loops over batch elements doing inverse-CDF sampling in Rust, and -- worse -- calls log_probs.get(b)?.flatten_all()?.to_vec1() once PER batch element (line 305), i.e. a tensor extraction/sync per sample. This forces a GPU->CPU round-trip every rollout step on the categorical path. If the logits already live on mlx (fused with the Finding #1 forward), mlx random::categorical (present in the mlx-rs `random` module per crate docs) or a gumbel-max + take_along_axis keeps sampling on-device and returns only the int action index, eliminating the sync. SKEPTICAL CAVEAT (symmetric): this is largely recoverable in pure Candle -- the repo ALREADY has Categorical::sample_gumbel_max (categorical.rs 122-146) which does gumbel-noise + argmax fully on tensors with no per-element CPU loop. So the mlx-specific value is marginal; the dominant fix is to route PPO's discrete sampler through the existing on-tensor gumbel path. mlx random::categorical presence is from the crate-level search, not the pinned-version API page -- verify against the exact mlx-rs version before relying on it.
evidence: ppo.rs:305 inside `for b in 0..batch_size { ... let lp_vec: Vec<f32> = log_probs.get(b)?.flatten_all()?.to_vec1()?; action_log_probs.push(lp_vec[action]); }` -- a to_vec1 per batch element. Contrast categorical.rs:142-145 `let perturbed = self.logits.broadcast_add(&gumbel)?; let samples = perturbed.argmax(1)?;` which is fully on-tensor.
proposed_fix: Short term (Candle, no mlx): replace the CPU inverse-CDF loop with the existing gumbel-max + gather/argmax tensor path. Only consider mlx random::categorical if/when the forward is already mlx-resident (Finding #1), so logits never leave the device; verify random::categorical exists in the pinned mlx-rs version first.
bench_to_confirm: ppo_benchmark on a discrete env: per-step rollout latency, current CPU-loop sampler vs on-tensor gumbel vs mlx categorical.
verify_reasoning: Verified: the discrete branch pulls full probs to host via probs.flatten_all().to_vec1() (ppo.rs:278), then inside `for b in 0..batch_size` calls log_probs.get(b)?.flatten_all()?.to_vec1() per element (305), an extraction/sync per sample every rollout step. The on-tensor alternative Categorical::sample_gumbel_max (categorical.rs:122-146) exists and does gumbel-noise + argmax with no per-element host loop, so the dominant fix is recoverable in pure Candle, exactly as the finding's symmetric caveat states. The mlx random::categorical availability is appropriately flagged as unverified against the pinned version. fix_is_safe=false: the existing sample_gumbel_max uses rand::thread_rng() (categorical.rs:128) rather than the agent's seeded self.rng, so routing the PPO sampler through it would silently break seed reproducibility (PPOConfig exposes a seed field); additionally it returns only the action index, so the selected-action log-prob still needs a separate gather. Both are subtle side effects relative to the current seeded inverse-CDF path.

title: Standalone Gaussian log-prob and PPO loss-reduction kernels are a marshaling anti-pattern (full GPU sync per call) AND have no autodiff path -- do NOT replicate as marshaled mlx side-path ops
location: gaussian_log_prob 461-498, ppo_policy_loss 528-573, ppo_loss/ppo_combined_loss 638-697 (per-call create_buffer + commit + wait_until_completed at 494-497, 566-567, 689-691)
description: These are tiny elementwise/reduction ops over [batch] or [batch,action_dim]. The existing Metal implementation is a textbook example of the marshaling cost the task warns about, and an mlx side-path would inherit both flaws: (1) MARSHALING > WIN: every call freshly create_buffer's each input slice, commit()s, then wait_until_completed() -- a FULL GPU sync per call -- then read_buffer's back to Vec<f32>. For PPO batch_size=64 and small action_dim the per-call sync + buffer setup dwarfs the arithmetic (a handful of FLOPs per element). (2) NO GRADIENT: gaussian_log_prob/ppo_policy_loss/ppo_combined_loss return Vec<f32> with no autodiff, so they cannot participate in backward() and are unusable inside the actual training loop regardless of speed (the live PPO update at ppo.rs:482-507 computes ratio/min/mean and calls total_loss.backward() in Candle, never touching these kernels). Porting log-prob or loss-reduction to mlx as standalone marshaled kernels would repeat exactly this: pay a per-minibatch f32 round-trip for an op that is cheaper to keep inside the autodiff graph. This is the most defensible 'flag where marshaling exceeds the win' finding.
evidence: metal.rs:494-497 `command_buffer.commit(); command_buffer.wait_until_completed(); Ok(self.read_buffer(&output_buf, n))` -- per call. metal.rs:201-203 combined-loss kernel writes plain floats `total_losses[id] = policy_loss + vf_coef*value_loss - ent_coef*entropy[id];` returned as Vec<f32> (no grad). Live training instead uses Candle autodiff: ppo.rs:507 `let mut grads = total_loss.backward()?;`.
proposed_fix: Keep Gaussian log-prob and PPO loss reduction inside the Candle (or a future fully-mlx) autodiff graph; never expose them as standalone marshaled buffer kernels. If mlx is adopted, fuse log-prob+loss with the forward/backward graph so no extra f32 round-trip is paid, and only after confirming mlx autodiff (transforms::grad, confirmed in mlx-rs) can differentiate through them.
bench_to_confirm: Micro-bench: candle in-graph gaussian_log_prob/loss vs the marshaled metal.rs kernel at batch {64,256} -- expected to show the marshaled path slower due to wait_until_completed per call.
verify_reasoning: Verified: MetalCompute::gaussian_log_prob (metal.rs:461-498), ppo_policy_loss (528-573), and ppo_loss/ppo_combined_loss (638-697) each create_buffer per input, then commit()+wait_until_completed() a full GPU sync per call, then read_buffer back to Vec<f32> (494-497, 566-567, 689-691). The combined-loss kernel writes plain f32 (201-203) with no gradient. I confirmed by grep that these MetalCompute methods are not referenced anywhere in the live training path; the live PPO update uses Candle autodiff via total_loss.backward() (507). The candle-based gaussian_log_prob hits in sac.rs/cql.rs/redq.rs are separate Tensor methods, and simd/log_prob.rs is CPU SIMD, not these kernels. So both claimed flaws (per-call marshaling+sync over tiny ops, and no autodiff) hold, and porting them to mlx as standalone marshaled kernels would repeat the anti-pattern. fix_is_safe: the recommendation is to keep these inside the autodiff graph and not expose standalone marshaled kernels; it does not modify any correct code path or API.

title: Gaussian sampling (randn) is memory-bound and tiny -- a standalone mlx sampler loses to marshaling
location: sample_standard_normal 149-162 and Distribution::sample 195-201; also ppo.rs:325 Tensor::randn_like, sac.rs:248 Tensor::randn_like
description: Drawing batch*action_dim standard normals plus an axpy (mean + std*noise) is a single memory-bound pass over a small buffer. DiagGaussian already generates noise on CPU via rng then Tensor::from_slice (gaussian.rs:155-160); PPO/SAC use Tensor::randn_like. Routing this through an mlx side-path means marshaling either the noise out, or mean/std in and the sample out, for an op whose arithmetic is trivial. The f32 buffer marshaling cost exceeds the RNG+axpy cost, so a standalone mlx Gaussian sampler is not a candidate. The only sampling-related win is keeping sampling FUSED with an on-device forward (Finding #1) so noise/mean/std never round-trip -- not a separate mlx sampler.
evidence: gaussian.rs:155-160 `let samples: Vec<f32> = (0..batch_size*action_dim).map(|_| rng.sample(StandardNormal)).collect(); let noise = Tensor::from_slice(&samples, &[batch_size, action_dim], device)?;` -- CPU RNG then one tensor build; sample() at 197-200 is just `mean + std*noise`.
proposed_fix: Do not build a standalone mlx Gaussian sampler. If mlx is adopted for the forward, generate noise and apply mean+std*noise inside the same mlx graph as the policy forward so there is zero extra marshaling.
bench_to_confirm: Micro-bench randn+axpy at [num_envs, action_dim] candle-metal vs mlx including marshaling -- expected marshaling-dominated.
verify_reasoning: Verified: DiagGaussian::sample_standard_normal (gaussian.rs:149-162) draws batch*action_dim normals on CPU via rng then a single Tensor::from_slice, and Distribution::sample (195-201) is just mean + std*noise, a single memory-bound axpy. ppo.rs:325 and sac.rs:248 use Tensor::randn_like for the same trivial-arithmetic op. The reasoning that f32 buffer marshaling would dominate the RNG+axpy cost for a standalone mlx sampler is sound, and the conclusion (the only real win is fusing sampling with an on-device forward, not a separate sampler) is consistent with finding 1. fix_is_safe: the recommendation is not to build a standalone mlx sampler; it changes nothing in the current code path.

title: The compute-dense PPO/SAC update loop is the only region that could amortize an mlx engine, but it cannot be a marshaled side-path -- per-minibatch weight round-trips dwarf the matmul savings
location: update minibatch loop 455-543 (evaluate_actions 478, backward 507); SAC update 389-483 (sac.rs)
description: The arithmetically heavy region is the update loop, not the rollout: PPO runs n_epochs=10 x n_batches x (2 MLP forwards over [256,256] in evaluate_actions + a full backward) per rollout; SAC's update does ~7 MLP forwards (policy + q1 + q2 + target_q1 + target_q2 + policy-again q1/q2) plus backward, at batch_size=256, every env step (gradient_steps=1). This is where a fast Metal autodiff (mlx transforms::grad, confirmed present in mlx-rs) could in principle help. BUT to compute gradients mlx must OWN the parameters. As a side-path 'running parallel to Candle paying f32 marshaling', you would have to copy ALL trainable weights into mlx and gradients back EVERY minibatch -- PPO ~10*n_batches times per rollout, SAC every step -- for policy+value (PPO) or policy+2xQ+2xtarget (SAC). That weight+grad round-trip is O(num_params) marshaling per step and dwarfs the matmul savings on [256,256] nets. Honest conclusion: mlx only pays off in training if it OWNS the loop end-to-end (params + optimizer migrated off Candle VarMap/AdamW), which is a full migration, not a cheap experimental side-path. Note also SAC reconstructs AdamW optimizers every update (sac.rs:435,439,452), discarding Adam moment state each step -- a structural issue an mlx side-path cannot fix without also owning the optimizer.
evidence: ppo.rs:478 `self.evaluate_actions(&batch_obs, &batch_actions)?` then :507 `total_loss.backward()?` inside `for _epoch in 0..self.config.n_epochs` (=10) over n_batches. sac.rs:435 `let mut q1_optimizer = AdamW::new(self.q1_var_map.all_vars(), params.clone())?;` recreated each update -- Adam state lost. Weights live in candle VarMap (ppo.rs:508 self.var_map.all_vars()); an mlx side-path would have to marshal all of these per minibatch.
proposed_fix: Do not attempt training as a marshaled mlx side-path. Either (a) keep training in Candle, or (b) commit to a full mlx training backend that owns params + optimizer (and fix the SAC per-step optimizer recreation as part of that), comparing end-to-end update throughput before adopting.
bench_to_confirm: ppo_benchmark / gpu_benchmark: full update() step throughput, candle autodiff vs a prototype mlx-owned-params update, explicitly accounting for any per-step weight marshaling.
verify_reasoning: Verified: PPO update runs n_epochs=10 (config.rs:80) x n_batches x (evaluate_actions = policy_forward + value_forward at ppo.rs:348-349, i.e. 2 MLP forwards) + total_loss.backward() (507). SAC update (sac.rs:389-483) does ~7 MLP forwards (target_q1, target_q2, current_q1, current_q2, q1_new, q2_new plus policy sampling twice) + backward, and SACConfig default (config.rs:555-565) is batch_size=256, train_freq=1, gradient_steps=1, so it runs essentially every env step as claimed. The structural note is correct and verified: AdamW optimizers are reconstructed every update at sac.rs:435/439/452 (q1/q2/policy), discarding Adam moment state each step. The perf reasoning that a marshaled side-path would require O(num_params) weight+grad round-trips per minibatch, dwarfing [256,256] matmul savings, is sound, and the honest conclusion (mlx only pays off if it owns params+optimizer end-to-end) follows. fix_is_safe: the recommendation is keep training in Candle or commit to a full mlx backend, and the embedded suggestion to persist AdamW state across SAC updates is itself the correct behavior, not a regression.
